entries.title: Horoscope for Thursday, April 09, 2026
entries.summary: <h2>Moon Alert</h2> <p>There are no restrictions to shopping or important decisions today. The moon is in Capricorn.</p><h2>Aries (March 21-April 19)</h2> <p></p> <div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="HtmlModule"><a class="AnchorLink" id="html-embed-module-6f0000" name="html-embed-module-6f0000"></a><div class="HoroscopeStarbox HoroscopeStarbox_five"> <div class="HoroscopeStarbox_container"> <svg class="HoroscopeStar"><use xlink:href="#icon-star"></use></svg> <svg class="HoroscopeStar"><use xlink:href="#icon-star"></use></svg> <svg class="HoroscopeStar"><use xlink:href="#icon-star"></use></svg> <svg class="HoroscopeStar"><use xlink:href="#icon-star"></use></svg> <svg class="HoroscopeStar"><use xlink:href="#icon-star"></use></svg> </div> <div class="HoroscopeStarbox_description"> <p>A <b>dynamic</b> day</p> </div> </div> <style> .HoroscopeStarbox { background: #f8f8f8; display: flex; align-items: center; } .HoroscopeStarbox_container { display: grid; grid-gap: 5px; grid-template-columns: 1fr 1fr 1fr 1fr 1fr; padding: 20px; width: 40%; min-width: 160px; max-width: 200px; } .HoroscopeStarbox_description { padding: 20px; font-family: var(--font-1); font-size: var(--body-2); } .HoroscopeStarbox_container svg { object-fit: cover; aspect-ratio: 1 / 1; width: 100%; } </style> </div> </div><p> Today, your ruler, fiery Mars, enters your sign, introducing a six-week window of increased activity and a chance to further your own interests. You’ll be more of a fighter for your own rights than usual. You won’t let anyone take anything from you. Your energy will be pumped!</p><h2>Taurus (April 20-May 20)</h2> <p></p> <div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="HtmlModule"><a class="AnchorLink" id="html-embed-module-4d0000" name="html-embed-module-4d0000"></a><div class="HoroscopeStarbox HoroscopeStarbox_five"> <div class="HoroscopeStarbox_container"> <svg class="HoroscopeStar"><use xlink:href="#icon-star"></use></svg> <svg class="HoroscopeStar"><use xlink:href="#icon-star"></use></svg> <svg class="HoroscopeStar"><use xlink:href="#icon-star"></use></svg> </div> <div class="HoroscopeStarbox_description"> <p>An <b>average</b> day</p> </div> </div> <style> .HoroscopeStarbox { background: #f8f8f8; display: flex; align-items: center; } .HoroscopeStarbox_container { display: grid; grid-gap: 5px; grid-template-columns: 1fr 1fr 1fr 1fr 1fr; padding: 20px; width: 40%; min-width: 160px; max-width: 200px; } .HoroscopeStarbox_description { padding: 20px; font-family: var(--font-1); font-size: var(--body-2); } .HoroscopeStarbox_container svg { object-fit: cover; aspect-ratio: 1 / 1; width: 100%; } </style> </div> </div><p> For the next six weeks, Mars will be hiding in your chart, which can be challenging. You might be frustrated and feel resigned to being in the background. You might not get credit for what you do. You might unwittingly create the wrong impression. Oops.</p><h2>Gemini (May 21-June 20)</h2> <p></p> <div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="HtmlModule"><a class="AnchorLink" id="html-embed-module-7b0000" name="html-embed-module-7b0000"></a><div class="HoroscopeStarbox HoroscopeStarbox_five"> <div class="HoroscopeStarbox_container"> <svg class="HoroscopeStar"><use xlink:href="#icon-star"></use></svg> <svg class="HoroscopeStar"><use xlink:href="#icon-star"></use></svg> <svg class="HoroscopeStar"><use xlink:href="#icon-star"></use></svg> <svg class="HoroscopeStar"><use xlink:href="#icon-star"></use></svg> </div> <div class="HoroscopeStarbox_description"> <p>A <b>positive</b> day</p> </div> </div> <style> .HoroscopeStarbox { background: #f8f8f8; display: flex; align-items: center; } .HoroscopeStarbox_container { display: grid; grid-gap: 5px; grid-template-columns: 1fr 1fr 1fr 1fr 1fr; padding: 20px; width: 40%; min-width: 160px; max-width: 200px; } .HoroscopeStarbox_description { padding: 20px; font-family: var(--font-1); font-size: var(--body-2); } .HoroscopeStarbox_container svg { object-fit: cover; aspect-ratio: 1 / 1; width: 100%; } </style> </div> </div><p> You're entering a lovely six-week window that's the perfect time to formulate your goals and actively pursue them. You can work successfully with others, especially if you coordinate your needs with theirs. Physical activity with friends, especially competitive athletics, will appeal.</p><h2>Cancer (June 21-July 22)</h2> <p></p> <div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="HtmlModule"><a class="AnchorLink" id="html-embed-module-6f0000" name="html-embed-module-6f0000"></a><div class="HoroscopeStarbox HoroscopeStarbox_five"> <div class="HoroscopeStarbox_container"> <svg class="HoroscopeStar"><use xlink:href="#icon-star"></use></svg> <svg class="HoroscopeStar"><use xlink:href="#icon-star"></use></svg> <svg class="HoroscopeStar"><use xlink:href="#icon-star"></use></svg> <svg class="HoroscopeStar"><use xlink:href="#icon-star"></use></svg> <svg class="HoroscopeStar"><use xlink:href="#icon-star"></use></svg> </div> <div class="HoroscopeStarbox_description"> <p>A <b>dynamic</b> day</p> </div> </div> <style> .HoroscopeStarbox { background: #f8f8f8; display: flex; align-items: center; } .HoroscopeStarbox_container { display: grid; grid-gap: 5px; grid-template-columns: 1fr 1fr 1fr 1fr 1fr; padding: 20px; width: 40%; min-width: 160px; max-width: 200px; } .HoroscopeStarbox_description { padding: 20px; font-family: var(--font-1); font-size: var(--body-2); } .HoroscopeStarbox_container svg { object-fit: cover; aspect-ratio: 1 / 1; width: 100%; } </style> </div> </div><p> Holy cannelloni! Today, fiery Mars moves to the top of your chart to stay for the next six weeks, arousing your ambition as never before. You'll get a lot done, but you might end up in conflict with authority figures, bosses or parents. Even co-workers might feel threatened. Easy does it.</p><h2>Leo (July 23-Aug. 22)</h2> <p></p> <div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="HtmlModule"><a class="AnchorLink" id="html-embed-module-6f0000" name="html-embed-module-6f0000"></a><div class="HoroscopeStarbox HoroscopeStarbox_five"> <div class="HoroscopeStarbox_container"> <svg class="HoroscopeStar"><use xlink:href="#icon-star"></use></svg> <svg class="HoroscopeStar"><use xlink:href="#icon-star"></use></svg> <svg class="HoroscopeStar"><use xlink:href="#icon-star"></use></svg> <svg class="HoroscopeStar"><use xlink:href="#icon-star"></use></svg> <svg class="HoroscopeStar"><use xlink:href="#icon-star"></use></svg> </div> <div class="HoroscopeStarbox_description"> <p>A <b>dynamic</b> day</p> </div> </div> <style> .HoroscopeStarbox { background: #f8f8f8; display: flex; align-items: center; } .HoroscopeStarbox_container { display: grid; grid-gap: 5px; grid-template-columns: 1fr 1fr 1fr 1fr 1fr; padding: 20px; width: 40%; min-width: 160px; max-width: 200px; } .HoroscopeStarbox_description { padding: 20px; font-family: var(--font-1); font-size: var(--body-2); } .HoroscopeStarbox_container svg { object-fit: cover; aspect-ratio: 1 / 1; width: 100%; } </style> </div> </div><p> Exciting news! For the next six weeks, you'll be more intellectually creative with goals related to writing, publishing, exploring new ideas and traveling. Put your energy into expanding your mind and look for experiences that will open up new dimensions of reality.</p><h2>Virgo (Aug. 23-Sept. 22)</h2> <p></p> <div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="HtmlModule"><a class="AnchorLink" id="html-embed-module-4d0000" name="html-embed-module-4d0000"></a><div class="HoroscopeStarbox HoroscopeStarbox_five"> <div class="HoroscopeStarbox_container"> <svg class="HoroscopeStar"><use xlink:href="#icon-star"></use></svg> <svg class="HoroscopeStar"><use xlink:href="#icon-star"></use></svg> <svg class="HoroscopeStar"><use xlink:href="#icon-star"></use></svg> </div> <div class="HoroscopeStarbox_description"> <p>An <b>average</b> day</p> </div> </div> <style> .HoroscopeStarbox { background: #f8f8f8; display: flex; align-items: center; } .HoroscopeStarbox_container { display: grid; grid-gap: 5px; grid-template-columns: 1fr 1fr 1fr 1fr 1fr; padding: 20px; width: 40%; min-width: 160px; max-width: 200px; } .HoroscopeStarbox_description { padding: 20px; font-family: var(--font-1); font-size: var(--body-2); } .HoroscopeStarbox_container svg { object-fit: cover; aspect-ratio: 1 / 1; width: 100%; } </style> </div> </div><p> In the next six weeks, you might encounter confrontations about shared property, taxes, debt, insurance issues or inheritances. You might also disagree about jointly held resources with a spouse or partner. On the upside, physical intimacy will be memorable!</p><h2>Libra (Sept. 23-Oct. 22)</h2> <p></p> <div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="HtmlModule"><a class="AnchorLink" id="html-embed-module-4d0000" name="html-embed-module-4d0000"></a><div class="HoroscopeStarbox HoroscopeStarbox_five"> <div class="HoroscopeStarbox_container"> <svg class="HoroscopeStar"><use xlink:href="#icon-star"></use></svg> <svg class="HoroscopeStar"><use xlink:href="#icon-star"></use></svg> <svg class="HoroscopeStar"><use xlink:href="#icon-star"></use></svg> </div> <div class="HoroscopeStarbox_description"> <p>An <b>average</b> day</p> </div> </div> <style> .HoroscopeStarbox { background: #f8f8f8; display: flex; align-items: center; } .HoroscopeStarbox_container { display: grid; grid-gap: 5px; grid-template-columns: 1fr 1fr 1fr 1fr 1fr; padding: 20px; width: 40%; min-width: 160px; max-width: 200px; } .HoroscopeStarbox_description { padding: 20px; font-family: var(--font-1); font-size: var(--body-2); } .HoroscopeStarbox_container svg { object-fit: cover; aspect-ratio: 1 / 1; width: 100%; } </style> </div> </div><p> Tread carefully in the next six weeks because fiery Mars is opposite your sign. (This happens approximately every two years.) The problem is that this can create confrontations in all your one-to-one relationships, especially spouses, partners and close friends. (But it's a good time to air grievances.)</p><h2>Scorpio (Oct. 23-Nov. 21)</h2> <p></p> <div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="HtmlModule"><a class="AnchorLink" id="html-embed-module-7b0000" name="html-embed-module-7b0000"></a><div class="HoroscopeStarbox HoroscopeStarbox_five"> <div class="HoroscopeStarbox_container"> <svg class="HoroscopeStar"><use xlink:href="#icon-star"></use></svg> <svg class="HoroscopeStar"><use xlink:href="#icon-star"></use></svg> <svg class="HoroscopeStar"><use xlink:href="#icon-star"></use></svg> <svg class="HoroscopeStar"><use xlink:href="#icon-star"></use></svg> </div> <div class="HoroscopeStarbox_description"> <p>A <b>positive</b> day</p> </div> </div> <style> .HoroscopeStarbox { background: #f8f8f8; display: flex; align-items: center; } .HoroscopeStarbox_container { display: grid; grid-gap: 5px; grid-template-columns: 1fr 1fr 1fr 1fr 1fr; padding: 20px; width: 40%; min-width: 160px; max-width: 200px; } .HoroscopeStarbox_description { padding: 20px; font-family: var(--font-1); font-size: var(--body-2); } .HoroscopeStarbox_container svg { object-fit: cover; aspect-ratio: 1 / 1; width: 100%; } </style> </div> </div><p> You'll be surprised at how productive you'll be in the next six weeks because fiery Mars will help you accomplish whatever you want to do, especially at work. You'll put all your energy into getting things done. Naturally, you want credit for what you do. This is not the time to be a team player.</p><h2>Sagittarius (Nov. 22-Dec. 21)</h2> <p></p> <div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="HtmlModule"><a class="AnchorLink" id="html-embed-module-6f0000" name="html-embed-module-6f0000"></a><div class="HoroscopeStarbox HoroscopeStarbox_five"> <div class="HoroscopeStarbox_container"> <svg class="HoroscopeStar"><use xlink:href="#icon-star"></use></svg> <svg class="HoroscopeStar"><use xlink:href="#icon-star"></use></svg> <svg class="HoroscopeStar"><use xlink:href="#icon-star"></use></svg> <svg class="HoroscopeStar"><use xlink:href="#icon-star"></use></svg> <svg class="HoroscopeStar"><use xlink:href="#icon-star"></use></svg> </div> <div class="HoroscopeStarbox_description"> <p>A <b>dynamic</b> day</p> </div> </div> <style> .HoroscopeStarbox { background: #f8f8f8; display: flex; align-items: center; } .HoroscopeStarbox_container { display: grid; grid-gap: 5px; grid-template-columns: 1fr 1fr 1fr 1fr 1fr; padding: 20px; width: 40%; min-width: 160px; max-width: 200px; } .HoroscopeStarbox_description { padding: 20px; font-family: var(--font-1); font-size: var(--body-2); } .HoroscopeStarbox_container svg { object-fit: cover; aspect-ratio: 1 / 1; width: 100%; } </style> </div> </div><p> The planet Mars symbolizes our ego, and in the next six weeks, you'll demand to be yourself and express yourself exactly how you want. You'll shun self-denial, self-discipline and postponing self-gratification. Romance will be passionate and sexy. Sports energy will be aggressive.</p><h2>Capricorn (Dec. 22-Jan. 19)</h2> <p></p> <div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="HtmlModule"><a class="AnchorLink" id="html-embed-module-7b0000" name="html-embed-module-7b0000"></a><div class="HoroscopeStarbox HoroscopeStarbox_five"> <div class="HoroscopeStarbox_container"> <svg class="HoroscopeStar"><use xlink:href="#icon-star"></use></svg> <svg class="HoroscopeStar"><use xlink:href="#icon-star"></use></svg> <svg class="HoroscopeStar"><use xlink:href="#icon-star"></use></svg> <svg class="HoroscopeStar"><use xlink:href="#icon-star"></use></svg> </div> <div class="HoroscopeStarbox_description"> <p>A <b>positive</b> day</p> </div> </div> <style> .HoroscopeStarbox { background: #f8f8f8; display: flex; align-items: center; } .HoroscopeStarbox_container { display: grid; grid-gap: 5px; grid-template-columns: 1fr 1fr 1fr 1fr 1fr; padding: 20px; width: 40%; min-width: 160px; max-width: 200px; } .HoroscopeStarbox_description { padding: 20px; font-family: var(--font-1); font-size: var(--body-2); } .HoroscopeStarbox_container svg { object-fit: cover; aspect-ratio: 1 / 1; width: 100%; } </style> </div> </div><p> You can expect increased activity (even chaos) on the home front for the next six weeks — perhaps due to houseguests, residential moves, redecorating projects or renovations. Make an effort to get along with family members to avoid domestic strife. Living with a parent might be challenging.</p><h2>Aquarius (Jan. 20-Feb. 18)</h2> <p></p> <div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="HtmlModule"><a class="AnchorLink" id="html-embed-module-6f0000" name="html-embed-module-6f0000"></a><div class="HoroscopeStarbox HoroscopeStarbox_five"> <div class="HoroscopeStarbox_container"> <svg class="HoroscopeStar"><use xlink:href="#icon-star"></use></svg> <svg class="HoroscopeStar"><use xlink:href="#icon-star"></use></svg> <svg class="HoroscopeStar"><use xlink:href="#icon-star"></use></svg> <svg class="HoroscopeStar"><use xlink:href="#icon-star"></use></svg> <svg class="HoroscopeStar"><use xlink:href="#icon-star"></use></svg> </div> <div class="HoroscopeStarbox_description"> <p>A <b>dynamic</b> day</p> </div> </div> <style> .HoroscopeStarbox { background: #f8f8f8; display: flex; align-items: center; } .HoroscopeStarbox_container { display: grid; grid-gap: 5px; grid-template-columns: 1fr 1fr 1fr 1fr 1fr; padding: 20px; width: 40%; min-width: 160px; max-width: 200px; } .HoroscopeStarbox_description { padding: 20px; font-family: var(--font-1); font-size: var(--body-2); } .HoroscopeStarbox_container svg { object-fit: cover; aspect-ratio: 1 / 1; width: 100%; } </style> </div> </div><p> The everyday tempo of your life will accelerate in the next six weeks because you have things to do, places to go and people to see. Do what you can to avoid arguments because you will be strongly opinionated and counter anyone who disagrees with you. Easy does it.</p><h2>Pisces (Feb. 19-March 20)</h2> <p></p> <div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="HtmlModule"><a class="AnchorLink" id="html-embed-module-7b0000" name="html-embed-module-7b0000"></a><div class="HoroscopeStarbox HoroscopeStarbox_five"> <div class="HoroscopeStarbox_container"> <svg class="HoroscopeStar"><use xlink:href="#icon-star"></use></svg> <svg class="HoroscopeStar"><use xlink:href="#icon-star"></use></svg> <svg class="HoroscopeStar"><use xlink:href="#icon-star"></use></svg> <svg class="HoroscopeStar"><use xlink:href="#icon-star"></use></svg> </div> <div class="HoroscopeStarbox_description"> <p>A <b>positive</b> day</p> </div> </div> <style> .HoroscopeStarbox { background: #f8f8f8; display: flex; align-items: center; } .HoroscopeStarbox_container { display: grid; grid-gap: 5px; grid-template-columns: 1fr 1fr 1fr 1fr 1fr; padding: 20px; width: 40%; min-width: 160px; max-width: 200px; } .HoroscopeStarbox_description { padding: 20px; font-family: var(--font-1); font-size: var(--body-2); } .HoroscopeStarbox_container svg { object-fit: cover; aspect-ratio: 1 / 1; width: 100%; } </style> </div> </div><p> You'll work hard to boost your income in the next six weeks. You might also find that you seem to identify more strongly with your wealth, your assets and the things that you own. Likewise, you might identify more strongly with your beliefs and opinions. Stay chill.</p><h2>If today is your birthday</h2><p>Actress Cynthia Nixon (1966) shares your birthday today. You are competent, practical, energetic and focused. You have a strong drive and strength of purpose. This year, exciting changes will bring you increased personal freedom. Stay flexible. Let go of what holds you back. Seek out new opportunities and be ready to act fast. Expect to travel.</p>
entries.author: Georgia Nicols

entries.title: Overnight shooting leaves man dead in Brighton Park
entries.summary: <p>A man was dead on the scene early Thursday after someone shot him in the head and body in the Brighton Park neighborhood. </p><p>Police found the unidentified man outside about 4:15 a.m. in the 3100 block of West 39th Place suffering from multiple gunshot wounds, police said. </p><p>He was pronounced dead on the scene and nobody else was hurt. </p><p>Area 1 detectives are investigating.</p>
entries.author: Sun-Times Wire

entries.title: West Side fire leaves man dead
entries.summary: <p>A man in his 60s was killed during a predawn blaze on the West Side. </p><p>The fire erupted about 2:15 a.m. at an apartment building in the 3400 block of West Monroe Street, in the Garfield Park neighborhood, police said.</p><p>By the time officers got there, a woman and a 4-year-old had already escaped and were outside, standing on the sidewalk. <br>Firefighters began extinguishing the flames and found a 62-year-old man inside. He was treated on scene by paramedics and taken to Stroger Hospital, where he was pronounced dead, police said. </p><p>The woman and child were also taken in good condition to Stroger, as a precaution, police said. </p><p>No one was displaced and the cause of the fire was under investigation. </p>
entries.author: Sun-Times Wire

entries.title: Property owners deserve compensation when police damage their property
entries.summary: <p>In 2022, police <a class="Link" href="https://reason.com/2023/12/20/this-innocent-woman-is-on-the-hook-for-thousands-after-a-swat-team-destroyed-her-home/" target="_blank" >caused extensive damage</a> to Amy Hadley's home in South Bend, Indiana, because they mistakenly believed a fugitive was inside the house. That same year, a Los Angeles SWAT team <a class="Link" href="https://reason.com/2023/07/27/a-swat-team-destroyed-an-innocent-mans-shop-then-the-city-left-him-with-the-bill/" target="_blank" >wrecked</a> Carlos Pena's print shop while trying to arrest a fugitive who had barricaded himself inside.</p><p>Through no fault of their own, Hadley and Pena were stuck with the tab for the havoc wrought by police operations — a plainly unfair but increasingly common <a class="Link" href="https://reason.com/2024/12/23/a-swat-team-destroyed-an-innocent-womans-house-the-supreme-court-wont-hear-her-case/" target="_blank" >situation</a> that could be rectified by the "just compensation" that the Fifth Amendment <a class="Link" href="https://constitution.congress.gov/browse/essay/amdt5-9-1/ALDE_00013280/" target="_blank" >requires</a> when property is "taken for public use." In petitions filed this week, Hadley and Pena are <a class="Link" href="https://reason.com/2026/04/07/police-destroyed-innocent-peoples-property-and-left-them-with-the-bill-will-the-supreme-court-step-in/" target="_blank" >asking</a> the Supreme Court to recognize that remedy.</p><p>On a Friday afternoon in June 2022, according to Hadley's <a class="Link" href="https://ij.org/wp-content/uploads/2026/04/Hadley-v.-City-of-South-Bend-Petition-Appendix.pdf" target="_blank" >petition</a>, city and county police looking for "a dangerous fugitive" surrounded her home. They had erroneously concluded, "based on an IP address," that the fugitive was using a computer inside the house.</p><div class="RichTextSidebarModule Enhancement" data-module data-align-center><a class="AnchorLink" id="module-fd0000" name="module-fd0000"></a> <div class="RichTextSidebarModule-title">Columnists bug</div> <div class="RichTextModule-items RichTextBody"><h2>Columnists</h2><br><i>In-depth political coverage, sports analysis, entertainment reviews and cultural commentary.</i><br></div> </div><p>In reality, the only person in the house was Hadley's 15-year-old son, who promptly obeyed police orders to come out with his hands up. Hadley, who came home shortly afterward, told police they had made a mistake, adding that security cameras confirmed no one else was inside.</p><p>The officers nevertheless fired dozens of tear gas rounds through the windows of the unoccupied house, which they eventually ransacked while searching for a fugitive who was not there and never had been. The damage, which totaled about $16,000, was only partly covered by insurance, and local officials refused to pay the difference.</p><p>A couple of months later, according to Pena's <a class="Link" href="https://ij.org/wp-content/uploads/2026/04/Pena-v.-City-of-Los-Angeles-Petition-Appendix.pdf" target="_blank" >petition</a>, he was working in his North Hollywood print shop when he "heard a commotion outside." After he opened the door to see what was happening, a man running from U.S. marshals forced his way in and expelled Pena.</p><p>During the ensuing standoff, a SWAT team saturated Pena's shop with tear gas, destroying equipment valued at $60,000. The fugitive escaped, but Pena's business was ruined, and the city refused to compensate him.</p><p>Last October, the U.S. Court of Appeals for the 7th Circuit <a class="Link" href="https://reason.com/2025/10/10/this-indiana-city-doesnt-have-to-pay-an-innocent-mom-16000-after-police-wrecked-her-home-court-rules/" target="_blank" >barred</a> Hadley from seeking compensation under the Fifth Amendment, <a class="Link" href="https://ij.org/wp-content/uploads/2023/12/South-Bend-SWAT_7th-Cir.-Decision.pdf" target="_blank" >saying</a> the requirement does not cover "property damage resulting from police executing a lawful search warrant." In November, the U.S. Court of Appeals for the 9th Circuit likewise <a class="Link" href="https://reason.com/2025/12/29/innocent-man-sues-for-over-60000-after-police-blew-up-his-business-a-court-says-hes-entitled-to-nothing/" target="_blank" >blocked</a> Pena's bid for compensation, <a class="Link" href="https://ij.org/wp-content/uploads/2025/11/CA9-37-OPINION-11.4.2025.pdf" target="_blank" >saying</a> the takings clause does not apply to property damage "necessary for the defense of public safety."</p><p>The Institute for Justice, which represents both Hadley and Pena, argues that such exceptions are not supported by the text or history of the takings clause, which requires compensation precisely when property is taken "for public use," meaning the intervention is legitimate. The point is not to punish abuses but to prevent the government from "forcing some people alone to bear public burdens which, in all fairness and justice, should be borne by the public as a whole," as the Supreme Court <a class="Link" href="https://tile.loc.gov/storage-services/service/ll/usrep/usrep364/usrep364040/usrep364040.pdf" target="_blank" >put it</a> in 1960.</p><p>Such burdens manifestly include the cost of law enforcement, which aims to protect the public from dangerous criminals like the fugitives whom police were trying to apprehend when they assaulted Hadley's home and Pena's business. The need for compensation in such cases should be obvious, especially since insurers typically do not cover damage caused by the government.</p><p>Decisions blocking compensation, Pena's lawyers note, are based on the "flawed premise" that "when the government is acting for a good reason, it would somehow be unfair or unwise to 'penalize' it by requiring it to pay for the property it takes." That premise, they argue, "fundamentally misunderstands the nature of the Takings Clause, which starts from the assumption that the government is acting for a good reason."</p><p>The Supreme Court so far has <a class="Link" href="https://reason.com/2024/12/23/a-swat-team-destroyed-an-innocent-womans-house-the-supreme-court-wont-hear-her-case/" target="_blank" >declined</a> to resolve a circuit split on this issue. These cases give the justices another chance to correct the confusion that has left innocent property owners with bills they should not have to pay.</p><p><i>Jacob Sullum is a senior editor at Reason magazine.</i></p><div class="RichTextSidebarModule Enhancement" data-module data-align-center><a class="AnchorLink" id="module-b30000" name="module-b30000"></a> <div class="RichTextModule-items RichTextBody">Send letters to <a class="Link" href="mailto:letters@suntimes.com" target="_blank" >letters@suntimes.com</a>. More about <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/submitting-opinion-and-letters" >how to submit here</a>.</div> </div><p></p>
entries.author: Jacob Sullum

entries.title: Dear Trump supporters: How much more are you willing to ignore?
entries.summary: <p>Tuesday morning, the president of the United States threatened genocide against another nation, all because he does not know how to get out of a war he started to distract Americans from his terrible economy, disastrous immigration headlines and attempts to bury the Epstein files.</p><p>“A whole civilization will die tonight, never to be brought back again,” he <a class="Link" href="https://truthsocial.com/@realDonaldTrump/posts/116363336033995961" target="_blank" >wrote</a> on Truth Social, the social network he had to create so he can say deranged things like this without being deplatformed.</p><p>“I don’t want that to happen, but it probably will,” he continues flippantly, suggesting that he will “probably” nuke a nation that just a month ago he was insisting he wanted to liberate.</p><p>In case it needs pointing out, this threat of genocide would be a war crime, as are the many threats he’s made to strike Iranian infrastructure like water and bridges.</p><div class="RichTextSidebarModule Enhancement" data-module data-align-center><a class="AnchorLink" id="module-850000" name="module-850000"></a> <div class="RichTextSidebarModule-title">Columnists bug</div> <div class="RichTextModule-items RichTextBody"><h2>Columnists</h2><br><i>In-depth political coverage, sports analysis, entertainment reviews and cultural commentary.</i><br></div> </div><p>So as we sit on the precipice of what could be NUCLEAR WAR, my question to Donald Trump voters is this: What more are you willing to ignore?</p><p>What more are you willing to “compartmentalize” while you tell yourself this is all an acceptable bargain to get judges and tax cuts?</p><p>What indefensible, illiberal, un-American and inhumane garbage are you willing to defend in service of MAGA’s childish obsession with “owning the libs”?</p><p>How much more chaos, b.s., gaslighting, lying, grifting, incompetence and corruption are you willing to put up with?</p><p>And how much economic pain and destruction are you willing to suffer so that he can get rich and spend your taxpayer dollars bombing other countries, suing his political enemies and building monuments to himself?</p><p>Ten years ago, Trump told us exactly who he was, and he’s been revealing himself ever since.</p><p>He was a bad person then. During campaign rallies and his presidency, you watched as he made fun of people with disabilities, prisoners of war, women and children. You ignored it.</p><p>He was a bigot then. You watched as he defended neo-Nazis, called for travel bans against Muslims, called African countries “sh- -hole nations,” denigrated Black and Brown communities in American cities, called Mexican immigrants rapists and murderers, called himself a nationalist and welcomed white supremacists into the party. You ignored it.</p><p>He was a misogynist then. You watched as the “Access Hollywood” tape came out, as woman after woman told their stories of rape and sexual assault, as the Epstein rumors swirled for years, as porn stars revealed their affairs, as he made fun of women’s faces and biology. You ignored it.</p><p>He was a fraud then. You watched every story about the fraudulent Trump University and Trump Foundation, stories of tax fraud, licensing scams, real estate fraud, contractor nonpayment, election interference and now crypto corruption. You ignored it.</p><p>He was a terrible Christian then. You watched as he repeatedly broke nearly every Commandment, every tenet of Judeo-Christian faith, all while corrupting the religious right to sell Bibles with his name on them. You ignored it.</p><p>He was a terrible conservative then. You watched as he abandoned nearly every long-held conservative principle that Republicans had stood for, from lowering the debt and deficit, to anti-protectionism, to family values. You ignored it.</p><p>And he was a terrible leader then. You watched as he turned American against American, broke the trust of our allies, propped up our enemies, sent his own supporters down dangerous conspiracy theory rabbit holes, all while taking their hard-earned money to fight imaginary election fraud. You ignored it.</p><p>So what now? Does he need to nuke a nation to lose your trust and respect? Does he need to shoot someone in the middle of Fifth Avenue to test your moral compass? Does he need to bankrupt our country, destroy 250 years of American exceptionalism, undo 70-year-old international treaties and start World War III to get you to finally object to one more second of his unhinged lunacy?</p><p>If not now, when?</p><p>Sincerely,</p><p>Sane America</p><p><i>S.E. Cupp is the host of “S.E. Cupp Unfiltered” on CNN.</i></p><div class="RichTextSidebarModule Enhancement" data-module data-align-center><a class="AnchorLink" id="module-5b0000" name="module-5b0000"></a> <div class="RichTextModule-items RichTextBody">Send letters to <a class="Link" href="mailto:letters@suntimes.com" target="_blank" >letters@suntimes.com</a>. More about <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/submitting-opinion-and-letters" >how to submit here</a>.</div> </div><p></p>
entries.author: S. E. Cupp

entries.title: Dear Abby: Sibling ready to limit time with obnoxious sister
entries.summary: <p><b>DEAR ABBY:</b> For the past three years, my family has had reunions with my older half-sister, "Sybil." We did not grow up together, and we have very different lifestyles. I grew up with little money and numerous hardships; Sybil grew up having every need and want met. She's successful and wealthy.</p><p>I am proud of her and everything she has accomplished, but she is difficult to be around. Sybil is materialistic and talks constantly about everything she has. She's class-conscious and uncomfortable in "low-class" environments, like affordable restaurants and stores. It literally makes me ill.</p><p>I can no longer spend time with Sybil, which is why I won't be able to show up for family reunions the way I did before. How do I let Sybil know I can't do anything more than grab a bite to eat or have a cup of coffee to catch up? I don't want to hurt or offend her. How can I explain why I can no longer spend time with her? — <i>INCOMPATIBLE IN MONTANA</i></p><p><b>DEAR INCOMPATIBLE:</b> How about telling Sybil that you are busy and won't be coming to all of the gatherings that you used to attend, but you would love to catch up with her for a cup of coffee when they are over or at a later date? You do not have to give her or any other relative a more detailed explanation than that.</p><p><b>DEAR ABBY:</b> We live on a street with garage access via a commercial alley. My neighbor uses his garage to store a collector's vehicle along with bicycles and other boxed items. Every few months, he removes the car to clean his garage. He doesn't sweep; he blows the trash from the garage into the middle of the alley into a pile. Then he returns the car and other items, closes the garage door and leaves his trash in the alley.</p><p>Last week, the trash pile was 20 feet long, 3 feet wide and 3 inches high. The wind and cars that drive over the pile scatter the debris into neighbors' lots, commercial property parking areas, etc. On several occasions, I have spoken to him nicely, as have other neighbors, about sweeping up the pile and putting it into the trash receptacle, which is within 5 feet of the pile(s). The debris ultimately enters storm drains and the adjacent river, which pollutes our shoreline and environment.</p><p>This man always responds with "good to know" and "will do so next time," but the behavior hasn't changed in 10 years. He's a well-educated high school teacher at a prestigious school, so we are baffled about why he continues to trash the neighborhood with his mess. Any advice on what can be done? — <i>EXASPERATED IN CALIFORNIA</i></p><p><b>DEAR EXASPERATED:</b> I think 10 years is long enough to wait for your neighbor to show some consideration, don't you? Pick up the phone and contact your city's code enforcement bureau and report the address where this is happening. The bureau can help by sending an inspector to the property to assess and take photos. A case will be created if the property owner does not comply with the city's regulations.</p><p><i>Dear Abby is written by Abigail Van Buren, also known as Jeanne Phillips, and was founded by her mother, Pauline Phillips. Contact Dear Abby at www.DearAbby.com or P.O. Box 69440, Los Angeles, CA 90069.</i></p><p>To receive a collection of Abby's most memorable — and most frequently requested — poems and essays, send your name and mailing address, plus check or money order for $8 (U.S. funds) to: Dear Abby — Keepers Booklet, P.O. Box 446, Kings Mills, OH 45034-0446. (Shipping and handling are included in the price.)</p>
entries.author: Abigail Van Buren

entries.title: Rental listing site Domu launches mobile app for apartment hunters
entries.summary: <p>Searching for an apartment in Chicago?</p><p>Rental listing service Domu launched a mobile app to help renters in their hunt for a new apartment.</p><p>The app’s Thursday launch marks the company’s next step toward growth, as it expands its presence in a competitive real estate listing market dominated by titans such as Zillow and Apartments.com.</p><p>“We are competing and holding our own,” Domu CEO Sead Odzic said. “We are definitely very local, and the goal is to get more local.”</p><p>Odzic, who has a real estate background, bought the Chicago-based company from founder Noah Schatz and part-owner Andrew Porter in 2024. Odzic declined to share the purchase price, citing an agreement signed with the previous owners that prevents him from disclosing that information.</p><p>While the company is known for its large ads that plaster CTA buses and trains, Domu itself is small. The staff includes Odzic, an operations chief and an engineering chief focused on software development. The rest of the work is contracted out, mainly to workers around Chicago. For SEO support, it employs a worker based in Toronto.</p><p>Since Domu’s founding in 2010, the company has operated as a listing website. With the app’s launch, Odzic hopes to make Domu more user friendly, allowing renters to browse listings more quickly and efficiently.</p><p>Odzic said about 16% of their current users access the platform on their phones via Domu’s website.</p><p>The app, which has been in the works for nearly a year, will largely have the same features as the website — where renters can search by price and property amenities — but users’ ability to message landlords on the app and website is new for Domu.</p><p>“We have been known for our great inventory, and now [we're] going to be known for a great user experience after this hopefully,” Odzic said.</p><p>Property managers and owners will still need to post listings through the website. Domu’s current listings are mostly within city limits.</p><p>Odzic, who called himself a “luddite,” said in addition to the app, he has focused on building Domu’s brand by increasing its social media presence. He said he wants Chicagoans to better understand that Domu is a local company and not a big national corporation.</p><p>Since Odzic took over Domu, he has established more partnerships with local management companies. He said that has translated to growth in the number of listings on the site. In March 2024, there were about 900 listings. For the same period this year, there were over 2,000 listings, Odzic said. By next month, Domu expects more than 4,000 listings, though its ultimate goal is to have up to 6,000 listings on the site regularly.</p><p>The company’s revenue is through fees landlords pay to list their properties. Odzic declined to share Domu’s financial details.</p><p>Odzic positions Domu to landlords as the more affordable, local and easier-to-work-with company compared to national listing sites.</p><p>“We don’t provide the volume of leads the other companies do, but we provide quality leads,” Odzic said.</p><p>But he said owners who rent a single unit, may pay more on Domu than a site like Zillow, where you can list a property for free.</p><p>To list a unit on Domu, the company charges $40 a month. Domu is more affordable for bulk listings since owners can get bulk pricing, Odzic said. Its website shows pricing packages for as few as 10 units to as many as 100 units.</p><p>For mom-and-pop landlords, Domu staff can offer free advice on how to make the listings look their best, from photos to listings descriptions and price.</p><p>“We are the local guy, and we pick up the phone and you talk to a human being,” Odzic said.</p><p>Another new feature — and new revenue stream — for the company is providing credit reports for landlords through TransUnion. Odzic said his goal is to help mom-and-pop landlords easily perform credit checks for renters. The $50 credit check fee is paid by applicants, if a landlord who lists a property on Domu requests a credit check.</p><p>Domu’s future goal is to add more free resources for renters, including an interactive map of Chicago area neighborhoods where people can compare rent prices in different locations, Odzic said. He also wants to add more suburban listings.</p><p>“We have been very city-focused,” Odzic said. “The suburbs really don’t get the same amount of love so part of one of my goals is to get more listings in the suburbs.”</p><p>Odzic described Domu’s new app as the “first step to other things.”</p><p>“We don’t plan on stopping here,” he said.</p>
entries.author: Lizzie Kane

entries.title: Illinois regulators have little power to stop hospital shutdowns, lawmakers are stepping in to change that
entries.summary: <p>When West Suburban Medical Center abruptly closed its doors temporarily last month, saying it had run out of cash to pay staff, there was little warning for thousands of patients and hundreds of employees.</p><p>Illinois regulators tasked with overseeing hospitals had little power to stop it. They also might not be able to keep open some of the dozens of other safety net hospitals around the state that mostly treat low-income and immigrant communities amid <a class="Link" href="https://www.kff.org/medicaid/medicaid-what-to-watch-in-2026/" target="_blank" ><u>federal changes next year</u></a> that could drastically reduce how much money medical centers get paid.</p><p>But two Democratic state lawmakers are proposing <a class="Link" href="https://ilga.gov/Legislation/BillStatus/FullText?LegDocId=209492&DocName=10400SB3916sam001&DocNum=3916&DocTypeID=SB&LegID=167375&GAID=18&SessionID=114&SpecSess=&Session=" target="_blank" ><u>legislation</u></a> to help the state better prepare. Bills from state Sen. Laura Fine and state Rep. Robyn Gabel would require all hospitals to file plans with the state in case they close or scale back services to make sure patients don’t fall through the cracks. For-profit hospitals owned by investors, like West Suburban, also would have to give the state a deeper look into their finances. Now, that’s largely shielded from the public.</p><p>“The state, with legislation like this, is really stepping up to make sure that our hospital system is strong and that people are able to get the care that they need … so we are really ready for whatever happens in the future,” said Fine, who represents the northern suburbs and is sponsoring the Senate bill.</p><div class="Enhancement" data-align-hangRight> <div class="Enhancement-item" data-crop=""> <figure class="Figure"><a class="AnchorLink" id="image-df0000" name="image-df0000"></a> <picture data-crop="medium"> <source type="image/webp" width="490" height="275" data-srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/6a0dbc3/2147483647/strip/true/crop/3585x2012+0+189/resize/490x275!/format/webp/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F4a%2Ff1%2F1bcca8724d6899d0afd59e3c5a0a%2F2018election-047.JPG 1x,https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/a185255/2147483647/strip/true/crop/3585x2012+0+189/resize/980x550!/format/webp/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F4a%2Ff1%2F1bcca8724d6899d0afd59e3c5a0a%2F2018election-047.JPG 2x" data-lazy-load="true" srcset="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" /> <source width="490" height="275" data-srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/55b3b48/2147483647/strip/true/crop/3585x2012+0+189/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F4a%2Ff1%2F1bcca8724d6899d0afd59e3c5a0a%2F2018election-047.JPG" data-lazy-load="true" srcset="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" /> <img class="Image" alt="A portrait of State Sen. Laura Fine" srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/55b3b48/2147483647/strip/true/crop/3585x2012+0+189/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F4a%2Ff1%2F1bcca8724d6899d0afd59e3c5a0a%2F2018election-047.JPG 1x,https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/a24375c/2147483647/strip/true/crop/3585x2012+0+189/resize/980x550!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F4a%2Ff1%2F1bcca8724d6899d0afd59e3c5a0a%2F2018election-047.JPG 2x" width="490" height="275" data-src="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/55b3b48/2147483647/strip/true/crop/3585x2012+0+189/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F4a%2Ff1%2F1bcca8724d6899d0afd59e3c5a0a%2F2018election-047.JPG" data-lazy-load="true" src="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" > </picture> <div class="Figure-content"><figcaption class="Figure-caption"><p>State Sen. Laura Fine is proposing legislation that she hopes will better protect patients if there is a closure and requires hospitals to plan ahead.</p></figcaption><span class="line"></span><div class="Figure-credit"><p>Rich Hein/Sun-Times file</p></div></div> </figure> </div> </div><p>The proposed legislation was filed in February. Fine said she doesn’t know if it would have prevented West Suburban’s closure, “but it would have prepared us for it, and a plan would have been in place once that process started.”</p><p>When the Oak Park hospital suddenly closed, several lawmakers called for better state oversight, especially considering the millions of taxpayer dollars hospitals receive to treat patients and pay for operations.</p><p>“We do need to have a warning system,” said Democratic state Rep. Norma Hernandez, whose district includes West Suburban and <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/business/2022/3/10/22970989/westlake-hospital-owner-sell-chicago-area-hospitals-pipeline-health" target="_blank" ><u>Westlake Hospital</u></a>, which closed in 2019. “What they’re doing now, it should be illegal because it’s so harmful.”</p><p>She recommends tracking public dollars hospitals receive to understand how they’re spent, such as if they’re paying their bills on time.</p><p>Dr. Manoj Prasad, West Suburban’s owner and CEO of Resilience Healthcare, said he told state officials for months that he needed support and more money. But he owes <a class="Link" href="https://www.wbez.org/health-medicine/2026/04/01/west-suburban-medical-center-struggled-treat-patients-closed-vows-to-reopen-weiss-memorial-inspection-reports-oak-park" target="_blank" ><u>more than $100 million</u></a> to the state and vendors. A financial consultant reviewed the hospital’s finances, but it’s not complete, a state spokeswoman said, alleging the hospital hadn’t provided full transparency into its finances and operations.</p><p>Prasad <a class="Link" href="https://www.ilga.gov/ftp/JCAR/AdminCode/077/077011300B02400R.html" target="_blank" ><u>followed the law</u></a> when he suspended services at West Suburban. He said it’s temporary. He had 30 days to notify the state and local lawmakers after suspending services and explain why. Now he must provide updates every 30 days.</p><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="Enhancement-item" data-crop=""> <figure class="Figure"><a class="AnchorLink" id="image-bc0000" name="image-bc0000"></a> <picture data-crop="medium"> <source type="image/webp" width="490" height="275" data-srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/200e752/2147483647/strip/true/crop/6122x3436+0+324/resize/490x275!/format/webp/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F66%2F8d%2Fb96552d5461da2a9ce0de22027da%2Fwestsubfolo-260402-03-1.jpg 1x,https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/27cc906/2147483647/strip/true/crop/6122x3436+0+324/resize/980x550!/format/webp/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F66%2F8d%2Fb96552d5461da2a9ce0de22027da%2Fwestsubfolo-260402-03-1.jpg 2x" data-lazy-load="true" srcset="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" /> <source width="490" height="275" data-srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/de6cf38/2147483647/strip/true/crop/6122x3436+0+324/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F66%2F8d%2Fb96552d5461da2a9ce0de22027da%2Fwestsubfolo-260402-03-1.jpg" data-lazy-load="true" srcset="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" /> <img class="Image" alt="Dr. Manoj Prasad, who owns West Suburban Medical Center, speaks before a bank of microphones at the hospital in April." srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/de6cf38/2147483647/strip/true/crop/6122x3436+0+324/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F66%2F8d%2Fb96552d5461da2a9ce0de22027da%2Fwestsubfolo-260402-03-1.jpg 1x,https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/63e8885/2147483647/strip/true/crop/6122x3436+0+324/resize/980x550!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F66%2F8d%2Fb96552d5461da2a9ce0de22027da%2Fwestsubfolo-260402-03-1.jpg 2x" width="490" height="275" data-src="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/de6cf38/2147483647/strip/true/crop/6122x3436+0+324/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F66%2F8d%2Fb96552d5461da2a9ce0de22027da%2Fwestsubfolo-260402-03-1.jpg" data-lazy-load="true" src="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" > </picture> <div class="Figure-content"><figcaption class="Figure-caption"><p>Dr. Manoj Prasad, who owns West Suburban Medical Center, blames a new electronic medical record system for the hospital’s financial woes. Prasad said he had to suspend services because the system failed to bill for much of the hospital’s work for the last year. </p></figcaption><span class="line"></span><div class="Figure-credit"><p>Anthony Vazquez/Sun-Times</p></div></div> </figure> </div> </div><p>Dr. Linda Rae Murray, a retired local physician, understands how little power the state has to regulate hospitals. She was on the Illinois Health Facilities and Services Review Board, which regulates the health care industry, when Mercy Hospital on the Near South Side <a class="Link" href="https://www.wbez.org/environment-public-health/2021/10/25/inside-the-fight-to-save-chicagos-historic-mercy-hospital" target="_blank" ><u>planned to shutter</u></a> during the pandemic, before getting bought for $1. It’s now called Insight Hospital and Medical Center.</p><p>The board <a class="Link" href="https://www.wbez.org/environment-public-health/2020/12/15/state-regulators-say-mercy-hospital-must-stay-open-for-now" target="_blank" ><u>voted against Mercy’s closure</u></a>, but <a class="Link" href="https://www.wbez.org/environment-public-health/2021/03/23/historic-mercy-hospital-can-be-sold-the-next-hurdle-is-reviving-it" target="_blank" ><u>had to approve</u></a> the new owner. All it required was the new owner filling out paperwork correctly. Murray said she was so frustrated by the board’s broader policies and procedures that she left when her term was up.</p><p>She understands the frustration over West Suburban’s sudden closure. People have deep ties to their community hospital, and it’s often the biggest employer in the area.</p><p>But, Murray said, “I don’t understand how anyone can expect any state facility board or any other function of state government to force a hospital to stay open if, in fact, the hospital is losing money.”</p><p>She has an idea for how the state could toughen up: require hospitals to keep reserves for emergencies, like making payroll, and keep a closer eye on the quality of care provided.</p><p>Inspectors visited West Suburban <a class="Link" href="https://www.wbez.org/health-medicine/2026/04/01/west-suburban-medical-center-struggled-treat-patients-closed-vows-to-reopen-weiss-memorial-inspection-reports-oak-park" target="_blank" ><u>several times</u></a> over patient care issues before the hospital closed. Prasad <a class="Link" href="https://www.wbez.org/health-medicine/2025/08/08/weiss-memorial-hospital-emergency-room-in-uptown-closes" target="_blank" ><u>closed his other hospital</u></a>, Weiss Memorial in Uptown on Chicago’s North Side, last August.</p><h3><b>Bill would make all hospitals develop a ‘financial resolution plan’ </b></h3><p>Fine’s proposed legislation, which Gabel mirrors in the state House, would require all hospitals licensed by the state to submit a “financial resolution plan” in the event of “material financial distress.”</p><div class="Enhancement" data-align-hangRight> <div class="Enhancement-item" data-crop=""> <figure class="Figure"><a class="AnchorLink" id="image-110000" name="image-110000"></a> <picture data-crop="medium"> <source type="image/webp" width="490" height="275" data-srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/35e0ff9/2147483647/strip/true/crop/6122x3436+0+324/resize/490x275!/format/webp/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2Fa8%2F7e%2F35c582b74ec49381239f615096d7%2Fwestsubfolo-260402-14-1.jpg 1x,https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/f97c14d/2147483647/strip/true/crop/6122x3436+0+324/resize/980x550!/format/webp/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2Fa8%2F7e%2F35c582b74ec49381239f615096d7%2Fwestsubfolo-260402-14-1.jpg 2x" data-lazy-load="true" srcset="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" /> <source width="490" height="275" data-srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/366986b/2147483647/strip/true/crop/6122x3436+0+324/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2Fa8%2F7e%2F35c582b74ec49381239f615096d7%2Fwestsubfolo-260402-14-1.jpg" data-lazy-load="true" srcset="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" /> <img class="Image" alt="A sign at West Suburban Medical Center on April 1 alerting patients of its closure." srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/366986b/2147483647/strip/true/crop/6122x3436+0+324/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2Fa8%2F7e%2F35c582b74ec49381239f615096d7%2Fwestsubfolo-260402-14-1.jpg 1x,https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/d313012/2147483647/strip/true/crop/6122x3436+0+324/resize/980x550!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2Fa8%2F7e%2F35c582b74ec49381239f615096d7%2Fwestsubfolo-260402-14-1.jpg 2x" width="490" height="275" data-src="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/366986b/2147483647/strip/true/crop/6122x3436+0+324/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2Fa8%2F7e%2F35c582b74ec49381239f615096d7%2Fwestsubfolo-260402-14-1.jpg" data-lazy-load="true" src="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" > </picture> <div class="Figure-content"><figcaption class="Figure-caption"><p>A sign at West Suburban Medical Center on April 1 alerting patients of its closure.</p></figcaption><span class="line"></span><div class="Figure-credit"><p>Anthony Vazquez/Sun-Times</p></div></div> </figure> </div> </div><p>The plans would include everything from who owns the hospital, to how to safely transfer patients to other hospitals if their owners temporarily suspend services or close. Hospitals would need to identify potential gaps in services they would create, and what would happen to their employees and medical trainees.</p><p>Hospitals that don’t comply would be fined no more than $500 a week, or just over $25,000 a year.</p><p>For for-profit hospitals, there would be even more requirements: audited financial statements and monthly balance sheets, among other documents, filed with the state every quarter that could provide clues into whether those medical centers are struggling. If they don’t comply, the Illinois Department of Healthcare and Family Services, which oversees Medicaid public health insurance for people who are low-income or have a disability, could cut off funding.</p><p>The <a class="Link" href="https://www.kff.org/state-health-policy-data/state-indicator/hospitals-by-ownership/?currentTimeframe=0&sortModel=%7B%22colId%22:%22Location%22,%22sort%22:%22asc%22%7D" target="_blank" ><u>majority of hospitals </u></a>in Illinois are nonprofits, according to KFF, a health policy and research organization. Their finances are easier to find than a for-profit, from public tax returns filed with the government, to hospitals posting audited financial statements online.</p><p>Prasad said it would be too expensive to file audits each quarter. But he said it’s fair for the state to want to look deeper.</p><p>Fine hopes her legislation doesn’t get watered down during negotiations with the Illinois Health and Hospital Association, a powerful lobbyist for almost every hospital in the state and others.</p><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="Enhancement-item" data-crop=""> <figure class="Figure"><a class="AnchorLink" id="image-d50000" name="image-d50000"></a> <picture data-crop="medium"> <source type="image/webp" width="490" height="275" data-srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/1bf606c/2147483647/strip/true/crop/5088x2856+0+268/resize/490x275!/format/webp/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2Fe5%2F46%2Ffd59ac86482dbb46f20a1d5132ce%2Fpritzkersigning-073024-04.jpg 1x,https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/17ba6d3/2147483647/strip/true/crop/5088x2856+0+268/resize/980x550!/format/webp/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2Fe5%2F46%2Ffd59ac86482dbb46f20a1d5132ce%2Fpritzkersigning-073024-04.jpg 2x" data-lazy-load="true" srcset="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" /> <source width="490" height="275" data-srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/fac8338/2147483647/strip/true/crop/5088x2856+0+268/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2Fe5%2F46%2Ffd59ac86482dbb46f20a1d5132ce%2Fpritzkersigning-073024-04.jpg" data-lazy-load="true" srcset="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" /> <img class="Image" alt="State Representative Robyn Gabel speaks at a podium on July 29, 2024. " srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/fac8338/2147483647/strip/true/crop/5088x2856+0+268/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2Fe5%2F46%2Ffd59ac86482dbb46f20a1d5132ce%2Fpritzkersigning-073024-04.jpg 1x,https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/17f1f74/2147483647/strip/true/crop/5088x2856+0+268/resize/980x550!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2Fe5%2F46%2Ffd59ac86482dbb46f20a1d5132ce%2Fpritzkersigning-073024-04.jpg 2x" width="490" height="275" data-src="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/fac8338/2147483647/strip/true/crop/5088x2856+0+268/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2Fe5%2F46%2Ffd59ac86482dbb46f20a1d5132ce%2Fpritzkersigning-073024-04.jpg" data-lazy-load="true" src="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" > </picture> <div class="Figure-content"><figcaption class="Figure-caption"><p>State Rep. Robyn Gabel is sponsoring the legislation in the state House to try to hold hospitals more accountable.</p></figcaption><span class="line"></span><div class="Figure-credit"><p>Anthony Vazquez/Sun-Times file</p></div></div> </figure> </div> </div><p>A.J. Wilhelmi, president and CEO of the IHA, said some of the information that would be required is already reported to the state, such as how many licensed beds hospitals have.</p><p>“If the goal here is to have a better understanding of hospitals that are struggling so that there can be continuity of care in the event that service lines or hospitals close, let’s have that conversation,” Wilhelmi said. “But I think broadening that scope to the entire hospital community is challenging.”</p><p>Spokespeople for HFS and the Illinois public health department would not comment on the proposed legislation.</p><p><i>Contributing: Kaitlin Washburn</i></p>
entries.author: Kristen Schorsch | WBEZ

entries.title: avery r. young reimagines opera in world premiere "safronia"
entries.summary: <p>Inside Chicago’s grand Lyric Opera House, world-class singing is a given. What is far more rare in that vaunted venue, however, is an audience clapping and dancing along. But avery r. young has made that kind of enthusiasm the stated goal for his upcoming world premiere, “<a class="Link" href="https://www.lyricopera.org/shows/upcoming/2025-26/safronia/?gclsrc=aw.ds&gad_source=1&gad_campaignid=23610820506&gbraid=0AAAAA-D4NFPQOYU_gtICu0HStB3dVUD30&gclid=Cj0KCQjws83OBhD4ARIsACblj19715VzKToQyv6KwPchlodCU9YKIVa5lrHRRfo5s4G_Ppf94TBA-HIaAklGEALw_wcB#id_Artists" target="_blank" ><u>safronia</u></a>.”</p><p>“This is unlike any opera you have ever seen in your life and you don’t have to think it is improper to have a good time,” said young, 51, who forgoes capital letters for both his own name and those of his characters. “Our job is to not be the only people in the theater clapping and rocking. We shouldn’t feel successful until that happens.”</p><div class="Enhancement FormEnhancement" data-align-center> <div id="Form-newsletter-wrapper" class="Form-newsletter-wrapper" data-module data-lazy-load="true" src="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSI1NTVweCIgd2lkdGg9Ijg0MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" > </picture> </div> <div class="Form-newsletter-text-wrapper"> <div class="Form-newsletter-title"> Green Room Newsletter </div> <div class="Form-newsletter-submissionSuccess"> <p><b>You’re subscribed!</b></p><p></p><p>Please check your inbox for your confirmation.</p> </div> <div class="Form-newsletter-description">Stay ahead of what’s hitting Chicago stages, galleries, museums and more with Green Room, WBEZ’s weekly arts & culture newsletter!</div> <div class="Form-newsletter-errorMessage"><p>Sorry, there was an error registering your email.</p></div> </div> <form data-ajax-endpoint="https://sfmc-subscribe.wbez.org/v1/subscription" class="Form-newsletter-form" method="POST" > <div class="Form-newsletter-item"> <div class="EmailInput"><label class="EmailInput-label"><span>Email</span> <input class="EmailInput-input" type="email" name="email" placeholder="name@email.com" required aria-required="true"> </label> </div> <button class="Button" type="submit">Sign Up </button> </div> <div class="Form-items-item"> <input type="hidden" name="acquisition_campaign" value="ARTS_Affordance"> </div> <div class="Form-newsletter-disclaimer"> <p>By subscribing, you agree with WBEZ’s <a class="Link" href="https://www.wbez.org/terms-of-use" target="_blank" >Terms of Use</a> and <a class="Link" href="https://www.wbez.org/privacy-policy" target="_blank" >Privacy Policy</a></p> </div> </form> </bsp-form> </div> </div><p>Young’s mainstage debut, which will close Lyric’s season, follows the booker family, who have been banished from their land in Mississippi and forced to move north as part of <a class="Link" href="https://www.npr.org/2010/09/13/129827444/great-migration-the-african-american-exodus-north" target="_blank" ><u>the Great Migration</u></a>. During that period in the 20th century, an estimated 6 million Black Americans resettled in northern cities — like Chicago, Detroit and New York — in an attempt to find new opportunities and escape the discrimination of the Jim Crow era. </p><p>Young’s tale is loosely based on his own family history, although he notes, “it is not a documentary.” Instead, this work is his attempt to tell a specific story about a time period that is often perceived as a monolith. And, by bringing notes of blues, funk and gospel to the Lyric stage, young is boldly asking: What exactly is opera? He hopes this production — which runs April 17-18 — will help redefine the answer. And, throw open the doors of the opera house to new audiences and new kinds of storytelling.</p><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="Enhancement-item" data-crop=""> <figure class="Figure"><a class="AnchorLink" id="image-cf0000" name="image-cf0000"></a> <picture data-crop="medium"> <source type="image/webp" width="490" height="275" data-srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/7621fd2/2147483647/strip/true/crop/3840x2155+0+2/resize/490x275!/format/webp/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F6d%2Fde%2Fea12fb9541459bc89698312a64d3%2Fsafronia-c-vam-studio-1.jpg 1x,https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/e30364a/2147483647/strip/true/crop/3840x2155+0+2/resize/980x550!/format/webp/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F6d%2Fde%2Fea12fb9541459bc89698312a64d3%2Fsafronia-c-vam-studio-1.jpg 2x" data-lazy-load="true" srcset="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" /> <source width="490" height="275" data-srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/e5cc810/2147483647/strip/true/crop/3840x2155+0+2/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F6d%2Fde%2Fea12fb9541459bc89698312a64d3%2Fsafronia-c-vam-studio-1.jpg" data-lazy-load="true" srcset="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" /> <img class="Image" alt="safronia - c VAM Studio (1).jpg" srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/e5cc810/2147483647/strip/true/crop/3840x2155+0+2/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F6d%2Fde%2Fea12fb9541459bc89698312a64d3%2Fsafronia-c-vam-studio-1.jpg 1x,https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/0fd1f2c/2147483647/strip/true/crop/3840x2155+0+2/resize/980x550!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F6d%2Fde%2Fea12fb9541459bc89698312a64d3%2Fsafronia-c-vam-studio-1.jpg 2x" width="490" height="275" data-src="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/e5cc810/2147483647/strip/true/crop/3840x2155+0+2/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F6d%2Fde%2Fea12fb9541459bc89698312a64d3%2Fsafronia-c-vam-studio-1.jpg" data-lazy-load="true" src="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" > </picture> <div class="Figure-content"><figcaption class="Figure-caption"><p>Young wrote both the libretto and score for, “safronia.” Plus, he appears on stage in the role of the family’s patriarch. He began developing the work after collaborating with Lyric on “Twilight: Gods” in 2021.</p></figcaption><span class="line"></span><div class="Figure-credit"><p>Courtesy of VAM Studio </p></div></div> </figure> </div> </div><p>“There are gonna be folks who are like, ‘This is not opera.’ And I'm going to say, ‘But, why not?’ From everything that I've studied opera to be, this is exactly what an opera is,” said young. “It doesn't sound like [‘The Marriage of Figaro’], but this music was built to communicate what a person is going through.”</p><p>Young, a native of the city’s West Side, is perhaps best known for being <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/entertainment-and-culture/2023/4/24/23693586/avery-r-young-named-chicago-poet-laureate" target="_blank" ><u>Chicago’s first-ever poet laureate</u></a>, a role he held until late last year. Any time spent in young’s orbit makes clear what a creative force he is as a multi-hyphenate artist with seemingly endless energy.</p><div class="RichTextSidebarModule Enhancement" data-module data-align-center><a class="AnchorLink" id="module-db0000" name="module-db0000"></a> <div class="RichTextModule-items RichTextBody"><h3><a class="Link" href="https://www.lyricopera.org/shows/upcoming/2025-26/safronia/" target="_blank" ><u>safronia</u></a></h3><br><b>When: </b>April 17-18<br><b>Where: </b>Lyric Opera House, 20 N. Wacker Dr.<br><b>Tickets:</b> From $54</div> </div><p>On a recent day backstage at Lyric, he sports a denim jacket, camo pants and sneakers, as he recounts how this collaboration began. During the COVID-19 pandemic, young worked with the company as a librettist and performer for “<a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/entertainment-and-culture/2021/4/27/22405922/lyric-opera-twilight-gods-wagner-drive-through-underground-garage-ring-cycle-millennium-lakeside" target="_blank" ><u>Twilight: Gods</u></a>,” a drive-through Wagner adaptation presented in an underground parking garage.</p><p>After that run, Lyric’s then-general director, Anthony Freud, <a class="Link" href="https://www.wbez.org/theater-stages/2024/06/24/lyric-opera-chicago-future-anthony-freud-departure" target="_blank" ><u>who retired in 2024</u></a>, asked young if there was a story he was interested in developing. At Freud’s encouragement, young began writing not only the libretto for “safronia,” but also the score, despite, as he puts it, not being a “proper musician.”</p><p>“I cannot, to this day, sit at a piano or strum a guitar, what I can do is kind of beatbox and hum,” said young, who immediately began reciting a beat. “That's how I construct a song and that's how I constructed this whole opera.”</p><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="Enhancement-item" data-crop=""> <figure class="Figure"><a class="AnchorLink" id="image-ba0000" name="image-ba0000"></a> <picture data-crop="medium"> <source type="image/webp" width="490" height="275" data-srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/86e7920/2147483647/strip/true/crop/5273x2959+0+279/resize/490x275!/format/webp/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F59%2F94%2F441370224164a16d2bd27827c177%2Flyricseason-031225-03.jpg 1x,https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/cbfa6a1/2147483647/strip/true/crop/5273x2959+0+279/resize/980x550!/format/webp/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F59%2F94%2F441370224164a16d2bd27827c177%2Flyricseason-031225-03.jpg 2x" data-lazy-load="true" srcset="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" /> <source width="490" height="275" data-srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/0b7fc81/2147483647/strip/true/crop/5273x2959+0+279/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F59%2F94%2F441370224164a16d2bd27827c177%2Flyricseason-031225-03.jpg" 1x,https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/6af0361/2147483647/strip/true/crop/5273x2959+0+279/resize/980x550!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F59%2F94%2F441370224164a16d2bd27827c177%2Flyricseason-031225-03.jpg 2x" width="490" height="275" data-src="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/0b7fc81/2147483647/strip/true/crop/5273x2959+0+279/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F59%2F94%2F441370224164a16d2bd27827c177%2Flyricseason-031225-03.jpg" data-lazy-load="true" src="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" > </picture> <div class="Figure-content"><figcaption class="Figure-caption"><p>“I really support avery’s work with all my heart,” said Enrique Mazzola (right), Lyric’s music director.</p></figcaption><span class="line"></span><div class="Figure-credit"><p>Anthony Vazquez/Sun-Times</p></div></div> </figure> </div> </div><p>Young will also appear on stage as baar booker, the family’s patriarch. Other cast members include Chicago’s own <a class="Link" href="https://www.wbez.org/music/2025/10/23/notes-peace-chicago-gun-violence-memorial-chicago-cso-symphony-orchestra-purpose-over-pain" target="_blank" ><u>Meagan McNeal</u></a> playing the titular safronia booker, the family’s loyal youngest daughter; Broadway actor Maiesha McQueen as the steady matriarch, magnolia booker; and Jeff Award-winner Lorenzo Rush Jr. who portrays safronia’s husband, king willie tate. All three singers — along with the show’s director, Timothy Douglas (who directed the opera “<a class="Link" href="https://www.wbez.org/theater-stages/2025/05/27/she-who-dared-chicago-opera-theatre-jasmine-barnes-deborah-deep-mouton" target="_blank" ><u>She Who Dared</u></a>” last year in Chicago) — are making their Lyric debut.</p><p>The concert-style show, which has the energy of a Sunday church service, is billed as a mix of folklore, poetry and history. The Lyric orchestra appears on stage, alongside members of young’s blues and funk ensemble, de deacon board. Together, the sound is an intricate mixture of strings, winds and brass with electric bass, organ and harmonica, courtesy of the Grammy-nominated Blues legend, Billy Branch.</p><p>Young said he wanted it to sound like, “you're walking down Chicago Avenue and you stumble upon a church where Curtis Mayfield is conducting Parliament-Funkadelic, but Mavis [Staples] and her sisters and daddy are singing. That's what I wanted to make the world.”</p><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="Enhancement-item" data-crop=""> <figure class="Figure"><a class="AnchorLink" id="image-d70000" name="image-d70000"></a> <picture data-crop="medium"> <source type="image/webp" width="490" height="275" data-srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/caeb69b/2147483647/strip/true/crop/6122x3436+0+324/resize/490x275!/format/webp/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2Ff6%2Fb8%2F3e50bfd141649f39110f7afc2901%2Flyricseason-031225-11.jpg 1x,https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/6401814/2147483647/strip/true/crop/6122x3436+0+324/resize/980x550!/format/webp/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2Ff6%2Fb8%2F3e50bfd141649f39110f7afc2901%2Flyricseason-031225-11.jpg
