width="490" height="275" data-srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/16fb4a4/2147483647/strip/true/crop/6122x3436+0+324/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2Ff6%2Fb8%2F3e50bfd141649f39110f7afc2901%2Flyricseason-031225-11.jpg" data-lazy-load="true" srcset="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" /> <img class="Image" alt="LYRICSEASON-031225-11.jpg" srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/16fb4a4/2147483647/strip/true/crop/6122x3436+0+324/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2Ff6%2Fb8%2F3e50bfd141649f39110f7afc2901%2Flyricseason-031225-11.jpg 1x,https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/098af39/2147483647/strip/true/crop/6122x3436+0+324/resize/980x550!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2Ff6%2Fb8%2F3e50bfd141649f39110f7afc2901%2Flyricseason-031225-11.jpg 2x" width="490" height="275" data-src="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/16fb4a4/2147483647/strip/true/crop/6122x3436+0+324/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2Ff6%2Fb8%2F3e50bfd141649f39110f7afc2901%2Flyricseason-031225-11.jpg" data-lazy-load="true" src="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" > </picture> <div class="Figure-content"><figcaption class="Figure-caption"><p>Rocker Billy Corgan and young appear at Lyric’s 2025-26 season announcement. Corgan’s “A Night of Mellon Collie and Infinite Sadness” and young’s “safronia” both marked new kinds of ventures for the classical company, as it looks to attract younger and more diverse audiences to its grand house.</p></figcaption><span class="line"></span><div class="Figure-credit"><p>Anthony Vazquez/Sun-Times</p></div></div> </figure> </div> </div><p>“safronia” draws its name both from young’s grandmother and as a nod to Nina Simone’s “Four Women.” The production is Lyric’s latest attempt to expand beyond the classical opera repertoire in order to reach younger and more diverse audiences. Last fall, the company teamed up with Smashing Pumpkins frontman Billy Corgan for a reimagined presentation of the band’s seminal album, “<a class="Link" href="https://www.wbez.org/music/2025/11/18/billy-corgan-smashing-pumpkins-night-of-mellon-collie-infinite-sadness-lyric-opera" target="_blank" ><u>Mellon Collie and the Infinite Sadness</u></a>.” The seven-show run was completely sold out and company leadership has high hopes for “safronia.”</p><p>“I really support avery’s work with all my heart,” Enrique Mazzola, Lyric’s music director, said last spring when the company announced the work. “When I think about what is the future of American opera, this is a possible answer.”</p><p>The story begins years after banishment, when the family returns to the South to give their patriarch a proper burial and face the forces that drove them away. But despite the heaviness of the storyline, the music is often quick and punchy. At an early workshop, one singer asked young, “Why do they sound so happy?”</p><p>“I said, well, I don't think we're at the right H word. The song's not fast because they're happy. The song is fast because they're in a hurry,” young said. “One of the things that I wanted to also convey with this music is: Despite the hardships that this family endures, they rock and roll through it.”</p><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="Enhancement-item" data-crop=""> <figure class="Figure"><a class="AnchorLink" id="image-2c0000" name="image-2c0000"></a> <picture data-crop="medium"> <source type="image/webp" width="490" height="275" data-srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/2e547b3/2147483647/strip/true/crop/4320x2424+0+228/resize/490x275!/format/webp/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F4e%2F74%2Ffc124a6e4a26a177d990fca58855%2Ffloydfeature-10xx25-15.jpg 1x,https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/1c4b658/2147483647/strip/true/crop/4320x2424+0+228/resize/980x550!/format/webp/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F4e%2F74%2Ffc124a6e4a26a177d990fca58855%2Ffloydfeature-10xx25-15.jpg 2x" data-lazy-load="true" srcset="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" /> <source width="490" height="275" data-srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/83ec2dc/2147483647/strip/true/crop/4320x2424+0+228/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F4e%2F74%2Ffc124a6e4a26a177d990fca58855%2Ffloydfeature-10xx25-15.jpg" data-lazy-load="true" srcset="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" /> <img class="Image" alt="avery r. young" srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/83ec2dc/2147483647/strip/true/crop/4320x2424+0+228/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F4e%2F74%2Ffc124a6e4a26a177d990fca58855%2Ffloydfeature-10xx25-15.jpg 1x,https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/2301d85/2147483647/strip/true/crop/4320x2424+0+228/resize/980x550!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F4e%2F74%2Ffc124a6e4a26a177d990fca58855%2Ffloydfeature-10xx25-15.jpg 2x" width="490" height="275" data-src="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/83ec2dc/2147483647/strip/true/crop/4320x2424+0+228/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F4e%2F74%2Ffc124a6e4a26a177d990fca58855%2Ffloydfeature-10xx25-15.jpg" data-lazy-load="true" src="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" > </picture> <div class="Figure-content"><figcaption class="Figure-caption"><p>Young, a native of the city’s West Side, is perhaps best known for being Chicago’s first-ever poet laureate. Any time spent in his orbit makes clear what a force he is as an interdisciplinary artist with seemingly endless energy.</p></figcaption><span class="line"></span><div class="Figure-credit"><p>Nima Taradji/For The Sun Times.</p></div></div> </figure> </div> </div><p>While they may be resilient, young is not rewriting history. The show sits squarely in the struggles and continued discrimination that Black families endured once they reached cities like Chicago.</p><p>“They used the Green Book to get red-lined,” young said, referring to the guidebook that alerted Black travelers to hotels and restaurants they were safe to patronize and the systemic discrimination that kept many Black families from buying homes. The trauma of that period had lasting effects on young’s family, including his grandmother, for whom the show is named.</p><p>“She never came to grips with the circumstances that led them to have to leave where they were from,” young said, noting that the pain of forced migration is something many populations globally can relate to. “I think she would be proud that I didn't give her this ‘well, all ended well’ [story], because it didn't.” </p><p>It was that unvarnished rawness that first attracted young to opera (and why he says Bonnie Raitt’s “I Can’t Make You Love Me” is his favorite song). He saw a show from the Baroque period in high school and the universality of the story struck him immediately. Sitting in the theater, he said he thought, “Oh, my goodness, these women around this well is like my aunties when they were sitting at the table playing cards or cooking. It's the same thing.”</p><p>As young prepares to bring his story to life, it already has legs beyond Lyric. Last week, Chicago’s <a class="Link" href="https://www.courttheatre.org/season-tickets/2026-2027-season/safronia/" target="_blank" ><u>Court Theatre announced</u></a> that “safronia” will close its next season in spring 2027, when the work will be reimagined as a fully-staged production.</p><p>But still, young hopes these hometown showings are just the beginning — and that someday “safronia” will travel the same path that his ancestors did during the Great Migration and be shown at opera houses from the South to the North.</p><p><i>Courtney Kueppers is an arts and culture reporter at WBEZ. </i></p>
entries.author: Courtney Kueppers | WBEZ

entries.title: Refugee families are the latest group to face SNAP food benefit cutoff
entries.summary: <p>K.Q. was sharing a hotel room with her husband and four children when she received a card loaded with funds from the Supplemental Nutrition Assistance Program.</p><p>The family had just arrived in Chicago, a city where they knew no one, after being accepted into the U.S. refugee resettlement program. They came from Lebanon, where they spent 12 years after fleeing Syria’s deadly civil war.</p><p>She took her four children to the grocery store, telling them they could buy anything they would like with the federal food assistance. It was a rare treat for the family that in Lebanon struggled to make ends meet.</p><p>“We are so happy because I can go buy everything for my children,” said K.Q., who asked not to be fully identified out of fear it could harm her application to become a legal permanent resident. “In Lebanon we had [a] hard time, and we don’t have enough money to buy anything the children need.”</p><p>But after less than three years here, they are on the verge of losing food assistance, along with thousands of other refugees, asylees and survivors of human trafficking. President Donald Trump’s sweeping tax overhaul law that passed last year limits which immigrants can access the SNAP food program, along with <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/politics/2026/03/13/older-chicagoans-high-risk-of-losing-snap-food-assistance-new-work-rules-kick-in-55-64-may" >enacting expanded work requirements</a> for others. The changes for immigrants went into effect in Illinois on April 1 and are part of broader restrictions on the refugee resettlement program.</p><p>As many as 16,000 people could lose SNAP benefits because of the change in <a class="Link" href="https://www.dhs.state.il.us/?item=177780" target="_blank" ><u>eligibility for immigrants</u></a>, according to the Illinois Department of Human Services.</p><p>Trump’s policy changes put refugees in a Catch-22, resettlement agencies say. They can only receive SNAP benefits once they become legal permanent residents. But the federal government isn’t processing their green card applications. Historically, refugees have been given a legal form of immigration with a pathway to citizenship.</p><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="Enhancement-item" data-crop=""> <figure class="Figure"><a class="AnchorLink" id="image-8f0000" name="image-8f0000"></a> <picture data-crop="medium"> <source type="image/webp" width="490" height="275" data-srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/f682765/2147483647/strip/true/crop/5429x3047+0+287/resize/490x275!/format/webp/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2Fc0%2F79%2F16b9e8534378bf1398c78fab4970%2Fsnaprefugee-260407-02.jpg 1x,https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/4a0b6fb/2147483647/strip/true/crop/5429x3047+0+287/resize/980x550!/format/webp/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2Fc0%2F79%2F16b9e8534378bf1398c78fab4970%2Fsnaprefugee-260407-02.jpg 2x" data-lazy-load="true" srcset="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" /> <source width="490" height="275" data-srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/c81c6a1/2147483647/strip/true/crop/5429x3047+0+287/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2Fc0%2F79%2F16b9e8534378bf1398c78fab4970%2Fsnaprefugee-260407-02.jpg" data-lazy-load="true" srcset="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" /> <img class="Image" alt="A photo of a Syrian refugee named K.Q., showing her from the neck down." srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/c81c6a1/2147483647/strip/true/crop/5429x3047+0+287/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2Fc0%2F79%2F16b9e8534378bf1398c78fab4970%2Fsnaprefugee-260407-02.jpg 1x,https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/51cd350/2147483647/strip/true/crop/5429x3047+0+287/resize/980x550!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2Fc0%2F79%2F16b9e8534378bf1398c78fab4970%2Fsnaprefugee-260407-02.jpg 2x" width="490" height="275" data-src="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/c81c6a1/2147483647/strip/true/crop/5429x3047+0+287/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2Fc0%2F79%2F16b9e8534378bf1398c78fab4970%2Fsnaprefugee-260407-02.jpg" data-lazy-load="true" src="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" > </picture> <div class="Figure-content"><figcaption class="Figure-caption"><p>K.Q. had hoped to get her GED diploma before trying to find a job but says she will now likely have to find work to make up for the expected loss in SNAP benefits for her family. </p></figcaption><span class="line"></span><div class="Figure-credit"><p>Ashlee Rezin/Sun-Times</p></div></div> </figure> </div> </div><p>For K.Q., this change has left her feeling confused and unsure how she will feed her family.</p><p>“We came here because they chose us to come,” she said. “Why now they [don’t] give us … help us to live here, because, here — it’s expensive.”</p><h3>Cloud of uncertainty for refugees </h3><p>For decades in the U.S., when refugees first arrived they could tap into SNAP and other safety-net programs, like Medicaid health insurance. That support was critical, people in refugee resettlement say, to provide stability because refugees' first jobs typically pay minimum wage. Most of their earnings go towards rent and other basic expenses.</p><p>“Refugees have counted on this vital part of the safety net to help them as they are trying to start over here in Chicago,” said Sally Schulze, communications manager for RefugeeOne, a Chicago resettlement nonprofit.</p><p>That was the case for K.Q. and her family, who lived in one hotel room for two months before they could move into an apartment in Chicago. The family initially received about $1,100 in monthly SNAP benefits, which was later reduced to $635 once her husband started working at an area airport. He is the sole breadwinner while K.Q. has focused on improving her English through classes with a community group.</p><p>The loss of SNAP is just one challenge refugees are facing. The Trump administration lowered the number of refugees allowed to 7,500 for the current fiscal year, the fewest since the program started in 1980, <a class="Link" href="https://www.npr.org/2025/10/30/g-s1-95823/trump-administration-sets-lowest-ever-cap-on-refugee-admissions" target="_blank" >NPR previously reported</a>. That’s down from 125,000 in 2025 under President Joe Biden.</p><p>Fears also persist in the community of being swept up in raids and ending up in an immigration detention center, advocates say.</p><p>World Relief Chicagoland, another local resettlement agency, has been meeting with families in anticipation of government plans to re-interview them, essentially serving as a second vetting of their refugee status, said Susan Sperry, executive director of World Relief Chicagoland.</p><div class="RichTextSidebarModule Enhancement" data-module data-align-center><a class="AnchorLink" id="module-9c0000" name="module-9c0000"></a> <div class="RichTextModule-items RichTextBody"><b>How to Help Refugee Families </b><br><ul class="rte2-style-ul" id="rte-434fda32-3346-11f1-aa5e-5198da5caf11"><li><b>Household items</b>: RefugeeOne is stocking up on toiletries, cleaning supplies and diapers to minimize expenses for families. The group has an<a class="Link" href="https://www.amazon.com/registries/gl/guest-view/HW4ZYERO21XP?ref_=cm_sw_r_cp_ud_ggr-subnav-share_TF4S1M04JE8K96EAMR2Z" target="_blank" ><u> Amazon wish list</u></a>, <a class="Link" href="https://tinyurl.com/3kykf68s" target="_blank" ><u>https://tinyurl.com/3kykf68s</u></a></li><li><b>Grocery gift cards</b>: Resettlement agencies are collecting grocery gift cards to provide immediate assistance to refugee families. Those cards can be mailed to RefugeeOne at 6008 N. California Ave., Chicago, IL, 60659, or World Relief Chicagoland at 191 S. Gary Ave., Suite 130, Carol Stream, IL, 60188.</li><li><b>Support local pantries</b>: Groups are expecting more refugees to depend on local food pantries. For a list of pantries, <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/chicago/2025/10/29/where-to-find-chicago-area-food-pantries-if-snap-goes-away" target="_blank" ><u>visit https://chicago.suntimes.com/chicago/2025/10/29/where-to-find-chicago-area-food-pantries-if-snap-goes-away</u></a>.</li></ul></div> </div><p>And last November, the U.S. Citizenship and Immigration Services in an internal memo said it was no longer processing refugee applications for green cards, according to the American Immigration Lawyers Association. It typically took about 13 months to receive a green card after applying.</p><p>In a statement, USCIS confirmed it paused adjudications for immigrants from countries deemed high risk by the Trump administration. It did not say when the pause would end.</p><p>“National security and the protection of every American must take precedence, even when weighed against potential challenges such as workforce shortages,” the agency stated in an email, adding that it believes the Biden’s administration did not properly vet immigrants.</p><p>Syria, where K.Q. and her family fled, is among the countries deemed high risk because it’s still emerging from civil unrest and “lacks an adequate central authority for issuing passports or civil documents,” according to the White House.</p><p>The family applied for legal permanent residency about a year ago, but like others, they’ve received no updates on their application.</p><p>“I hope everything could change because also here we need to be safe,” K.Q. said. “[If] we have [a] green card, we can stay here. I’m worried about, yes, about my children, about me.”</p><h3>‘The math just doesn’t add up’</h3><p>RefugeeOne anticipates 175 families, or 850 individuals they serve will lose SNAP benefits at some point this year, the majority of them children, Schulze said. World Relief Chicagoland expects more than 300 of their families will lose benefits.</p><p>The changes in green card applications adds to the uncertainty.</p><p>“Without a green card, that means they aren’t going to be able to get this food assistance that they desperately need for months, possibly even years, unless lawmakers make some changes,” Schulze said.</p><div class="Enhancement" alt="Sally Schulze sits in the RefugeeOne main office in Chicago's West Ridge neighborhood. The resettlement agency expects 850 individuals they serve, the majority of them children, will lose food assistance starting this month. | Candace Dane Chambers/Sun-Times" srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/7c5c018/2147483647/strip/true/crop/1024x575+0+54/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F9b%2F68%2Feeaf247341df9f654bcfe6cbb7af%2Fsnaprefugee-260409-005.jpg 1x,https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/62d802a/2147483647/strip/true/crop/1024x575+0+54/resize/980x550!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F9b%2F68%2Feeaf247341df9f654bcfe6cbb7af%2Fsnaprefugee-260409-005.jpg 2x" width="490" height="275" data-src="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/7c5c018/2147483647/strip/true/crop/1024x575+0+54/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F9b%2F68%2Feeaf247341df9f654bcfe6cbb7af%2Fsnaprefugee-260409-005.jpg" data-lazy-load="true" src="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" > </picture> <div class="Figure-content"><figcaption class="Figure-caption"><p>Sally Schulze sits in the RefugeeOne main office in Chicago’s West Ridge neighborhood. The resettlement agency expects 850 individuals they serve, the majority of them children, will lose food assistance starting this month. </p></figcaption><span class="line"></span><div class="Figure-credit"><p>Candace Dane Chambers/Sun-Times</p></div></div> </figure> </div> </div><p>She said her organization hears from families expecting benefits to end this month.</p><p>World Relief Chicagoland is trying to help refugees find additional jobs or ways to increase their income, Sperry said. RefugeeOne is also meeting with families to look at their household budgets to see what, if anything, could be cut.</p><p>“The math just doesn’t add up for so many of these families,” Schulze said. “Because especially for those with children, you just can’t make ends meet without SNAP.”</p><p>K.Q. is crunching the numbers for her family, but it’s hard to see how they will make up for the $635 they will lose in SNAP. She uses it to buy bread, cooking oil and Halal meat.</p><p>She hauls a black shopping cart to her English classes, where there’s also a weekly food pantry. During a recent trip, she grabbed two jugs of milk, lasagna pasta, eggs and a pineapple to supplement her family’s groceries. The family typically runs out of SNAP benefits before the end of the month.</p><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="Enhancement-item" data-crop=""> <figure class="Figure"><a class="AnchorLink" id="image-f90000" name="image-f90000"></a> <picture data-crop="medium"> <source type="image/webp" width="490" height="275" data-srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/1414965/2147483647/strip/true/crop/7001x3929+0+370/resize/490x275!/format/webp/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F11%2Fb9%2F7492a76c47bba94638e8b9ee42e4%2Fsnaprefugee-260407-03.jpg 1x,https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/1d47aba/2147483647/strip/true/crop/7001x3929+0+370/resize/980x550!/format/webp/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F11%2Fb9%2F7492a76c47bba94638e8b9ee42e4%2Fsnaprefugee-260407-03.jpg 2x" data-lazy-load="true" srcset="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" /> <source width="490" height="275" data-srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/7daae82/2147483647/strip/true/crop/7001x3929+0+370/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F11%2Fb9%2F7492a76c47bba94638e8b9ee42e4%2Fsnaprefugee-260407-03.jpg" data-lazy-load="true" srcset="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" /> <img class="Image" alt="A woman's black shopping cart with milk and other groceries." srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/7daae82/2147483647/strip/true/crop/7001x3929+0+370/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F11%2Fb9%2F7492a76c47bba94638e8b9ee42e4%2Fsnaprefugee-260407-03.jpg 1x,https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/5cbff1b/2147483647/strip/true/crop/7001x3929+0+370/resize/980x550!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F11%2Fb9%2F7492a76c47bba94638e8b9ee42e4%2Fsnaprefugee-260407-03.jpg 2x" width="490" height="275" data-src="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/7daae82/2147483647/strip/true/crop/7001x3929+0+370/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F11%2Fb9%2F7492a76c47bba94638e8b9ee42e4%2Fsnaprefugee-260407-03.jpg" data-lazy-load="true" src="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" > </picture> <div class="Figure-content"><figcaption class="Figure-caption"><p>K.Q. stocks up a local food pantry to supplement what she can afford with her SNAP benefits. </p></figcaption><span class="line"></span><div class="Figure-credit"><p>Ashlee Rezin/Sun-Times</p></div></div> </figure> </div> </div><p>K.Q. saw the language classes as a way to one day obtain a better paying job. But she now thinks she will likely have to look for a job sooner, though she was just reaching the classes to prepare for the GED exam.</p><p>“I don’t know what we can do, but I worry about that,” she said.</p><p>In other refugee families, young adults in school will have to pivot to finding full-time work to help feed the family, Schulze said.</p><p>On the state level, the Greater Chicago Food Depository and the Latino Policy Forum are advocating for a bill to provide cash assistance to immigrants, such as refugees, who will lose SNAP. The measure is pending in the state Legislature.</p><p>Both resettlement groups are also referring families to pantries and collecting grocery store gift cards to help families.</p><p>“This is not sustainable,” Schulze said. “We just can’t replace a federal government program by collecting gift cards.”</p><h3>Dreaming of a college-bound son </h3><p>K.Q.’s husband now paints furniture for a living, making just enough to survive. Their three younger children attend school and K.Q. looks after them. Although their rent has increased, they don’t want to risk moving and missing important letters about their immigration case.</p><p>“We do anything the government tells us,” she said.</p><p>She didn’t know anything about Chicago before living here, but she knew she needed to get her family out of Lebanon as Syria remained unstable. Her oldest son had to leave school in Lebanon to work and supplement her husband’s wages. K.Q. said Syrian children only received about four hours of schooling with limited subjects.</p><p>In Chicago, she has seen her children flourish in school. They have picked up English and are making friends. One of her sons, an 11th grader, is dreaming of going away to college to study computer science.</p><p>“He’s so excited about that, and I am so happy,” K.Q. said, lighting up. “He asked me before … ‘Mom, if I take a scholarship for college far from here, do you mind that?’ I told him, ‘No, this is your future, but I will miss you.’”</p><div class="RichTextSidebarModule Enhancement" data-module data-align-center><a class="AnchorLink" id="module-b90000" name="module-b90000"></a> <div class="RichTextModule-items RichTextBody"><h3>READ MORE</h3><ul class="rte2-style-ul" id="rte-9bf28172-1cac-11f1-b524-93678b2f15cd"><li><a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/politics/2026/03/13/older-chicagoans-high-risk-of-losing-snap-food-assistance-new-work-rules-kick-in-55-64-may" >Older Chicagoans at high risk of losing SNAP food assistance as new work rules kick in</a></li><li><a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/news/2026/01/26/new-snap-work-rules-leave-unemployed-englewood-man-thousands-of-others-at-risk-food-benefits-cut-off-trump" >New SNAP work rules leave unemployed Englewood man and thousands of others at risk</a></li><li><a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/news/2026/02/06/walmart-amazon-cps-top-employers-illinois-workers-still-need-snap-benefits-trump-work-requirements-lose-benefits" >Walmart, Amazon and CPS top list of employers where Illinois workers still need SNAP benefits</a></li><li><a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/health/2026/01/21/new-snap-work-requirements-cook-county-warn-health-consequences-cut-off-congress-donald-trump-food-stamps" >As new SNAP rules loom, Cook County officials warn of health consequences for people cut off</a></li></ul></div> </div><p></p>
entries.author: Elvia Malagón

entries.title: Want a better way to bond with your teen? Try writing a novel together
entries.summary: <p>The Pulitzer Prize-winning author Richard Ford was once asked for his 10 rules for writing. Rule No. 2: “Don’t have children.”</p><p>Of course, there are plenty of other successful authors who’ve had kids.</p><p>There’s also another way: Write <i>with</i> your children.</p><p>That’s what Ty Thompson has been doing since November 2022. He and his 14-year-old daughter, Vaughn, who live in Lake View, have just finished book II of their fantasy series, “Daniel the Different & the Unfinished Prophecy.”</p><p>And theirs might be one solution for parents looking to both bond with a teen and cobble together an itinerary for the looming summer break.</p><p>“I had thought that if we write a few chapters and she gets bored with it and wants to stop, then we’ll stop,” said Vaughn’s dad, chatting in the kitchen of their sunlight-flooded home. “We were having so much fun writing it that we kept going.”</p><p>Ty Thompson, 45, isn’t a writer. He’s in sales, but he recognized early on his daughter’s love of reading and writing.</p><p>“I love to read a lot of things, but fantasy is the thing that I always come back to,” Vaughn said, breaking into a grin revealing a full set of braces.</p><p>“At bedtime, she just blurted out, ‘Wouldn’t it be great to write a story about a boy who falls asleep and wakes up in this dream world and then when he falls asleep in the dream world, he wakes up back in the real world?’” he recalled from a conversation with his daughter when she was 10 and in fourth grade.</p><p>And so they began telling the tale of “Everworld,” where Daniel, the protagonist, encounters dogs with four heads, cats with nine tails, a candy store fluttering with flying chocolate bars and an ominous green cloud that threatens to destroy the dream world.</p><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="Enhancement-item" data-crop=""> <figure class="Figure"><a class="AnchorLink" id="image-a90000" name="image-a90000"></a> <picture data-crop="medium"> <source type="image/webp" width="490" height="275" data-srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/a9a6eaf/2147483647/strip/true/crop/6122x3436+0+324/resize/490x275!/format/webp/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F28%2F7f%2Fe5a10e5841b08874c25aae67feaa%2Fkidbook-260410-01.jpg 1x,https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/d0f31a7/2147483647/strip/true/crop/6122x3436+0+324/resize/980x550!/format/webp/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F28%2F7f%2Fe5a10e5841b08874c25aae67feaa%2Fkidbook-260410-01.jpg 2x" data-lazy-load="true" srcset="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" /> <source width="490" height="275" data-srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/6352e3a/2147483647/strip/true/crop/6122x3436+0+324/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F28%2F7f%2Fe5a10e5841b08874c25aae67feaa%2Fkidbook-260410-01.jpg" data-lazy-load="true" srcset="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" /> <img class="Image" alt="“Daniel the Different” authors and father and daughter Ty Thompson (right) and Vaughn Thompson at their home in Lakeview, Friday, March 27, 2026. " srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/6352e3a/2147483647/strip/true/crop/6122x3436+0+324/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F28%2F7f%2Fe5a10e5841b08874c25aae67feaa%2Fkidbook-260410-01.jpg 1x,https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/0a58162/2147483647/strip/true/crop/6122x3436+0+324/resize/980x550!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F28%2F7f%2Fe5a10e5841b08874c25aae67feaa%2Fkidbook-260410-01.jpg 2x" width="490" height="275" data-src="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/6352e3a/2147483647/strip/true/crop/6122x3436+0+324/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F28%2F7f%2Fe5a10e5841b08874c25aae67feaa%2Fkidbook-260410-01.jpg" data-lazy-load="true" src="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" > </picture> <div class="Figure-content"><figcaption class="Figure-caption"><p>After Vaughn blurted out a story idea at bedtime when she was 10, she and her father, Ty, began telling the tale of “Everworld,” where Daniel, the protagonist, encounters dogs with four heads, cats with nine tails, a candy store fluttering with flying chocolate bars and an ominous green cloud that threatens to destroy the dream world.</p></figcaption><span class="line"></span><div class="Figure-credit"><p>Anthony Vazquez/Sun-Times</p></div></div> </figure> </div> </div><p>Anyone who has attempted to write a novel (including this reporter), knows how lonely the experience can be and how inspiration is often fleeting.</p><p>But when you’re sharing the writing — in this case, alternating chapters — it’s like running a marathon with a buddy by your side.</p><p>“Vaughn will write something that will inspire me,” Ty Thompson said. “She’ll come up with something that I didn’t think about; it allows me to build off of that.”</p><p>It took them eight months to write their first novel, they said.</p><p>There were occasional creative differences. It helps if you’re willing to put your ego aside, Vaughn’s dad said.</p><p>“Definitely. She told me my first chapter stunk,” he said.</p><p>When they’d finished the first draft, they hired a professional editor at a cost of about $1,500.</p><p>“She helped us see [the novel] from the 30,000-foot view and, then, [helped with] specifics,” Ty Thompson said.</p><p>They took what they’d learned from that experience and didn’t feel the need to hire an editor for the sequel, which is set to be released May 20.</p><p>The novel is self-published, with a cover designed by a professional artist the family hired. The first book has sold about 300 copies, the father-and-daughter duo said.</p><p>As debut novelists, they haven’t yet figured out how to navigate the sometimes-maddening world of literary agents and publishers. But they hope that if the books do well enough in online sales that perhaps “Daniel the Different” might one day appear in a more traditional setting.</p><p>“I really, really, really want to go into a bookstore and see it on a bookstore shelf,” Vaughn said.</p><p>She says she hasn’t shown her finished novel to the other kids at school.</p><p>“My classmates are wonderful. But some of them are just very blunt and they say exactly what they think, and sometimes that’s not exactly what you want,” she said.</p><p>Father and daughter hope to inspire other parents to work with their children, in whatever way works.</p><p>“We hope that any parent who reads this …, they just go, ‘What’s my kid really into that I can get behind?’” Ty Thompson said.</p><p>Vaughn’s mother, Stacie Thompson, is fully behind the project.</p><p>“I’ve always felt badly for Ty,” she said. “He doesn’t have a son. We just have one girl. This is such a cool thing that he gets to have with her that is uniquely theirs and really fun and special.”</p><p>And what does she think of their work?</p><p>“I actually legitimately think it’s an excellent book,” she said.<br></p>
entries.author: Stefano Esposito

entries.title: To be the next Russell Wilson (or better), Caleb Williams must build on 4th-quarter wizardry
entries.summary: <p></p><p>Five years ago, <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/bears" target="_blank" >Bears</a> general manager Ryan Pace famously tried to trade three first-round picks to the Seahawks for quarterback Russell Wilson.</p><p>The Bears might have ended up with their own version of Wilson anyway.</p><p></p><p>That’s the comparison that Titans coach Robert Saleh made when asked about Bears quarterback <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/bears/2026/04/03/bears-caleb-williams-iceman-george-gervin-trademark-application-is-to-protect-my-business-interests" target="_blank" >Caleb Williams</a> at the NFL annual meeting last week.</p><p></p><p>“He reminds me a little of — and I’m trying to put a ceiling on him because he could be better — Russell Wilson,” he said.</p><p></p><p>The most myopic Bears fans might wrinkle their nose and wonder if Williams could be better. Wilson, though, is a future Pro Football Hall of Famer who would be, hands down, the greatest quarterback in Bears history. He reached nine Pro Bowls and two Super Bowls —winning one — in his first 10 years in the NFL. His Seahawks teams won two-thirds of their regular season games and nine of 16 playoff games before he was traded to the Broncos and started a series of underwhelming stints with teams across the league. He’s currently jobless.</p><p></p><p>There’s a natural comparison between the size of the two quarterbacks — in a world of giants, Williams is listed at 6-1 and Wilson at 5-11. But most importantly, Saleh said, is what they both can do in the fourth quarter. Wilson ranks second among active quarterbacks with 32 fourth-quarter comebacks and 40 game-winning drives.</p><p></p><p>“It didn’t matter how the game started,” Saleh said. “You knew as the moment got bigger and bigger and bigger, he was going to become more and more dangerous. It proved in his fourth-quarter heroics.</p><p></p><p>“[With Williams], the game is never over, I don’t care what the score is. I don’t care what the situation is. I think the throw he made in the playoff game proves that.”</p><p></p><p>Saleh wasn’t wowed simply by Williams’ game-tying, back-pedaling touchdown pass to tight end Cole Kmet in the playoff game they’d eventually lose to the Rams in overtime. Before the Titans named him head coach in January, he was the defensive coordinator of the 49ers. In Week 17, Williams threw for 330 yards and a 100.3 passer rating in a 42-38 loss in the Bay Area.</p><p></p><p>“Special young man,” Saleh said. “Special talent.”</p><p></p><p>Now the Bears need him to play that well throughout the season — starting in quarters 1-3.</p><p>Head coach Ben Johnson has made it one of his offseason priorities to try to make the rest of the game as impressive as the end.</p><p></p><p>The Bears scored more points in the fourth quarter last year than any other frame. Toward the end of the season, though, it got ridiculous. In the Bears’ last eight games — six regular season plus two in the playoffs — the Bears scored 95 of their 200 points in the fourth quarter. In two postseason games, they totaled 32 fourth-quarter points — and 16 the rest of the time.</p><p></p><p>“That wasn't the case in the first half of the season,” Johnson said. “So we've got to figure out why we started the second half of the season so slow on offense. And that's a big mission of mine.</p><p></p><p>"We don't want to put ourselves behind the 8 ball each and every week. That's not a formula for sustained success. We're better than that. We have too much talent on our offensive side of the ball for us to start off so slow and to dig ourselves in holes like that.”</p><p></p><p>For Williams to become Wilson — or be even better — he needs to dominate throughout the game, not just in the fourth quarter. And then he has to continue to play well at the end of games, too.</p><p></p><p>“I think that part needs to stay the same,” Johnson said. “In terms of when we need to be at our best in the fourth quarter.”</p><p></p><p>Williams had six fourth-quarter comebacks in the regular season last year. He posted one in the playoffs, too, when he rallied the Bears for 25 fourth-quarter points against the Bears' rivals.</p><p></p><p>“I just think what Caleb did at the end of the games and his ability to make off-schedule plays was incredible,” said Jeff Hafley, who was the Packers' defensive coordinator before being named the Dolphins’ head coach in January. "I mean, that’s just me being honest. At the end of the games, when he had to make off-schedule plays and somehow pull it off, he did.”</p><p></p><p>Hafley has spent his career shouting warnings about late-game comebacks into his headset, telling his defenders that games always come down to whether they can make a stop at the end.</p><p></p><p>Williams proved to be special at the end of their rivalry battles. Now the Bears want him to focusing on doing the same throughout each game.</p><p></p><p>“When you get guys that are really good like that,” Hafley said, “you have to make sure you’re on it.”</p>
entries.author: Patrick Finley

entries.title: Pat Fitzgerald says he's crawled through turmoil, and come out clean
entries.summary: <p>EAST LANSING, Mich. — This was 33 months in the making, in case you’ve lost count. There’s zero chance he can contain himself now, much less take the high road. He didn’t wait nearly three years to allow someone behind the wall, behind the safety and sanity of truth on your side, without knowing this day was coming.</p><p>Where for the first time, someone outside Pat Fitzgerald’s inner circle is hearing his side of the story — rising from months of misinformation, disinformation and finally, beautifully, sweet vindication — and he can barely hold it in.</p><p>“I feel like Andy Dufresne in 'Shawshank Redemption,’ ” Fitzgerald says, and while the exclusive interview with USA TODAY Sports has just begun, he hasn’t even settled in behind his desk at Michigan State and the words are racing out of his mouth. “I crawled all the way through that s---, and came out cleaner on the other side.”</p><p>We’ll get to the new job soon enough, the perfect fit of blue-collar, hard-working, wildly successful coach, and the blue-collar, hard-working Michigan State program that not so long ago had control of the Big Ten. But we’ve got other business to tackle first.</p><p>Specifically, the business of truth and reality from Fitzgerald’s ugly and unfair firing by his beloved alma mater, Northwestern, nearly three summers ago. How every allegation and shock headline of player mistreatment, every leaked story and false narrative, never broke Fitzgerald and his family.</p><p>But you better believe it damn near did.</p><div class="RelatedList Enhancement" data-module data-align-center> <div class="RelatedList-title">Related</div> <ul class="RelatedList-items"> <li class="RelatedList-items-item"> <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/college-sports/2025/11/06/former-northwestern-football-coach-pat-fitzgerald-says-he-feels-fully-vindicated-hazing-settlement" >Former Northwestern football coach Pat Fitzgerald says he feels ‘fully vindicated’</a> </li> <li class="RelatedList-items-item"> <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/northwestern-hazing-scandal/2025/08/21/northwestern-pat-fitzgerald-reach-lawsuit-settlement-over-firing-from-hazing-allegations" >Northwestern, Fitzgerald settle hazing lawsuit</a> </li> <li class="RelatedList-items-item"> <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/high-school-football/2026/01/28/pat-fitzgerald-michigan-state-football" >New Michigan State football coach Pat Fitzgerald fits right in recruiting local talent</a> </li> </ul> </div> <p>This isn’t about money and the multi-million dollar settlement from Northwestern, or most important, the public statement from the university completely exonerating Fitzgerald from player mistreatment allegations. This is about legacy and loyalty, about a man who spent a majority of his life bleeding and building for a college football program, a university, that had long prior lost relevancy and was swirling in the backwash of who cares.</p><p>So after an All-American career as a linebacker at Northwestern, after five years as an assistant coach and 17 as the head coach, and after building — literally piece by piece — the program into a respectable and at times dangerous team despite the inherent disadvantages, this was his reward.</p><p>He was the tip of the spear for all things Northwestern, the face of the university — not some stuffed shirt attorney on Michigan Avenue or groundbreaking researcher at Northwestern Memorial Hospital. He was the force behind getting that $270 million palace of a practice facility built on the banks of Lake Michigan, the one NFL franchises fantasize about. He was the one who convinced those same deep pocket boosters to throw $800 million more into replacing a decrepit stadium. Had the entire program poised to take a shot at what lovable loser Indiana later did.</p><p>Yet there he was, days after being told he had been fired because of allegations that would never be proved, skulking through a back stairwell of that 425,000-foot practice facility and into his former office with his wife, Stacy, and packing decades of real, tangible success into cardboard boxes. While a security guard made sure he didn’t take anything that wasn’t his.</p><p><i>Wasn’t his</i>.</p><p>The whole thing was his. The program, the highest graduation rate in college football, winning more with less, the winningest coach in school history, elite-level respect throughout college football. All of it.</p><p>And just like that, ripped away — a trail of tears left on those stairs all the way back down.</p><p>“We’re sitting in a loaded truck, packed completely full. Our life for all those years,” Fitzgerald says. “My wife is crying, can’t stop. This rock star of a woman who has been with me since high school. She's inconsolable.”</p><p>This is where it hits home. Losing a job for allegations that were never proved is one thing. Not being allowed to have closure is another.</p><p>But watching the woman you adore weep for you, your family and your future nearly brought him to his knees. The same woman who stuck around while Fitzgerald chased an NFL dream for a few months before pivoting to the nomadic world of coaching. The same woman who was later his rock when Northwestern coach and mentor Randy Walker suddenly died in the summer of 2006, and uncertainty arrived.</p><p>Fitzgerald was all of 31 — not that far removed from twice being named Big Ten defensive player of the year — when his alma mater gave him the keys to the kingdom. And said don’t screw it up.</p><p>The same woman who raised three boys while Fitzgerald worked two decades to change the way Northwestern thought about — and executed — football. There he was, lost in the moment in a packed truck of memories, and the only answer that came to mind were words from his dad, Pat Sr., that have rattled around his head for decades.</p><p>You can pout and feel sorry for yourself, or you can respond.</p><p>“The car is running and we’re just staring in front of us, not moving,” Fitzgerald continues, and what’s now 33 months in the rearview is suddenly his dark companion once again. “I looked at her and said, ‘When we pull onto Sheridan Road, I want you to stop crying because this is over, and we’re moving on.”</p><p>He stops and shakes his head, and yeah, all of the unthinkable is still a kick in the shorts. He signed a nondisclosure agreement last September with Northwestern, when he officially settled out of court and was awarded millions in damages. He loves his alma mater, and wouldn’t say anything that would harm it, anyway.</p><p>“But I never got a chance to say goodbye to the janitorial staff, you know?” Fitzgerald says. “To our great chefs and cooks. To our equipment people, to the staff.”</p><p>The thought trails off, and he exhales. The lasting, final indignation is still raw.</p><p>“I was treated like a criminal,” he says.</p><p>Which takes us all the way back to “Shawshank Redemption.”</p><p>So get busy living, or get busy dying.</p><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="Enhancement-item" data-crop=""> <figure class="Figure"><a class="AnchorLink" id="image-190000" name="image-190000"></a> <picture data-crop="medium"> <source type="image/webp" width="490" height="275" data-srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/550a15e/2147483647/strip/true/crop/3413x1915+0+0/resize/490x275!/format/webp/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F2b%2F65%2Fc4426f7f4857b11db01085966237%2Ffitz-2.JPG 1x,https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/231eb0c/2147483647/strip/true/crop/3413x1915+0+0/resize/980x550!/format/webp/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F2b%2F65%2Fc4426f7f4857b11db01085966237%2Ffitz-2.JPG 2x" data-lazy-load="true" srcset="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" /> <source width="490" height="275" data-srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/a034be2/2147483647/strip/true/crop/3413x1915+0+0/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F2b%2F65%2Fc4426f7f4857b11db01085966237%2Ffitz-2.JPG" data-lazy-load="true" srcset="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" /> <img class="Image" alt="Northwestern head coach Pat Fitzgerald looks on during the second half of a game against Minnesota on Saturday, Nov. 23, 2019, in Evanston." srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/a034be2/2147483647/strip/true/crop/3413x1915+0+0/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F2b%2F65%2Fc4426f7f4857b11db01085966237%2Ffitz-2.JPG 1x,https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/ac0d7c7/2147483647/strip/true/crop/3413x1915+0+0/resize/980x550!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F2b%2F65%2Fc4426f7f4857b11db01085966237%2Ffitz-2.JPG 2x" width="490" height="275" data-src="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/a034be2/2147483647/strip/true/crop/3413x1915+0+0/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F2b%2F65%2Fc4426f7f4857b11db01085966237%2Ffitz-2.JPG" data-lazy-load="true" src="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" > </picture> <div class="Figure-content"><figcaption class="Figure-caption"><p>Northwestern head coach Pat Fitzgerald looks on during the second half of a game against Minnesota on Saturday, Nov. 23, 2019, in Evanston.</p></figcaption><span class="line"></span><div class="Figure-credit"><p>Paul Beaty/AP </p></div></div> </figure> </div> </div><p>Before we go further, let’s get something vitally important on the table: no Northwestern player was ever charged with a violation of school code or policy because of player mistreatment allegations that centered around players hazing other players.</p><p>No Northwestern players were charged by, or had allegations sent to, the state’s attorney. Knowingly requiring harmful acts for school group initiation is illegal in Illinois, with felonies carrying potential prison terms of 1-3 years, and fines up to $25,000.</p><p>Yet Northwestern settled lawsuits with multiple players, and eventually had to capitulate in its defense of Fitzgerald’s lawsuit for wrongful termination, breach of contract and defamation — and pay him undisclosed monetary damages.</p><p>The private university with a $15 billion endowment simply paid its way out of the mess it created — then publicly apologized. Two weeks after Fitzgerald and Northwestern settled out of court, university president Michael Schill announced his resignation.</p><p>But the damage was done.</p><p>Fitzgerald looks back at two years of legal fighting, of self-exile, and calls it a sabbatical. Time away, and a chance to reconnect with a family that sacrificed for him for so many years.</p><p>Besides, he couldn’t sit around and stew about what was without confronting what is: he’s not an introvert. He’s not sitting at home and waiting for the truth to win out, which he knew it eventually would.</p><p>He had to be out and in the middle of it all. Had to be part of football again.</p><p>So he contacted his son’s football coach at Loyola Academy, and volunteered for the job no one wanted. There was Fitzgerald, winningest coach in Northwestern history, running the scout-team offense.</p><p>“One of the funnest times I’ve ever had as a coach,” he says.</p><p>He turns in his office chair in the football offices at Spartan Stadium, and points to the Breslin Center and the Munn Arena directly across Shaw Lane — and a world apart from where the football team at Michigan State currently sits.</p><p>There’s an elite men’s basketball team in the Breslin Center, and an elite men’s hockey team in Munn Arena. Both have won national titles this century.</p><p>“That’s the bar,” he says.</p><p>Get busy living, or get busy dying.</p><p><i>Read more at </i><a class="Link" href="https://www.usatoday.com" target="_blank" ><i>usatoday.com</i></a></p>
entries.author: Matt Hayes | USA TODAY SPORTS

entries.title: Missing woman, 88, found slain, wrapped in rug in Roseland, police and source say
entries.summary: <p>An 88-year-old woman who was reported missing late last month was found slain, wrapped in a rug on the Far South Side Tuesday, according to authorities and a source.</p><p>Police were called to the 10700 block of South Lafayette Avenue Tuesday about 11:15 a.m., where they found the woman unresponsive, wrapped in rug, inside a home, police and a source said. </p><p>The woman, who was pronounced dead on the scene, died of multiple injuries from an assault, according to the Cook County medical examiner's office, which ruled her death a homicide. </p><p>The 88-year-old was last seen on March 27, according to a Chicago police missing persons report. </p><p>Area 2 detectives are investigating. </p>

as the Trump administration’s tax and spending bill goes into effect.</p><p>🗞️ <b>Plus</b>: Lawmakers try to stop hospital closures, City Hall staffing shakeups and more news you need to know.</p><p>📝 <b>Keeping score</b>: <a class="Link" href="https://newsletters.suntimes.com/t/t-l-wijluyd-l-y/" target="_blank" >The Cubs beat the Rays</a>, 6-2; <a class="Link" data-lazy-load="true" src="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" > </picture> <div class="Figure-content"><figcaption class="Figure-caption"><p>K.Q., a Syrian civil war refugee, speaks with the Sun-Times.</p></figcaption><span class="line"></span><div class="Figure-credit"><p>Ashlee Rezin/Sun-Times</p></div></div> </figure> </div> </div><p></p><h3><a class="Link" href="https://newsletters.suntimes.com/t/t-l-wijluyd-l-i/" target="_blank" >Refugee families are latest group to face SNAP food benefits cutoff</a></h3><p></p><h5>By <a class="Link" href="https://newsletters.suntimes.com/t/t-l-wijluyd-l-d/" target="_blank" >Elvia Malagón</a></h5><p><b>Policy problem</b>: Thousands of refugees, asylees and survivors of human trafficking are on the verge of losing food assistance through the Supplemental Nutrition Assistance Program. President Donald Trump’s sweeping tax overhaul law, passed last year, limits which immigrants can access the SNAP food program, along with <a class="Link" href="https://newsletters.suntimes.com/t/t-l-wijluyd-l-h/" target="_blank" >enacting expanded work requirements</a> for others. The changes for immigrants went into effect in Illinois on April 1 and are part of broader restrictions on the refugee resettlement program</p><p><b>New rules</b>: Under Trump's policy, refugees can only receive SNAP benefits once they become legal permanent residents. But the federal government isn’t processing refugee applications for green cards. As many as 16,000 people could lose SNAP benefits because of the change, according to the state health department. Historically, refugees have been given a legal form of immigration with a pathway to citizenship.</p><p><b>KQ's story</b>: K.Q. and her husband and four children are refugees who fled the civil war in Syria before they were accepted into the U.S. refugee resettlement program. After nearly three years in Chicago, they expect to lose the monthly $635 they receive in SNAP to supplement her husband’s income. "We came here because they chose us to come," she said. "Why now they [don’t] … help us to live here, because, here — it’s expensive."</p><p><a class="Link" href="https://newsletters.suntimes.com/t/t-l-wijluyd-l-k/" target="_blank" ><b>READ MORE</b></a></p><hr><p> </p><h5>HOW TO HELP ✶</h5><p><b>Household items</b>: RefugeeOne, a Chicago resettlement nonprofit, is stocking up on toiletries, cleaning supplies and diapers to minimize expenses for families. The group has an<a class="Link" href="https://newsletters.suntimes.com/t/t-l-wijluyd-l-u/" target="_blank" > Amazon wish list</a>.</p><p><b>Grocery gift cards</b>: Resettlement agencies are collecting grocery gift cards to provide immediate assistance to refugee families. Those cards can be mailed to RefugeeOne at 6008 N. California Ave., Chicago, Illinois 60659; or to World Relief Chicagoland at 191 S. Gary Ave., Suite 130, Carol Stream, Illinois 60188.</p><p><b>Support local pantries</b>: Groups are expecting more refugees to depend on local food pantries. For a list of pantries, <a class="Link" href="https://newsletters.suntimes.com/t/t-l-wijluyd-l-o/" target="_blank" >head here</a>.</p><hr><p></p><h4>HEALTH 🚑</h4><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="Enhancement-item" data-crop=""> <figure class="Figure"><a class="AnchorLink" id="image-fa0000" name="image-fa0000"></a> <picture data-crop="medium"> <source type="image/webp" width="490" height="275" data-srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/15702a1/2147483647/strip/true/crop/6122x3436+0+324/resize/490x275!/format/webp/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F52%2F8d%2F7aaa47a944b2a18f0d7f6c7ab01d%2Fwestsubfolo-260402-17.jpg 1x,https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/a30e787/2147483647/strip/true/crop/6122x3436+0+324/resize/980x550!/format/webp/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F52%2F8d%2F7aaa47a944b2a18f0d7f6c7ab01d%2Fwestsubfolo-260402-17.jpg 2x" data-lazy-load="true" srcset="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" /> <source width="490" height="275" data-srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/0e7118d/2147483647/strip/true/crop/6122x3436+0+324/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F52%2F8d%2F7aaa47a944b2a18f0d7f6c7ab01d%2Fwestsubfolo-260402-17.jpg" data-lazy-load="true" srcset="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" /> <img class="Image" alt="Black plastic covers the emergency room sign at West Suburban Medical Center on April 1 after the owner announced he was temporarily closing the Oak Park hospital. Laura Fine and state Rep. Robyn Gabel, are proposing legislation to help Illinois better prepare for the sudden closure of safety net hospitals. The bills would require all hospitals to file plans with the state in case they close or scale back services, to make sure patients don’t fall through the cracks. For-profit hospitals owned by investors would have to give the state a deeper look into their finances, which now are largely shielded from the public.</p><p><b>Key context</b>: When West Suburban Medical Center abruptly closed its doors temporarily last month, saying it had run out of cash to pay staff, there was little warning for thousands of patients and hundreds of employees. Illinois regulators tasked with overseeing hospitals had little power to stop it.</p><p><b>The stakes</b>: Regulators might not be able to keep open some of the dozens of other safety net hospitals around the state that mostly treat low-income and immigrant communities, amid federal changes next year that could drastically reduce how much money medical centers get paid.</p><p><a class="Link" href="https://newsletters.suntimes.com/t/t-l-wijluyd-l-p/" target="_blank" ><b>READ MORE</b></a></p><hr><p> </p><h4>LOCAL NEWS DAY ✍️</h4><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center><div class="Enhancement-item"> <div class="ExternalContent-wrapper" data-embed> <iframe width="200" height="113" src="https://www.youtube.com/embed/K5TeJPLAOd4?feature=oembed" frameborder="0" allow="accelerometer; autoplay; clipboard-write; encrypted-media; gyroscope; picture-in-picture; web-share" referrerpolicy="strict-origin-when-cross-origin" allowfullscreen title="Local News Day with Chicago Journalists"></iframe> </div> </div></div><p>Today is national <a class="Link" href="https://newsletters.suntimes.com/t/t-l-wijluyd-l-m/" target="_blank" >Local News Day</a>, which brings communities together around something we all rely on: Trusted, independent local news. We’re joining newsrooms across the country to remind readers and listeners why local news matters. </p><p>In this video, a few of our journalists talk about why they love Chicago. State Street ‘that great street’ again</a></h3><p></p><h5>By <a class="Link" href="https://newsletters.suntimes.com/t/t-l-wijluyd-l-w/" target="_blank" >Abby Miller</a></h5><p><b>State of State</b>: City and business leaders gathered Tuesday at Macy’s for a sold-out summit on State Street. Panelists discussed multiple strategies for reinstating the street as a vibrant retail and entertainment corridor, and they said it is key to a thriving Downtown that attracts more visitors.</p><p><b>Key context</b>: Like many retail corridors across Chicago, State Street has struggled following the COVID-19 pandemic. Since 2020, more than 15 retailers vacated the corridor, according to Stone Real Estate principal John Vance. But the area is seeing signs of recovery, Vance said, with major retailers like Barnes & Noble and Gap announcing plans to open on the street this year.</p><p><b>The ideas</b>: Among the solutions discussed Tuesday were leaning into the nearby universities, improving walkability and street safety, increasing density, and offering more unique experiences.</p><p><a class="Link" href="https://newsletters.suntimes.com/t/t-l-wijluyd-l-yd/" target="_blank" ><b>READ MORE</b></a></p><hr><p> </p><h4>LET’S HEAR FROM YOU 🗣️</h4><p>What would you pitch to city and business leaders to make State Street "that great street" again?</p><p><a class="Link" href="mailto:newsletters@suntimes.com" target="_blank" ><i>Reply to this email</i></a><i> (please include your first and last name). href="https://newsletters.suntimes.com/t/t-l-wijluyd-l-yk/" target="_blank" >What are Chicago-area lighthouses used for?</a></h3><p></p><h5>By <a class="Link" href="https://newsletters.suntimes.com/t/t-l-wijluyd-l-yu/" target="_blank" >Katie Riordan</a></h5><p><b>Lakeside history</b>: In the Chicago area, lighthouses were operated by keepers for decades until automation took over. Now, preservationists are working to restore the most iconic one, the Chicago Harbor Lighthouse.</p><p><b>Key context</b>: The Chicago Harbor Lighthouse is hidden in plain sight, resting in Lake Michigan about half a mile from the end of Navy Pier. It was originally built in 1893 against the backdrop of the World’s Columbian Exposition. In 1917, officials decided to move it to its current location.</p><p><b>State of disrepair</b>: The U.S. Coast Guard operates the actual light as an active aid to navigation, but the station is rusted, crumbling and gutted. Advocates want to bring it back to life, but challenges remain, like raising the estimated $6 million necessary for the project.</p><p><b>Listen up</b>: <a class="Link" href="https://newsletters.suntimes.com/t/t-l-wijluyd-l-jl/" target="_blank" >WBEZ’s Curious City show</a> dove into the past, present and future of Chicago-area lighthouses. 🎧</p><p><a class="Link" href="https://newsletters.suntimes.com/t/t-l-wijluyd-l-jr/" target="_blank" ><b>MORE HERE</b></a></p><hr><p></p><h4>TODAY ON WBEZ 91.5 FM 📻</h4><p><a class="Link" href="https://newsletters.suntimes.com/t/t-l-wijluyd-l-jj/" target="_blank" ><b>In the Loop with Sasha-Ann Simons, 9 a.m.</b></a></p><ul class="rte2-style-ul" style="line-height: inherit; margin-top: 0px; margin-bottom: 20px; margin-left: 24px; padding: 0px; list-style-type: disc;" id="rte-8f233493-3416-11f1-b924-9b25a66fe2db"><li><b>Life with HIV/AIDS</b>: HIV research has advanced significantly, but women continue to be disproportionately affected by the virus. An online exhibition amplifies their journeys. Guests Jennifer Brier, a University of Illinois Chicago professor and founder of the exhibition, and Marta Santiago, an exhibition participant, join the show.<br></li><li><b>Hip hop showcase</b>: Rapper Doug E. Fresh stops by to discuss 1980s and 1990s hip hop and the retrospective he'll perform April 18 at Auditorium Theatre.<br></li></ul><p><a class="Link" href="https://newsletters.suntimes.com/t/t-l-wijluyd-l-jt/" target="_blank" ><b>Say More with Mary Dixon and Patrick Smith, 10 a.m.</b></a></p><ul class="rte2-style-ul" style="line-height: inherit; margin-top: 0px; margin-bottom: 20px; margin-left: 24px; padding: 0px; list-style-type: disc;" id="rte-8f233496-3416-11f1-b924-9b25a66fe2db"><li><b>Local News Day</b>: Jennifer Smith Richards of ProPublica explains the union fight happening in her nonprofit newsroom.<br></li><li><b>College grads' gripes</b>: Noam Scheiber of the New York Times talks about his new book, in which he describes a revolt by the "college-educated working class." We'll learn more about what's driving dissatisfaction among people with higher education degrees, as callers weigh in.</li></ul><hr><p></p><h4>FROM THE PRESS BOX 🏈⚾🏒</h4><p></p><ul He dove deep into the world of mystery, horror and thriller novels with the encouragement of his wife, Amanda Morse, an avid romance reader.</p><p>The couple’s combined book tastes have culminated in the opening of the new North Center bookstore, Partners in Crime. Fittingly, the romance-mystery bookstore at 4105 N. Lincoln Ave. opened to the public on Valentine’s Day, with a line out the door.</p><p>Amanda, 39, and Jeff, 35, moved to Chicago from Dallas the day much of the city shut down, in March 2020. They turned to reading during the pandemic amid remote work. But it wasn’t until Amanda was laid off from her job and faced an unfriendly market in 2024 that the couple decided it might be time to pivot to a longtime dream.</p><p>The two are the only employees working at the genre mashup bookstore, and Jeff still works full-time as a copywriter.</p><p>"We are the partners in crime," Jeff laughed.</p><p><a class="Link" href="https://newsletters.suntimes.com/t/t-l-wijluyd-l-tj/" target="_blank" ><b>READ MORE</b></a></p><hr><p> </p><h4>PICTURE CHICAGO 📸</h4><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div entries.author: Matt Moore

entries.title: Lyric Opera will bring back its summer musical with ‘Guys and Dolls’ after a four-season hiatus
entries.summary: <p>Lyric Opera of Chicago will bring back its summer musical with a 2027 production of “Guys and Dolls” after a four-season hiatus, the company announced Thursday.</p><p>Lyric’s upcoming 2026-27 season is slated to include 74 performances with six fully staged operas and another in concert, which is still less than the eight that was standard prior to the COVID-19 pandemic. The lineup includes everything from Mozart’s “Don Giovanni” to the return of Carlisle Floyd’s American opera, “Susannah.” Plus, the Chicago premiere of Rhiannon Giddens and Michael Abels’ Pulitzer Prize-winning, “Omar.”</p><p>The company also announced that Chicago's “hometown diva,” soprano <a class="Link" href="https://www.wbez.org/theater-stages/2025/10/06/sondra-radvanovsky-medea-lyric-opera-chicago" target="_blank" ><u>Sondra Radvanovsky</u></a>, will begin in a new role as artistic adviser for the next five seasons, which will include one mainstage appearance per year and close collaboration with the Ryan Opera Center, the company’s training program for emerging singers.</p><div class="Figure-content"><figcaption class="Figure-caption"><p>Chicago’s “hometown diva,” soprano Sondra Radvanovsky, will begin in a new Lyric Opera artistic adviser for the next five seasons</p></figcaption><span class="line"></span><div class="Figure-credit"><p>Manuel Martinez/WBEZ</p></div></div> </figure> </div> </div><p>Together, Lyric’s General Director <a class="Link" href="https://www.wbez.org/theater-stages/2024/10/28/john-mangum-lyric-opera-ceo-chicago-houston-symphony" target="_blank" ><u>John Mangum</u></a> called the lineup “one of the most robust and diverse seasons in recent memory.”</p><p>Mangum took over Lyric’s <a class="Link" href="https://www.wbez.org/music/2024/07/31/john-mangum-named-general-director-ceo-president-of-lyric-opera-of-chicago" target="_blank" ><u>top job in 2024</u></a> after the company’s longtime leader, <a class="Link" href="https://www.wbez.org/theater-stages/2024/06/24/lyric-opera-chicago-future-anthony-freud-departure" target="_blank" ><u>Anthony Freud</u></a>, retired. However, Mangum’s vision for Lyric won’t be fully reflected for another couple of seasons, due to the industry’s long planning process, which sets production schedules far in advance. Mangum, who came to Lyric from the Houston Symphony, has said his goal is to make the art form relevant and accessible to Chicagoans.</p><p>In the new season, he said the company is “expanding what opera means.”</p><p>"I think [this is] a lineup that shows our dedication to honoring the tradition, but also pushing the tradition forward and being a space where exciting new work can happen," Mangum said.</p><p>The company will open the season with “Don Giovanni” on Oct. 10, directed by Chicago theater legend <a class="Link" href="https://www.wbez.org/theater-stages/2025/11/12/amadeus-steppenwolf-goodman-theatre-robert-falls-director" target="_blank" ><u>Robert Falls</u></a>, who last directed the work at Lyric in 2019. The production completes Lyric’s presentation of the Mozart-Da Ponte trilogy, which began with “The Marriage of Figaro” and “Così fan tutte” over the past two seasons.</p><p>Lyric’s Music Director Enrique Mazzola said the work “has the entire world of opera inside it: comedy, drama, philosophy, and singular musical architecture.” The Italian maestro will conduct five works next season, including Verdi’s “La traviata” next spring and a concert presentation of Jules Massenet’s rarely performed “Hérodiade” in February. Mazzola has worked closely with the latter score, having recorded it at the Deutsche Oper Berlin.</p><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="Enhancement-item" data-crop=""> <figure class="Figure"><a class="AnchorLink" id="image-ce0000" name="image-ce0000"></a> <picture data-crop="medium"> <source type="image/webp" width="490" height="275"
