policía.</p></figcaption><span class="line"></span><div class="Figure-credit"><p>Esther Yoon-Ji Kang/WBEZ</p></div></div> </figure> </div> </div><p>Gilbert también mencionó que la nueva “ley de ocupantes ilegales” agrava las luchas por vivienda de los inquilinos de bajos ingresos porque no toma en cuenta situaciones en las que los inquilinos son defraudados por agentes de arrendamiento falsos, como el que encontró la familia de Daniela.</p><p>Los propietarios tienen la responsabilidad de hacer más seguras sus propiedades para disuadir a los estafadores, añadió.</p><p>Desde que la <a class="Link" href="https://www.ilga.gov/Legislation/PublicActs/View/104-0029" target="_blank" >ley</a> entró en vigor en enero, Gilbert comentó que su organización ha escuchado de casos en los que la policía se retira una vez que los inquilinos muestran recibos de renta o contratos de arrendamiento. En esos casos, los propietarios continúan con los procedimientos de desalojo estándar, donde una corte escucha las circunstancias de ambas partes y decide si el inquilino tiene derecho a quedarse. Un proceso legal también podría proporcionar a los inquilinos acceso a programas de asistencia de renta de emergencia.</p><p>Gilbert dijo que los nuevos administradores de propiedades podrían abusar de la ley de ocupantes ilegales llamando a la policía sobre inquilinos que no reconocen sin averiguar más.</p><p>“No se puede simplemente comprar un edificio y desocuparlo llamando a la policía”, dijo.</p><p>Los defensores de la vivienda también indicaron que forzar a los inquilinos a salir tan rápido los deja en una situación peor que la que tenían antes. En el caso de la familia de Daniela, tuvieron que dejar atrás la mayoría de sus pertenencias, todos sus muebles e incluso un carrito que usaban para vender empanadas, una fuente de ingresos tan necesaria.</p><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="Enhancement-item" data-crop=""> <figure class="Figure"><a class="AnchorLink" id="image-18dc96" name="image-18dc96"></a> <picture data-crop="medium"> <source type="image/webp" width="490" height="275" data-srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/a6b0076/2147483647/strip/true/crop/3742x2100+0+353/resize/490x275!/format/webp/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2Fa9%2F4c%2F35771da24e2d9a5ced268a79f026%2Fimg-6775.jpg 1x,https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/156c0a9/2147483647/strip/true/crop/3742x2100+0+353/resize/980x550!/format/webp/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2Fa9%2F4c%2F35771da24e2d9a5ced268a79f026%2Fimg-6775.jpg 2x" data-lazy-load="true" srcset="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" /> <source width="490" height="275" data-srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/9a786e3/2147483647/strip/true/crop/3742x2100+0+353/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2Fa9%2F4c%2F35771da24e2d9a5ced268a79f026%2Fimg-6775.jpg" data-lazy-load="true" srcset="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" /> <img class="Image" alt="Un edificio ubicado en 7900 S. Paxton." srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/9a786e3/2147483647/strip/true/crop/3742x2100+0+353/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2Fa9%2F4c%2F35771da24e2d9a5ced268a79f026%2Fimg-6775.jpg 1x,https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/e0a5ce3/2147483647/strip/true/crop/3742x2100+0+353/resize/980x550!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2Fa9%2F4c%2F35771da24e2d9a5ced268a79f026%2Fimg-6775.jpg 2x" width="490" height="275" data-src="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/9a786e3/2147483647/strip/true/crop/3742x2100+0+353/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2Fa9%2F4c%2F35771da24e2d9a5ced268a79f026%2Fimg-6775.jpg" data-lazy-load="true" src="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" > </picture> <div class="Figure-content"><figcaption class="Figure-caption"><p>Los apartamentos del edificio en 7900 S. Paxton fueron recientemente renovados.</p></figcaption><span class="line"></span><div class="Figure-credit"><p>Esther Yoon-Ji Kang/WBEZ</p></div></div> </figure> </div> </div><p>Un abogado de Atlas Asset Management, el brazo de gestión de propiedades de la empresa que posee el edificio donde vivía la familia de Daniela, dijo en un comunicado a WBEZ que al hacerse cargo del edificio, “nuestro equipo descubrió condiciones complejas y desafiantes en el sitio” y desocupó las unidades para “asegurar la seguridad de los residentes. Esto incluyó trabajar para identificar a todos los inquilinos autorizados con contratos de arrendamiento válidos”.</p><p>Atlas no respondió a múltiples preguntas de WBEZ, incluyendo cómo aseguró la gestión del edificio tras la compra, cómo determinó que había ocupantes ilegales y si la empresa trabajó con alguna organización sin fines de lucro de la zona para ayudar a las familias con reubicaciones.</p><p>Una revisión de los registros obtenidos mediante una solicitud de registros públicos de WBEZ al Departamento de Policía de Chicago (CPD) muestra que los oficiales fueron llamados al edificio el 2 de febrero por un administrador de la propiedad, “con respecto a ocupantes ilegales en 3 o 4 de las unidades vacías del edificio”.</p><p>Los registros públicos también muestran que el edificio, que había estado en mal estado durante mucho tiempo, fue anteriormente propiedad del 5812 Group, <a class="Link" href="https://www.wbez.org/housing/2025/09/05/chicago-landlords-renters-property-management-llc?subscription=true" target="_blank" >compañía de responsabilidad limitada</a>. Fue vendido a Osiris Chicago Portfolio LLC, que comparte dirección con Atlas, el 20 de noviembre de 2025, aproximadamente tres meses antes de que Daniela y su familia fueran desalojados.</p><p>Adam Walls, el jefe del anterior propietario 5812 Group, no respondió a una solicitud de entrevista.</p><p>A pesar de las condiciones del edificio, que reflejaban las de muchas propiedades en esa área, los migrantes desesperados se han asentado en South Shore, que ha llegado a ser conocida como la “capital de los desalojos de Chicago”.</p><p>El vecindario también es hogar del lugar de una redada federal de inmigración el año pasado. También hubo <a class="Link" href="https://www.propublica.org/article/chicago-venezuela-immigration-ice-raid-landlord-tren-de-aragua" target="_blank" >informes</a> de gerentes de propiedades que cobraron alquiler a migrantes desprevenidos en el edificio donde se llevó a cabo la redada.</p><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="Enhancement-item" data-crop=""> <figure class="Figure"><a class="AnchorLink" id="image-c21fa4" name="image-c21fa4"></a> <picture data-crop="medium"> <source type="image/webp" width="490" height="275" data-srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/11e0b48/2147483647/strip/true/crop/3704x2079+0+350/resize/490x275!/format/webp/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F73%2F1f%2Fa35b357a410197c02a6a1ab1e840%2Fimg-6790.jpg 1x,https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/b952bdf/2147483647/strip/true/crop/3704x2079+0+350/resize/980x550!/format/webp/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F73%2F1f%2Fa35b357a410197c02a6a1ab1e840%2Fimg-6790.jpg 2x" data-lazy-load="true" srcset="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" /> <source width="490" height="275" data-srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/86a80f6/2147483647/strip/true/crop/3704x2079+0+350/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F73%2F1f%2Fa35b357a410197c02a6a1ab1e840%2Fimg-6790.jpg" data-lazy-load="true" srcset="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" /> <img class="Image" alt="Chris Amatore, propietario de Manage Chicago, dijo que ha visto a muchos migrantes enfrentar desafíos de vivienda en los últimos años. También se solidariza con los administradores de propiedades y los propietarios, quienes enfrentan costos crecientes e impuestos más altos." srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/86a80f6/2147483647/strip/true/crop/3704x2079+0+350/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F73%2F1f%2Fa35b357a410197c02a6a1ab1e840%2Fimg-6790.jpg 1x,https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/1abaf1c/2147483647/strip/true/crop/3704x2079+0+350/resize/980x550!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F73%2F1f%2Fa35b357a410197c02a6a1ab1e840%2Fimg-6790.jpg 2x" width="490" height="275" data-src="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/86a80f6/2147483647/strip/true/crop/3704x2079+0+350/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F73%2F1f%2Fa35b357a410197c02a6a1ab1e840%2Fimg-6790.jpg" data-lazy-load="true" src="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" > </picture> <div class="Figure-content"><figcaption class="Figure-caption"><p>Chris Amatore, propietario de Manage Chicago, dijo que ha visto a muchos migrantes enfrentar desafíos de vivienda en los últimos años. También se solidariza con los administradores de propiedades y los propietarios, quienes enfrentan costos crecientes e impuestos más altos.</p></figcaption><span class="line"></span><div class="Figure-credit"><p>Esther Yoon-Ji Kang/WBEZ</p></div></div> </figure> </div> </div><p></p><h3>‘La compasión no es un sistema de alquiler’</h3><p>Como presidente de Manage Chicago, Chris Amatore supervisa miles de apartamentos para inquilinos de bajos ingresos. A finales de 2023, proporcionó apartamentos a cientos de migrantes en sus edificios tras experimentar un despertar espiritual.</p><p>Desde entonces, Amatore ha visto a muchos migrantes enfrentar desafíos de vivienda debido a la falta de oportunidades laborales, barreras lingüísticas y a “no saber a dónde acudir”, lo que los convierte en presas fáciles para estafas de apartamentos.</p><p>“Es realmente sólo matemáticas”, dijo Amatore. “No pueden pagar las rentas del mercado. Son de $2,000 a $2,700 por una unidad de tres habitaciones. Las rentas post-COVID han aumentado más rápido que el sueldo de un migrante”.</p><p>Destacó que los requisitos de puntaje de crédito y prueba de ingresos son también obstáculos para los migrantes que buscan alquilar apartamentos. El aumento de costos, los impuestos más altos y la inflación también han llevado a los propietarios a aumentar las rentas.</p><p>“Definitivamente simpatizo con los propietarios porque me ha pasado a mí y es muy frustrante”, dijo Amatore. “A nadie más le va a importar. A tu banco no le importa. Las empresas de servicios aún van a cortar el agua o el gas. Desafortunadamente, la compasión no es un sistema de alquiler”.</p><p>Aún así, Amatore dijo que hay una manera humana de trabajar con estas familias.</p><p>“Lo que haría es decir, ‘Está bien, yo soy el verdadero propietario, hagamos un trato’. [Los] pondría en un plan [de pago] y les daría una oportunidad”, señaló.</p><p>Recientemente, Amatore ha comenzado a notar que hay más migrantes durmiendo en las calles y en tiendas de campaña.</p><p>“Al principio, no veía muchos, pero ahora veo muchos más migrantes bajo el puente de Canalport y por 18th Street y Halsted, y en otras áreas”, dijo.</p><p>A medida que las rentas aumentan, la ayuda se agota y las opciones de vivienda disminuyen, dijo que cree que verá más migrantes en las calles y en carpas.</p><p>Daniela y su familia esperan que eso no les suceda.</p><p>Poco después de escapar del departamento de South Shore, encontraron uno nuevo, más caro, en el lado oeste por $1,400 al mes. Pero la mudanza, una vez más, desarraigó a la familia. Les tomó semanas inscribir a los niños en su nueva escuela. Y aunque ahora más adultos de la familia están empleados, aún luchan por reunir la renta cada mes.</p><p>Mientras tanto, de regreso en South Shore, el departamento donde vivían Daniela y su familia ha sido pintado y la cocina remodelada. A principios de abril, Atlas Asset Management había listado la unidad por $1,525.</p><p>El anuncio decía: “¡Bienvenidos a su nuevo hogar en el corazón de Chicago, Illinois! Este apartamento de dos habitaciones y un baño está disponible ahora y listo para impresionar”.</p><p><i>Traducido con una herramienta de inteligencia artificial (AI) y editado por </i><a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/la-voz" target="_blank" ><i>La Voz Chicago</i></a></p>
entries.author: Adriana Cardona-Maguigad |  WBEZ

entries.title: Looking for sports to watch for free? Here's a FAST way to do it
entries.summary: <p>I was being a couch slouch one night, channel-surfing for sports — one of my favorite activities — when I came across a sport that brought me back to my youth.</p><p>Indoor soccer.</p><p>As a kid, my introduction to soccer was the indoor game, watching Sting games in the 80s. I remember going to a game at the Chicago Stadium against the Dallas Sidekicks with Tatu, the exuberant Brazilian who would celebrate scoring by taking off his jersey, jumping on the sideboards and waving his shirt over his head like a lasso. It was wild to see in person.</p><p>So when I happened to catch a San Diego Sockers-Milwaukee Wave game in the Major Arena Soccer League — a successor to the Sting's Major Indoor Soccer League, the MISL — I was in!</p><div class="RelatedList Enhancement" data-module data-align-center> <div class="RelatedList-title">Related</div> <ul class="RelatedList-items"> <li class="RelatedList-items-item"> <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/nfl/2026/04/09/nfl-under-federal-investigation-for-potential-anticompetitive-practices-justice-department" >NFL under federal investigation for anticompetitive practices as TV costs rise</a> </li> </ul> </div> <p>But how was I able to experience such nostalgia? What portal had I entered that conjured flashes of Karl Heinz-Granitza, Pato Margetic and Victor Nogueira?</p><p>It’s called Unbeaten Sports Channel, a FAST channel that carries the MASL and Ultimate Frisbee Association (home of the Chicago Union), as well as little-known sports such as super netball and armored MMA — yes, armored. It also shows English Premier League club Tottenham Hotspur games and carried the FIBA 3x3 Champions Cup last month.</p><p>FAST stands for free ad-supported streaming television. There are hundreds of these niche channels available on your internet-connected TV, the keyword being FREE. Granted, FAST channels don’t carry the biggest games, but in a world where watching sports only gets more expensive, I refer you back to the word FREE.</p><p>I wear out the C button on my Xfinity remote calling up the live-sports menu, where I’ve seen these FAST channels, some of which might appeal to you. It’s not easy being a couch slouch (shout-out to the OG, Norman Chad). You have to get the pillows just right, and the coffee table can’t be too far for your feet to reach. But I’m here to serve.</p><h3>Scripps Sports Network</h3><p>The E.W. Scripps Company has become a player in sports rights, and it launched a FAST channel last month that carries live women’s sports. The National Women’s Soccer League, Major League Volleyball and the Professional Women’s Hockey League all air on SSN.</p><p>Scripps’ Ion channel carries WNBA games, but those will appear only in reruns on SSN. Still, Scripps is taking advantage of a growing market for women’s sports while giving less-publicized leagues, such as the Big Sky Conference, a platform.</p><h3>Women’s Sports Network & All Women’s Sports Network</h3><p>Want more women’s sports? Try WSN and AWSN. WSN, which launched first in 2022, has content agreements with better-known U.S. entities, such as Athletes Unlimited, the LPGA and NWSL. AWSN, which co-founder Whoopi Goldberg helped launch in ’24, has deals with FIBA, the Women’s Football Alliance and Women’s Elite Rugby (home of the Chicago Tempest).</p><h3>Ryz Sports Network</h3><p>Though it’s playing a part in the continued butchering of the English language — the name is pronounced “rise” — the network has a nice menu of sports that includes the American Association of Professional Baseball (home of the Chicago Dogs), Nippon Professional Baseball and several collegiate conferences, led by the Mountain West.</p><h3>PickleballTV</h3><p>Did you get swept up in the pickleball craze that spanned the nation a few years ago? Then maybe you missed the launch of this FAST channel, which offers pickleball content and matches 24/7. You don’t have to visit the local courts to hear the constant clicking of ball meeting paddle. You can get a headache right on your couch.</p><h3>Stadium</h3><p>Yes, Stadium is still around. The network, which essentially morphed into Chicago Sports Network, carries Banana Ball baseball games and combat sports (Bare Knuckle Fighting Championship sounds particularly gruesome). What traditionalist baseball fan wouldn’t like a game between the Loco Beach Coconuts and the Party Animals?</p><h3>Remote patrol</h3><p>Chicago Sports Network will air the inaugural Blackhawks Hall of Fame induction ceremony at 2 p.m. Saturday. The 12-member class will be inducted Friday at the Blackhawks Ice Center and celebrated during the game Sunday against the Blues, which will air on CHSN.</p><p>• Former Fire captain and current MLS analyst <b>Dax McCarty</b> will call his first Fire game Saturday, when his old team hosts Nashville on Apple TV. McCarty spent last season, his first in broadcasting, in the studio. He’s adding game analysis this year.</p><p>• The Bulls won’t be playing in the NBA Play-In Tournament this year, but some of you might not have watched anyway. The tournament, which begins Tuesday, will air exclusively on Prime Video.<br></p>
entries.author: Jeff Agrest

entries.title: How Frank Nazar's confidence swings influenced his second Blackhawks season
entries.summary: <p><a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/blackhawks" target="_blank" >Blackhawks</a> forward Frank Nazar seems to rely on confidence even more than most NHL players.</p><p>When he feels confident, it's evident. He plays assertively, aggressively and successfully, and that success builds more confidence, creating a positive snowball effect. When he doesn't feel confident, it's equally evident and creates a negative snowball effect.</p><p>Nazar understands those phenomena very well.</p><p>"When you're not getting the bounces and it's not going your way, sometimes you're thinking, 'Don't shoot,' or, 'Is it ever going to go in?'" Nazar said Wednesday. "But just sticking to my game and continuing to shoot the puck and find open plays, win battles and outwork opponents has helped.</p><p>"When you get a goal or something like that, it gives you a big confidence boost, almost like rush of a dopamine. It makes you feel a lot better and more confident in your game."</p><p>Looking back on Nazar's second NHL season, the numbers reveal precisely when he felt confident and when he didn't. He kicked it off feeling <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/blackhawks/2025/10/16/blackhawks-frank-nazar-evolution-star-forward-connor-bedard-rebuild" target="_blank" >fantastic</a> about himself, racking up 11 points in his first 10 games. </p><p>Then a pair of injuries — a knee/leg injury suffered Nov. 7 in Calgary and a broken jaw suffered Dec. 20 in Ottawa — not only cost him a combined 16 games of action but also derailed his momentum. He tallied only 13 points, including only two goals, in a 34-game <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/blackhawks/2026/02/27/blackhawks-things-to-prove-frank-nazar-ryan-greene-sam-rinzel-nhl" target="_blank" >span</a> between Oct. 30 and March 2.</p><p>Then around the trade deadline, something clicked and revved him up again. He has driven much of the Hawks' offense down the stretch. He entered Thursday with 17 points in his last 18 games, including seven multi-point games.</p><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center><div class="Enhancement-item"> <div class="ExternalContent-wrapper" > <div class="TweetUrl"> <blockquote class="twitter-tweet"><p lang="en" dir="ltr">Ladies and gentlemen… FRANK NAZAR<br><br>His 3rd goal in 4 games 🔥 <a href="https://t.co/Vu6R1ePlFj">pic.twitter.com/Vu6R1ePlFj</a></p>— Blackhawks on CHSN (@CHSN_Blackhawks) <a href="https://twitter.com/CHSN_Blackhawks/status/2032295187913826664?ref_src=twsrc%5Etfw">March 13, 2026</a></blockquote> <script async src="https://platform.twitter.com/widgets.js" charset="utf-8"></script> </div> </div> </div></div><p>Luck is a factor in those hot and cold stretches, of course. The Hawks' team shooting percentage during his five-on-five ice time has fluctuated from 15.6% to 8.2% to 12.8% in each aforementioned segment. But confidence is a major factor, too.</p><p>Adding it all up, this season represents a solid step forward for one of the Hawks' cornerstone centers, at least in terms of basic stats. He entered Thursday with 41 total points in 62 games, up from 26 in 53 as a rookie. (Interestingly, his analytics were actually better last season.)</p><p>One habit Nazar and the Hawks have discovered indicates his confidence — and makes a big difference in his effectiveness — is how low he comes down into the defensive zone on breakouts.</p><p>"If you get up too high too early, then you end up honestly standing still, and you can’t build your speed," coach Jeff Blashill said. "If he gets low and he’s available for the puck, he can build his speed.</p><p>"It’s all relative to where the puck is, where the puck’s going [and the] puck pressure. Is the puck going up to the wing? If you get above the winger, then you have no chance to get a bump back underneath. He’s just a guy who naturally does a pretty good job building speed through the middle of the ice."</p><p>Considering Nazar's skating is his most elite attribute, he can break down defenses in the neutral zone and at the offensive blue line if he has enough runway to build up speed beforehand.</p><p>"I'm not comparing myself, but guys like [Connor] McDavid get so low and then come up the ice with so much speed," Nazar said. "They're able to weave in and out and back teams off."</p><p>McDavid may not be an appropriate comparison, but Nazar's early-career production could be compared to Mammoth center Clayton Keller or Flyers winger Travis Konecny. They're both 5-foot-10 forwards who have panned out quite well.</p><p>As the Hawks prepare for Nazar's seven-year contract with a $6.6 million salary-cap hit to officially hit the books this summer, they would obviously love to see him continue to follow either of those trajectories.</p>
entries.author: Ben Pope

entries.title: How changes at the U.S. Postal Service could affect when your mail is postmarked
entries.summary: <p>Mailing your tax returns this year or planning to vote by mail in the upcoming general election?</p><p>Changes at the U.S. Postal Service could lead to a filing delay.</p><p>The Postal Service said more mail may not be postmarked the day it’s dropped off, due to adjustments in its transportation operations. Historically, a postmark has been applied the same day you mail an item and indicates the date your mail was processed.</p><p>The agency said last year that the adjustments could delay mail arrival by a day to local processing facilities.</p><p>The change is a part of the Postal Service’s 10-year strategic plan, called Delivering for America. It’s a 2021 pledge to implement significant operational and infrastructure changes to boost efficiency and reduce costs.</p><p>The plan calls for consolidating some of its facilities into the agency’s regional centers, which means some mail may now travel “hundreds of miles away” instead of a short distance, according to the Brookings Institution.</p><p>Postmark delays could have legal and administrative consequences for consumers, especially around <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/taxes" >tax season</a>. Taxpayers have until April 15 to file their returns.</p><p><a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/taxes/2026/01/08/early-birds-filing-income-taxes-jan-26-irs" ></a></p><p>Most consumers go online to file their taxes or pay bills, but some people still mail time-sensitive documents through the Postal Service.</p><p>Of the roughly 2.4 million early votes from Illinois in the 2024 General Election, 832,898 were cast by mail, according to data from the University of Florida’s Election Lab. More than 93% of individual tax returns were filed electronically in Illinois last year, IRS data shows.</p><p>The operational change “disrupts an evidentiary tool that has long been woven into election law, tax administration, court procedure, and many other regulatory frameworks,” Brookings Institution Senior Fellow Elena Patel wrote in a 2025 report .</p><p>Approximately one-third of Illinois voters are at risk of experiencing postmark delays, the Brookings report found, which is more moderate exposure compared to other high-risk states like South Dakota and Arkansas.</p><p>The agency said the postmark wasn’t intended to serve as proof of when mail is sent. A postmark also doesn’t necessarily indicate the date on which the item was collected, but it can serve as proof of possession by the agency, according to the Postal Service.</p><p>In order to meet mailing deadlines, the agency said customers can request a free manual postmark at the retail counter, or send their item via certified mail for $5.30, which includes a return receipt showing when the item was delivered. </p><p>There's also a certificate of mailing that can be purchased for $2.40, which serves as the customer's proof of delivery.</p><p>With anticipated delays for some mail, the best practice would be sending time-sensitive documents at least several days ahead of its due date.</p><p>Separately, some shipping prices will increase starting Sunday.</p><p>Prices increase by about 6.6% for priority mail service, 5.1% for priority mail express, 7.8% for USPS ground advantage and 6% for parcel select, the agency said on its website.</p>
entries.author: Lizzie Kane

entries.title: How many balloons does it take to woo a star? And the Sky's other pressing free agency questions
entries.summary: <p>Now that the <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/chicago-sky" target="_blank" ><u>Sky</u></a> <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/chicago-sky/2026/04/06/sky-trade-angel-reese-to-atlanta-dream-for-2027-2028-draft-picks" target="_blank" ><u>traded</u></a> their biggest star to the Dream for mediocre draft picks years into the future, the question is: What's next?</p><p>One part of the plan is to swing for the fences in free agency, per a source familiar with the team's thinking.</p><p>Most of the league’s best players are available, though some have announced they're staying put. The Liberty’s Big 3 of Breanna Stewart, Sabrina Ionescu and Jonquel Jones recommitted to New York. Reigning MVP A'ja Wilson said she's returning to Las Vegas.</p><p>And many top free agents have already been "cored" by their current teams, meaning they can only move via sign-and-trade.</p><p>Those players: Kelsey Plum, Kelsey Mitchell, Ionescu, Arike Ogunbowale, Napheesa Collier, Allisha Gray, Ezi Magbegor, Bridget Carleton and Marina Mabrey.</p><p>Could any of them end up on the Sky? Well, if you think anyone would trade Napheesa Collier for the Sky's best assets — Kamilla Cardoso, the No. 5 pick in the 2026 draft and a bag of magic beans from Atlanta — feel free to keep pondering that list.</p><p>If not: Consider the core players dead to us. </p><p>On to the more attainable ones.</p><p><b>Stars who are shopping around:</b> Skylar Diggins, Nneka Ogwumike, Satou Sabally.</p><p><b>Stars whose current team is salary cap-constrained, making a bigger offer elsewhere plausible: </b>Jewell Loyd, Emma Meesseman.</p><p><b>Stars who may not fit their current team's future: </b>Brittney Sykes, Gabby Williams, Brionna Jones, Natasha Cloud, Azurá Stevens, Brittney Griner, Courtney Williams, Tina Charles, DeWanna Bonner.</p><p>Let’s start with the point guard spot. Team sources say veteran Courtney Vandersloot is progressing well, but standard ACL recovery time suggests she won't be ready for opening day.</p><p>Which means general manager Jeff Pagliocca should be targeting Skylar Diggins.</p><p>It wouldn't be the first time he's tried. The Sun-Times reported in 2024 that Pagliocca made aggressive pitches to both Diggins and Ogwumike. Neither was interested.</p><p>How many balloons will it take to change that? (If you missed it: The Lynx ordered a fancy balloon bouquet to welcome Ogwumike to her free agency visit, then the balloon company leaked a photo.)</p><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center><div class="Enhancement-item"> <div class="ExternalContent-wrapper" > <div class="TweetUrl"> <blockquote class="twitter-tweet"><p lang="en" dir="ltr">Nneka Ogwumike had a meeting with the Minnesota Lynx on Wednesday—and it was leaked by a balloon company.<br><br>Amazing Balloons MN was first to report the news when the company posted and quickly deleted a video setting up a balloon arch around a sign with her name.<br><br>More ⬇️</p>— Front Office Sports (@FOS) <a href="https://twitter.com/FOS/status/2041979916569231598?ref_src=twsrc%5Etfw">April 8, 2026</a></blockquote> <script async src="https://platform.twitter.com/widgets.js" charset="utf-8"></script> </div> </div> </div></div><p>The Sky's pitch can focus on things that have changed since 2024. Like renderings of their <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/chicago-sky/2026/02/06/skys-new-practice-facility-will-be-ready-by-late-spring-ceo-adam-fox-says" >new training facility in Bedford Park,</a> or a Top 10 Courtney Vandersloot Dimes highlight reel.</p><p>Or maybe they should skip the slideshow and just drop a sack of cash on the table.</p><p>Thanks to the league's new TV deal, WNBA salaries have risen dramatically, and some rebuilding teams are hoping it will help them add talent.</p><p>The maximum salary is now $1.19 million, up from $214,466 in 2024. That means stars like Diggins and Ogwumike, who have taken pay cuts in the past to play for preferred destinations, may be less willing to do so.</p><p>The Sky have plenty of cap space to offer max contracts.</p><p>The problem is that other teams — especially the Valkyries, Fire and Tempo — can pay them the max, too. Money alone won't sell the biggest stars on a team coming off two losing seasons, with an unproven coach and a practice facility whose completion timeline keeps shifting.</p><p>However, money might attract players unlikely to receive a max offer elsewhere. Think Brittney Griner, DeWanna Bonner, and Natasha Cloud. If their next-best offer is $800,000, they might look past the Sky's shortcomings.</p><p>That, Sky fans, is far more likely than Diggins taking her talents to Wintrust. That, or the front office trading away a truckload of assets for Marina Mabrey — again.</p><p>But let's think happier thoughts for a moment.</p><p>Imagine the Sky somehow land one of the elite: Diggins, Ogwumike, Sabally, or Sykes. That immediately changes the narrative and softens the blow of losing Angel Reese.</p><p>Even bringing back a semi-star <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/chicago-sky/2026/04/07/the-bright-side-of-the-angel-reese-trade-still-looks-dim" target="_blank" ><u>like Stevens or Courtney Williams</u></a> — both of whom left Chicago in recent years — would signal confidence.</p><p>But if they strike out again? If the league's best choose expansion teams over them?</p><p>There's a bandwagon in Atlanta you can get on.</p><p></p><p></p><p></p><bsp-list-loadmore class="PageListH" data-module data-hide-authors="true" data-hide-categories="true" data-hide-dates="true" data-show-loadmore="true" ><a class="AnchorLink" id="list-010001" name="list-010001"></a><div class="PageList-header" > <div entries.author: Alissa Hirsh

entries.title: Cámara de Illinois avanza proyecto de ley que restringe los centros de detención en las comunidades
entries.summary: <p>La Cámara de Representantes de Illinois aprobó un proyecto de ley el miércoles que restringe la construcción de futuros centros de detención de inmigrantes cerca de lugares comunitarios.</p><p>El proyecto de ley prohibiría la ubicación de centros de detención de inmigrantes a menos de 1,500 pies de escuelas, iglesias, guarderías, cementerios, parques públicos, reservas forestales, residencias privadas y vivienda pública.</p><p>Las instalaciones de detención existentes que violan el proyecto de ley, como el centro de procesamiento del Servicio de Inmigración y Control de Aduanas (ICE) en Broadview, no se verían afectadas por la legislación.</p><div class="RichTextSidebarModule Enhancement" data-module data-align-center><a class="AnchorLink" id="module-520000" name="module-520000"></a> <div class="RichTextModule-items RichTextBody"><h3>La Voz Chicago WhatsApp</h3>Encuentra más noticias en nuestro canal de WhatsApp. <a class="Link" href="https://www.whatsapp.com/channel/0029Vb0XT1XCHDylm3rCs40P" target="_blank" ><b>Síguenos.</b></a></div> </div><p>Después de ser aprobado en la Cámara de Representantes con divisiones partidistas, con el apoyo de los demócratas y la oposición de los republicanos, el proyecto de ley ahora pasa al Senado de Illinois.</p><p>El presidente de la Cámara, Emanuel “Chris” Welch, quien patrocinó el proyecto de ley y representa áreas en y alrededor de Broadview, dijo que la legislación es producto que proviene directamente de lo que los residentes de esa comunidad soportaron durante el Operativo Midway Blitz.</p><p>“Lo que debería ser un lugar de paz y rutina para esa comunidad se ha convertido muy a menudo en un lugar de miedo, interrupción, trauma e inestabilidad”, afirmó Welch durante el debate del proyecto de ley. “Este proyecto de ley no se trata de política, se trata de personas”.</p><p>“Se trata del niño pequeño en el cuidado infantil que no debería crecer en medio del caos. Se trata de la familia en su hogar que no debería sentir que vive al lado de una zona de crisis. Se trata del miembro de la iglesia que entra a adorar y que debería ser acogido con paz, no con tensión. Se trata de decir que en Illinois, no colocaremos instalaciones de detención en el corazón de nuestros vecindarios y pretender que no hay consecuencias”.</p><p>El líder de la mayoría republicana en la Cámara, Patrick Windhorst, legislador de Metropolis, expresó su preocupación por la legalidad de la legislación y la comparó con una ley de California, anulada en una corte federal, que habría eliminado gradualmente las cárceles y centros de detención privados con fines de lucro.</p><p>Welch argumentó que su proyecto de ley no correría la misma suerte, diciendo que la ley de California era una prohibición, mientras que esto es solo una restricción.</p><p>“Estamos continuamente peleando con el gobierno federal”, comentó Windhorst. “El resultado de este esfuerzo por no trabajar juntos con el gobierno federal para resolver los problemas, particularmente los relacionados con la inmigración y la aplicación de nuestras leyes, ha resultado en enormes problemas en nuestro estado”.</p><p>Welch respondió, “No estamos iniciando una pelea con el gobierno federal. El gobierno federal está iniciando una pelea con nosotros. Tenemos los derechos de los estados; conocemos nuestros derechos, conocemos nuestro poder”.</p><p><i>Traducido con una herramienta de inteligencia artificial (AI) y editado por </i><a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/la-voz" target="_blank" ><i>La Voz Chicago</i></a></p>
entries.author: Matt Trunfio

entries.title: CTA expanding bus service to Obama Presidential Center
entries.summary: <p>The Chicago Transit Authority is expanding its No. 10 bus route to service the Obama Presidential Center that's expected to open in June.</p><p>The seasonal bus route that currently runs express between Water Tower Place and the Griffin Museum of Science and Industry will, beginning May 23, reach nearly a mile farther south to the Presidential Center, according to the CTA.</p><p>The revised route — to be renamed the No. 10 Obama Presidential Center/Museum of Science & Industry Express — will run year-round instead of being limited between Memorial Day and Labor Day.</p><div class="Enhancement" data-align-hangRight> <div class="Enhancement-item" data-crop=""> <figure class="Figure"><a class="AnchorLink" id="image-880000" name="image-880000"></a> <picture class="Figure-credit"><p>Screengrab from CTA board meeting livestream</p></div></div> </figure> </div> </div><p>The CTA expects ridership on the new route to triple to around 450 daily riders, Molly Poppe, CTA's chief innovation officer, told the CTA board on Wednesday.</p><p>Service will run about every half hour from roughly 9 a.m. to 6 p.m., she said.</p><p>Other bus and train service currently services the area near the Presidential Center includes: the No. 2 Hyde Park Express, No. 6 Jackson Park Express, No. 15 Jeffery Local, No. 28 Stony Island, No. 59 59th/61st and No. 63 63rd. </p><p>The Metra Electric District Line also stops nearby at the 59th St.-Univ. Of Chicago and 63rd Street Metra stops.</p><p>Tickets for the Obama Presidential Center go on sale to the general public May 6. The campus is scheduled to open June 19.</p><div class="RelatedList Enhancement" data-module data-align-center> <div class="RelatedList-title">Related</div> <ul class="RelatedList-items"> <li class="RelatedList-items-item"> <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/architecture-design/2026/04/04/obama-presidential-center-replica-oval-office-michael-smith" target="_blank" >Obama Presidential Center’s replica Oval Office is nearly complete</a> </li> <li class="RelatedList-items-item"> <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/museums-zoos/2026/04/06/dates-for-ticket-sales-announced-for-the-obama-presidential-center" target="_blank" >Obama Presidential Center museum ticket sales go live May 6</a> </li> </ul> </div> <p></p>
entries.author: David Struett

entries.title: Janet Murphy, who helped lead Sister Cities program with Casablanca, dies at 83
entries.summary: <p></p><p></p><p></p><p>Janet Murphy wanted to talk to you.</p><p>She wanted to know your story, where you were from, where you went to school — often leading to zigzagging conversations that could leave people enamored and possibly thinking they'd just shared more with this person they'd just met than they ever had with close friends.</p><p>The effect was especially acute on many a stony-faced cab driver in Chicago who ended up cracking a broad smile when the small, freckled lady in the back seat started speaking to them in Arabic — prompting a dialogue about each other's back stories. </p><p>Naturally curious, Mrs. Murphy wanted to see the world from a young age.</p><p>She grew up the youngest of eight siblings in a three-bedroom apartment in Boston. Her father, Patrick O'Donnell, was a police captain. Her mother, Elizabeth, was a homemaker.</p><p>She had lots of ideas about seeing the world; her protective parents approved of two.</p><p>The first took her to France for summer courses at Sorbonne University, a plan endorsed by nuns at Mrs. Murphy's school, Emmanuel College — a Catholic school in Boston — who provided housing in their convent.</p><p>The second was joining the Peace Corps, an initiative started by former President John F. Kennedy, a hometown hero.</p><p>Assigned to the North African country of Morocco, she arrived in 1964. It soon became apparent that the young women she was charged with teaching to sew and cook knew much more about those subjects than she did.</p><p>"Here we came from an ordinary educational experience in the United States to teach these things in a country renowned for its cuisine and textiles," said friend and fellow Peace Corps worker Froma Walsh. "We learned from them about their rich culture and history. Our genuine human connection with the people was the most important thing."</p><p>Mrs. Murphy settled in Chicago after serving two years in Morocco and married Michael Rosen, an architect she met during Peace Corps training. They had two kids and divorced.</p><p>Mrs. Murphy, who earned a master's degree in social work from the University of Illinois before enrolling in a Ph.D. program at the University of Chicago, worked as a youth and family social worker in the 1970s and 1980s, including for Cook County. She also worked as a real estate agent.</p><p>A neighbor, former Cook County Assessor Jim Houlihan, introduced Mrs. Murphy to her second husband, Tom Murphy, a City Hall lobbyist and former Quigley Catholic High School classmate of his.</p><p>Then-Mayor Richard M. Daley appointed Mrs. Murphy in 2001 to the board of directors of the city’s Sister Cities International program, where she served as co-chair of the Casablanca Committee.</p><p>Mrs. Murphy coordinated cultural exchanges that included visiting doctors, business leaders, artists, students, diplomats and politicians.</p><p>She often hosted Moroccan guests at her family home in Chicago or their summer home in Beverly Shores, Ind.</p><p>Her Moroccan counterpart visited so often that he kept a pair of shoes at Mrs. Murphy's Chicago home.</p><p>"People were always connecting with her," said her daughter, Liz Fischer. "It was kind of her superpower ... the diplomacy of being curious. And she was open to other people and cultures, and had no preconceived notions."</p><p>Mrs. Murphy died of natural causes on April 7. She was 83.</p><p>Family members said Mrs. Murphy had an open door policy that extended beyond Moroccan guests.</p><p>When the doorman of her downtown building suffered a fire at home, he and his family stayed at the Murphy residence until repairs were made.</p><p>When a fire damaged the home of one of her kids' grade school classmates, the family stayed with the Murphys.</p><p>"She's a person who sprang into action, and she was undaunted by what other people would find to be a difficult decision," her husband said.</p><p>The motto among her kids and friends became “What would Janet do?”</p><p>Mrs. Murphy who visited Morocco dozens of times and often recited a traditional Arabic blessing before meals, was honored by Moroccan King Mohammed VI for her dedication to the country and given the title Lady Janet Murphy.</p><p>She loved drinking coffee and playing bridge and Scrabble.</p><p>Her strong wit lasted through her last days, her husband said. When a doctor warned her that a breathing mask would feel like she was driving with her head out of the window, she replied, “I always ride like that.”</p><p>Mrs. Murphy is survived by her husband, Tom Murphy, her children Isabel Scharmer, Patrick Rosen, Maureen Murphy and Liz Fischer, as well as 10 grandchildren.</p><p>A visitation is scheduled for Friday at 1 p.m. at Holy Name Cathedral with a funeral Mass immediately following at 2 p.m.<br></p>
entries.author: Mitch Dudek

entries.title: Cardinal Cupich, other Chicago-area faith leaders push back on religious rhetoric used to justify war
entries.summary: <p>As global tensions rise amid the conflict in Iran, some Chicago-area faith leaders are raising urgent concerns about the use of religious language to justify war and nationalism.</p><p>For them, the issue is not just political — it is both deeply theological and deeply troubling.</p><p>“Diplomacy and dialogue have to be the pathway forward for a genuine peace,” Cardinal Blase Cupich, who leads the Archdiocese of Chicago, said in an exclusive interview with WBEZ and the Chicago Sun-Times on Thursday. “Peace can’t be imposed by force. It never works.”</p><p>Cupich’s remarks come on the heels of Pope Leo XIV’s criticism of U.S. President Donald Trump’s increasingly belligerent social media posts. They include a profanity-laced Easter message demanding that Iran open the Strait of Hormuz by Tuesday, “or you’ll be living in Hell – JUST WATCH! Praise be to Allah,” followed by a threat to <a class="Link" href="https://apnews.com/article/trump-iran-threats-civilization-war-crimes-758eb5cd680d7d275c4e1c38b2e01e6d" target="_blank" ><u>wipe out Iranian civilization</u></a>. The American pope called Trump’s comments “truly unacceptable.”</p><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="Enhancement-item" data-crop=""> <figure class="Figure"><a class="AnchorLink" id="image-390000" name="image-390000"></a> <picture data-crop="medium"> <source type="image/webp" width="490" height="275" data-srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/b7842fd/2147483647/strip/true/crop/4477x2513+0+236/resize/490x275!/format/webp/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2Fd1%2Fa1%2F410c8f6b138bd9722102f6c3f90b%2Fmerlin-102493338.jpg 1x,https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/bec65df/2147483647/strip/true/crop/4477x2513+0+236/resize/980x550!/format/webp/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2Fd1%2Fa1%2F410c8f6b138bd9722102f6c3f90b%2Fmerlin-102493338.jpg 2x" data-lazy-load="true" srcset="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" /> <source width="490" height="275" data-srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/c53f626/2147483647/strip/true/crop/4477x2513+0+236/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2Fd1%2Fa1%2F410c8f6b138bd9722102f6c3f90b%2Fmerlin-102493338.jpg" data-lazy-load="true" srcset="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" /> <img class="Image" alt="Cardinal Blase Cupich told WBEZ and the Chicago Sun-Times that “peace can’t be imposed by force. It never works.”" srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/c53f626/2147483647/strip/true/crop/4477x2513+0+236/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2Fd1%2Fa1%2F410c8f6b138bd9722102f6c3f90b%2Fmerlin-102493338.jpg 1x,https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/da6f473/2147483647/strip/true/crop/4477x2513+0+236/resize/980x550!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2Fd1%2Fa1%2F410c8f6b138bd9722102f6c3f90b%2Fmerlin-102493338.jpg 2x" width="490" height="275" data-src="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/c53f626/2147483647/strip/true/crop/4477x2513+0+236/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2Fd1%2Fa1%2F410c8f6b138bd9722102f6c3f90b%2Fmerlin-102493338.jpg" data-lazy-load="true" src="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" > </picture> <div class="Figure-content"><figcaption class="Figure-caption"><p>Cardinal Blase Cupich echoed Pope Leo XIV saying “peace can’t be imposed by force. It never works.”</p></figcaption><span class="line"></span><div class="Figure-credit"><p>Pat Nabong / Sun-Times</p></div></div> </figure> </div> </div><p>“Many people throughout the world echo those sentiments,” Cupich said.</p><p> A similar view was held by wide range of local faith leaders who spoke this week to WBEZ and the Chicago Sun-Times. While many observers have criticized Iran’s repressive theocratic regime, this group of religious leaders was also concerned about the Trump administration’s escalating rhetoric.</p><p>“I think the Holy Father is calling on all warring parties to put aside their weapons, not just in the Middle East,” Cupich said, “but also the conflict that we have in Ukraine and with Russia and the war that's being fought throughout the different places of the world.”</p><p>On Wednesday, White House Press Secretary Karoline Leavitt defended President Donald Trump insisting he retains the “moral high ground” against Iran and calling his remarks a “very strong threat” that delivered results.</p><p>But Trump isn’t the only one explicitly citing Christianity to bolster the administration’s case for military action in Iran.</p><p>During a press briefing at the Pentagon on Wednesday, U.S. Secretary of War Pete Hegseth praised the last-minute ceasefire, declaring, "Our troops, our American warriors, deserve the credit for this day. But God deserves all the glory. Tens of thousands of sorties, refuelings, and strikes, carried out under the protection of divine providence. A massive effort with miraculous protection."</p><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="Enhancement-item" data-crop=""> <figure class="Figure"><a class="AnchorLink" id="image-220000" name="image-220000"></a> <picture data-crop="medium"> <source type="image/webp" width="490" height="275" data-srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/e0d5f57/2147483647/strip/true/crop/4066x2282+0+214/resize/490x275!/format/webp/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2Fe8%2F44%2F83c2557e45e09db3f3e9d4f0a6fe%2F2270373796.jpg 1x,https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/66bb841/2147483647/strip/true/crop/4066x2282+0+214/resize/980x550!/format/webp/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2Fe8%2F44%2F83c2557e45e09db3f3e9d4f0a6fe%2F2270373796.jpg 2x" data-lazy-load="true" srcset="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" /> <source width="490" height="275" data-srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/c47b02b/2147483647/strip/true/crop/4066x2282+0+214/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2Fe8%2F44%2F83c2557e45e09db3f3e9d4f0a6fe%2F2270373796.jpg" data-lazy-load="true" srcset="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" /> <img class="Image" alt="Pete Hegseth And Dan Caine Hold Pentagon Press Briefing On War In Iran" srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/c47b02b/2147483647/strip/true/crop/4066x2282+0+214/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2Fe8%2F44%2F83c2557e45e09db3f3e9d4f0a6fe%2F2270373796.jpg 1x,https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/a9aa68f/2147483647/strip/true/crop/4066x2282+0+214/resize/980x550!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2Fe8%2F44%2F83c2557e45e09db3f3e9d4f0a6fe%2F2270373796.jpg 2x" width="490" height="275" data-src="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/c47b02b/2147483647/strip/true/crop/4066x2282+0+214/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2Fe8%2F44%2F83c2557e45e09db3f3e9d4f0a6fe%2F2270373796.jpg" data-lazy-load="true" src="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" > </picture> <div class="Figure-content"><figcaption class="Figure-caption"><p>U.S. Secretary of War Pete Hegseth speaks during a press briefing at the Pentagon on April 08, 2026 in Arlington, Virginia. It was announced that a temporary ceasefire has been reached between the U.S., Iran, and Israel, pausing attacks for about two weeks while Iran reopens the Strait of Hormuz and negotiations continue toward a longer-term agreement.</p></figcaption><span class="line"></span><div class="Figure-credit"><p>Andrew Harnik/Getty</p></div></div> </figure> </div> </div><p>Previously, Hegseth had compared the rescue of a downed pilot on Easter Sunday to the resurrection of Jesus Christ.</p><p>“We're all very pleased and rejoiced that the pilot was rescued and is able to receive treatment for injuries that he received,” Cupich said. </p><p>But Cupich cautioned against relying on biblical references to advance war aims.</p><p>“It’s not the first time that political leaders have tried to compromise scripture and the Word of God for their own purposes, and I think most people see through that,” Cupich said. “It seems to me to be a pretty weak argument if that's the stretch you have to make in order to justify your actions.”</p><h3><b>‘Ill-timed and unhelpful’</b></h3><p>“I have seen some statements from the President and Secretary of War that I am happy with, and some I take issue with,” said Pastor Jon Herr of Christ Covenant Church of Chicago, which is affiliated with the conservative Communion of Reformed Evangelical Churches (CREC).</p><p>Last year, Secretary Hegseth <a class="Link" href="https://apnews.com/article/pete-hegseth-crec-church-christian-nationalism-wilson-e71c3ea072fa959b5bee09a4d2093f1a" target="_blank" ><u>said he was proud</u></a> to be a member of a CREC congregation outside Nashville, Tennessee. Hegseth frequently invokes his evangelical faith in depicting a Christian nation trying to vanquish its foes with military might.</p><p>In an email, Herr pointed to CREC’s governing documents that state: "Christians are called by our Lord to be peacemakers. We therefore renounce all acts of aggression and terrorism, while recognizing the right to self-defense for individuals and nations. Combat is lawful when it defends life, liberty, and property against criminal action. Biblical principles of warfare must be followed, never returning evil for evil.”</p><p>“Whether Secretary of War Hegseth’s <i>words</i> are in line with these standards or not, I desire that the <i>actions</i> of the United States military would be in line with these biblical standards,” Herr wrote.</p><p>“I was very disappointed by President Trump’s Easter morning social media message,” Herr continued. “The President’s message, especially with its mention of Allah, was very ill-timed and unhelpful.”</p><h3><b>‘Idolatry dressed up in conviction’</b></h3><p>The Rev. Quincy Worthington, pastor of Highland Park Presbyterian Church in Highland Park, went even further.</p><p>“I find it absolutely shocking and abhorrent that they would try to co-opt and use the language of faith to support this war,” Worthington said. “The talking of wiping out an entire civilization is antithetical to just about every single Christian ethic I've read, and antithetical to the gospel and teachings of Jesus Christ.”</p><p>Worthington said he knows some of his congregation attends church for comfort and guidance, but he argued that churches risk irrelevance if they retreat and avoid difficult conversations.</p><p>“Our role as clergy is to help people make sense of the world theologically,” he said. “That includes asking what it means to live faithfully in moments like this.”</p><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="Enhancement-item" data-crop=""> <figure class="Figure"><a class="AnchorLink" id="image-440000" name="image-440000"></a> <picture data-crop="medium"> <source type="image/webp" width="490" height="275" data-srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/a638bdb/2147483647/strip/true/crop/1586x890+0+142/resize/490x275!/format/webp/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F3d%2F7f%2F508cb3e54d269089d713208697f4%2F9710d659-dc48-43c6-9d27-414f3aea1185.jpg 1x,https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/fe2370c/2147483647/strip/true/crop/1586x890+0+142/resize/980x550!/format/webp/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F3d%2F7f%2F508cb3e54d269089d713208697f4%2F9710d659-dc48-43c6-9d27-414f3aea1185.jpg 2x" data-lazy-load="true" srcset="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" /> <source width="490" height="275" data-srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/a01ccff/2147483647/strip/true/crop/1586x890+0+142/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F3d%2F7f%2F508cb3e54d269089d713208697f4%2F9710d659-dc48-43c6-9d27-414f3aea1185.jpg" data-lazy-load="true" srcset="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" /> <img class="Image" alt="9710d659-dc48-43c6-9d27-414f3aea1185.jpg" srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/a01ccff/2147483647/strip/true/crop/1586x890+0+142/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F3d%2F7f%2F508cb3e54d269089d713208697f4%2F9710d659-dc48-43c6-9d27-414f3aea1185.jpg 1x,https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/3f832fb/2147483647/strip/true/crop/1586x890+0+142/resize/980x550!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F3d%2F7f%2F508cb3e54d269089d713208697f4%2F9710d659-dc48-43c6-9d27-414f3aea1185.jpg 2x" width="490" height="275" data-src="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/a01ccff/2147483647/strip/true/crop/1586x890+0+142/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F3d%2F7f%2F508cb3e54d269089d713208697f4%2F9710d659-dc48-43c6-9d27-414f3aea1185.jpg" data-lazy-load="true" src="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" > </picture> <div class="Figure-content"><figcaption class="Figure-caption"><p>Quincy Worthington, pastor of High Park Presbyterian Church in Highland Park. “Our role as clergy is to help people make sense of the world theologically,” he says. “That includes asking what it means to live faithfully in moments like this.”</p></figcaption><span class="line"></span><div class="Figure-credit"><p>Provided</p></div></div> </figure> </div> </div><p>Central to his critique is what he and others identify as the rise of Christian nationalism — a blending of religious identity with political power.</p><p>“It becomes idolatry dressed up in conviction. The recent public rhetoric around the war has included explicit claims that God is on the side of the United States,” Worthington said. “We can't use God to baptize national ambitions. That's not the way it works.”</p><p>The Rev. Kristin Hutson of Edgewater Presbyterian Church in Chicago echoes that concern, describing a growing divide within American Christianity itself.</p><p>One version “is shaped by the ethics of Jesus, which are grounded in justice and care for the poor, liberation from oppressive powers,” Hutson said. “Then you have this other version of ‘Christianity’ that is shaped by … a strange fascination or fixation with power. Kind of rejoicing around things like violence. I think it's got a lot of grounding in toxic masculinity and American exceptionalism.”</p><h3><b>‘God is not a tool in the hands of politicians’</b></h3><p>Imam Hassan Aly, director of the Humanitarian Faith Initiative at MedGlobal in Chicago, agrees that religion should never be weaponized.</p><p>“At its core, faith in general means to guide us towards justice, mercy and peace, not to justify violence. As people of faith, we believe God is not a tool in the hands of politicians,” Aly said.</p><p>Aly said framing the conflict with Iran as a holy war is both misleading and dangerous.</p><p>“The situation is far more complex, involving people and allied alliances from many faiths and backgrounds,” Aly said. “We have to understand that not everybody in Iran is Muslim or Christian or Jews. It's a diverse country just like other countries.</p><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="Enhancement-item" data-crop=""> <figure class="Figure"><a class="AnchorLink" id="image-d80000" name="image-d80000"></a> <picture data-crop="medium"> <source type="image/webp" width="490" height="275" data-srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/c85861e/2147483647/strip/true/crop/3000x1684+0+158/resize/490x275!/format/webp/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F12%2F20%2Ff525be974aa481fc72dd65e2883b%2Fwadee-101524-19.jpg 1x,https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/75968e1/2147483647/strip/true/crop/3000x1684+0+158/resize/980x550!/format/webp/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F12%2F20%2Ff525be974aa481fc72dd65e2883b%2Fwadee-101524-19.jpg alt="WADEE-101524-19.jpg" srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/cec057a/2147483647/strip/true/crop/3000x1684+0+158/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F12%2F20%2Ff525be974aa481fc72dd65e2883b%2Fwadee-101524-19.jpg 1x,https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/0d3162c/2147483647/strip/true/crop/3000x1684+0+158/resize/980x550!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F12%2F20%2Ff525be974aa481fc72dd65e2883b%2Fwadee-101524-19.jpg 2x" width="490" height="275" data-src="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/cec057a/2147483647/strip/true/crop/3000x1684+0+158/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F12%2F20%2Ff525be974aa481fc72dd65e2883b%2Fwadee-101524-19.jpg" data-lazy-load="true" src="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" > </picture> <div class="Figure-content"><figcaption class="Figure-caption"><p>Imam Hassan Aly gives a prayer at the Muslim Community Center in Irving Park, Monday, Oct. 14, 2024. Of the Iran war he says “reducing it to be a battle between the religions only deepens division and makes peace harder to achieve.”</p></figcaption><span class="line"></span><div class="Figure-credit"><p>Tyler Pasciak LaRiviere/Sun-Time</p></div></div> </figure> </div> </div><p>Aly said many of the Gulf countries, which are allied with the United States, are also majority Muslim.</p><p>“It’s very, very complicated. Reducing it to be a battle between the religions only deepens division and makes peace harder to achieve when political leaders invoke God or use sacred language or quote from sacred holy texts to support military actions,” he said, adding many serving in the U.S. Armed Forces are also practicing Muslims.</p><h3><b>‘There are no brakes’</b></h3><p>Theresa Gross-Diaz, a historian and co-director of the Medieval Studies program at Loyola University Chicago, points out that religious justifications for war are not new.</p><p>In the Middle Ages, both Christian and Muslim leaders often spoke in terms of divine will or religious superiority. But that kind of language can be far scarier coming from a nuclear superpower led by a war secretary who has approvingly cited the 11th-century Christian invasion of the Holy Land known as the Crusades.</p><p>“What Trump and his supporters are doing now is perhaps even more alarming. They are using apocalyptic rhetoric which evokes end-times scenarios. The apocalyptic context is the expectation of global conversion, final conflagration, and Last Judgment, ushering in the end of the world and the Second Coming of the Messiah,” Gross-Diaz wrote in an email. “That's really terrifying, since it suggests that a global conflagration would ultimately be a good thing. There are no brakes.”</p><p>Cupich said the Catholic Church’s stance against the war shouldn’t be seen as a sign of being anti-American.</p><p>“America has always been a country in which different views are expressed. That is the First Amendment that we have that protects that free speech and we're better off when we listen to the voices of each other,” Cupich said. “I think that it's at the heart of democracy that people who have solid reasons for opposing a particular policy speak up. We're being patriotic when we do so.”<br></p>
entries.author: Michael Puente

entries.title: Candy Hall of Fame experience coming to Mag Mile
entries.summary: <p>Chicago will be home to the Candy Hall of Fame Experience when the new attraction opens in summer 2027 on the Magnificent Mile, the National Confectionery Sales Association announced on Thursday.</p><p>The 60,000-square-foot, multi-level space at 830 N. Michigan Ave. will spotlight candy brands, innovators and confectionery history and feature a large store.</p><p>Chicago beat Orlando and New York City to permanently host the candy museum, in part to the city’s major role in candy making history.</p><p>“For more than a century, Chicago has been home to some of the most iconic names in American candy, including Ferrara, Mars, Brach’s, Tootsie and Wrigley. Even Milton Hershey found his chocolate inspiration in Chicago at the 1893 World’s Fair,” Shelly Clarey, National Confectionery Sales Association's chairman, said in a news release. “Establishing the Candy Hall of Fame Experience here reflects the city’s deep connection to the industry and creates a home to celebrate the people who helped build it.”</p><p>The candy museum’s 20-year lease gives a boost to Chicago’s struggling Downtown, which has seen a slew of business closures since the COVID-19 pandemic. Saks Fifth Avenue will close its Michigan Avenue store this spring, after its parent company filed for bankruptcy in January. <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/business/2021/1/5/22215598/macys-closing-water-tower-place-retail-department-store" target="_blank" ><u>Macy’s</u></a> left Water Tower Place on Michigan Avenue in 2021, leaving Chicago’s high street without a full-line department store.</p><p>The attraction's building was formerly home to retailer Uniqlo, which vacated in 2021. But the Japanese clothing brand last month opened a new store at 600 N. Michigan Ave.</p><p>Other signs of revival include JPMorgan Chase’s announcement in December of a new <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/money/2025/12/17/jpmorgan-chase-mag-mile-financial-center-branch" >flagship branch</a> also at 830 N. Michigan, scheduled to open this fall. Chocolatier Hotel Chocolat opened a store in the same building late last year. The property is owned by an entity linked to developer Scott Goodman, who owed <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/the-watchdogs/2026/03/15/chicago-transit-authority-late-rent-records-developer-scott-goodman-legal-dispute" target="_blank" ><u>millions in late fees</u></a> for unpaid rent for a space in a CTA-owned West Loop building.</p><p>The Candy Hall of Fame expects to attract 2 million visitors each year and will employ about 200. Founded in 1971, the Candy Hall of Fame has not had a permanent home for many years, according to a spokesperson.</p><p>Chicago’s candy making history dates from the late 1800s. Fannie May’s first store was in the Loop and Frango mints were produced at Marshall Field’s department store on State Street for 70 years.</p><p>“Generations of companies built this industry here, and the stories behind those creations deserve to live where they began. This isn’t just a location choice; it’s a homecoming,” Jeff Rubin, CEO of the Candy Hall of Fame Experience, said in a news release. Rubin is the former founder and CEO of candy store It’Sugar.</p><p>But candy manufacturers have made high-profile exits in recent years. Blommer Chocolate Co. closed its <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/money/2024/03/22/blommer-chocolate-close-chicago-factory" >iconic Chicago factory</a> in 2024. Mars Wrigley also shuttered its <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/real-estate/2024/09/30/mars-wrigley-enlists-partner-mccaffery-redevelop-decorative-candy-factory-galewood" >factory in Galewood</a> on the Far West Side.</p><div class="RichTextSidebarModule Enhancement" data-module data-align-center><a class="AnchorLink" id="module-030000" name="module-030000"></a> <div class="RichTextModule-items RichTextBody"><h3>Chicago’s candy connection</h3><br>1891: William Wrigley Jr. comes to Chicago to sell baking soda. He starts offering merchants free chewing gum with each can of baking soda.<br><br>1904: Brach’s Palace of Sweets opens in Chicago.<br><br>1908: Ferrara founded in 1908 in Little Italy.<br><br>1920: Fannie May opens at 11 N. LaSalle St.<br><br>1929: Mars relocates from Minneapolis and opens a plant in Chicago.</div> </div><div class="RelatedList Enhancement" data-module data-align-center> <div class="RelatedList-title">Related</div> <ul class="RelatedList-items"> <li class="RelatedList-items-item"> <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/entertainment-and-culture/2024/1/12/23982982/m-ms-matthew-kradenpoth-candies-mars-wrigley-goose-island" >M&M’s guru mulls chocolate all day at Mars Wrigley headquarters on Goose Island</a> </li> <li class="RelatedList-items-item"> <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/news/2012/2/21/18533472/nello-ferrara-93-invented-lemonheads-saw-macarthur-in-occupied-japan-sang-with-sinatra" >Nello Ferrara, 93, invented Lemonheads, saw MacArthur in occupied Japan, sang with Sinatra</a> </li> <li class="RelatedList-items-item"> <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/2019/10/18/20908468/halloween-candy-from-chicago" >Your favorite Halloween candy probably has Chicago ties</a> </li> </ul> </div> <p></p>
entries.author: Amy Yee

entries.title: Berlin Nightclub’s vacant space set to transform into new cocktail and dance bar
entries.summary: <p>The space that was once home to Berlin Nightclub is set to reopen this month as a new venue: The Belmont, a cocktail bar, and Decibel, a late-night dance club.</p><p>Berlin, the eccentric Lake View nightclub at 954 W. Belmont Ave once filled with murals, drag performers and blaring music, <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/entertainment-and-culture/2023/11/21/23971686/berlin-nightclub-lake-view-closes-after-40-years-chicago-lgbt" target="_blank" >closed its doors in 2023</a> after pay negotiations between owners Jim Schuman and Jo Webster and employees came to a standstill. The storefront will now operate as The Belmont until 10 p.m. and later transform into Decibel (or dB for short) on Wednesday, Friday and Saturday nights.</p><p>Marcus Devin, one of the new managers at the bar, was a regular at Berlin as far back as the 1990s. Sneaking into the bar at only 17, Devin eventually became an employee in 2016 and climbed up the ranks until the doors shut. He said he hopes Decibel and The Belmont bring back the same sense of acceptance that Berlin provided for so many people.</p><p>“It is separate from Berlin,” Devin, 47, said. “It's just trying to bring back the feeling and the community and the night life that Berlin used to have, especially before the pandemic.”</p><p>“It used to be, 'Everyone's welcome here, from any background,'” he added. “You would come here and you would feel comfortable and everyone accepted you, and you got to be yourself. That is what we're going for.”</p><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="Enhancement-item" data-crop=""> <figure class="Figure"><a class="AnchorLink" id="image-1b0000" name="image-1b0000"></a> <picture data-crop="medium"> <source type="image/webp" width="490" height="275" data-srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/26eff28/2147483647/strip/true/crop/3000x1684+0+158/resize/490x275!/format/webp/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2Fcd%2F2b%2Fddc8c86043c5bf8b97b65b02ae7d%2Fberlinbelmont-260410-4.jpg
