data-lazy-load="true" srcset="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" /> <img class="Image" alt="BERLINBELMONT_260410-4.jpg" srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/87656ea/2147483647/strip/true/crop/3000x1684+0+158/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2Fcd%2F2b%2Fddc8c86043c5bf8b97b65b02ae7d%2Fberlinbelmont-260410-4.jpg 1x,https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/f7b9011/2147483647/strip/true/crop/3000x1684+0+158/resize/980x550!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2Fcd%2F2b%2Fddc8c86043c5bf8b97b65b02ae7d%2Fberlinbelmont-260410-4.jpg 2x" width="490" height="275" data-src="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/87656ea/2147483647/strip/true/crop/3000x1684+0+158/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2Fcd%2F2b%2Fddc8c86043c5bf8b97b65b02ae7d%2Fberlinbelmont-260410-4.jpg" data-lazy-load="true" src="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" > </picture> <div class="Figure-content"><figcaption class="Figure-caption"><p>Marcus Devin and Austin Neff, manager and programming manager of Decibel, stand together inside the future nightclub and bar which occupies the former Berlin located at 954 W. Belmont Ave. in the Lake View neighborhood.</p></figcaption><span class="line"></span><div class="Figure-credit"><p>Tyler Pasciak LaRiviere/Sun-Time</p></div></div> </figure> </div> </div><p>Decibel and The Belmont will have a private opening April 25 and 26, and will open to the public once the bar receives approval from the city. Marcus said the bar could open to the public as early as April 25 if it receives approval.</p><p>The Belmont’s door will be open Tuesday to Saturday and patrons at Decibel will be charged a $5-$10 cover after 10 p.m.</p><p>The new venue will keep Berlin’s elevated stages for drag performances while adding new lighting and sound systems, bathrooms and two LED-lit bars. The new team will also include previous Berlin employees, including programming director Austin Neff and special events DJ Greg Haus.</p><div class="RelatedList Enhancement" data-module data-align-center> <div class="RelatedList-title">Related</div> <ul class="RelatedList-items"> <li class="RelatedList-items-item"> <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/art/2026/01/07/at-the-now-shuttered-berlin-club-art-was-for-celebration-and-mourning" >At the now-shuttered Berlin Nightclub, art was for celebration and mourning</a> </li> <li class="RelatedList-items-item"> <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/entertainment-and-culture/2023/12/4/23984021/berlin-nightclub-lake-view-drag-bar-farewell-close-memories" >Berlin Nightclub’s denizens, staffers will always have their memories</a> </li> </ul> </div> <p>Joseph Giannini, who performed in drag under the name of Gina Taye at Berlin, said the shuttered nightclub could never be recreated, but he is thrilled for a new generation of revelers to enjoy the space.</p><p>“I'm overjoyed that the space is being revitalized,” Giannini said. “I was hoping that it wasn't going to get demolished and turn into condos or something like that. So knowing that the space is going to stay there, it's kind of like our Chicago Stonewall, if you will.”</p><p>Giannini first danced in Berlin nightclub when he was 18. Now 55, he said he is excited to visit the new bar. A few decades older, he said the Belmont cocktail bar may suit him more now.</p><p>“I don't know if I can make it out past 10 p.m. anymore,” Giannini joked. “But I'm willing to come out of retirement for a grand opening party."</p><p>While a landmark for the LGBTQ+ community in Chicago, Giannini reflected on Berlin as a place of extravagance and self-expression for all types of people.</p><p>“I hope that the space can be all inclusive again without having to be tagged as a gay bar,” Giannini said.</p>
entries.author: Zoe Singer

entries.title: Angel Reese after trade: 'I just feel that urge right now that I'm going to win again'
entries.summary: <p>Former <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/chicago-sky" target="_blank" >Sky</a> forward Angel Reese is ready to win again.</p><p>Speaking at a Coach event Wednesday in Los Angeles, Reese compared being <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/chicago-sky/2026/04/06/sky-trade-angel-reese-to-atlanta-dream-for-2027-2028-draft-picks" >traded to the Dream</a> to her college transfer experience.</p><p>"Transferring from Maryland and going to LSU and winning a championship, it feels like deja vu right now," Reese said. "And I get chills right now because being a part of Chicago my first two years and going to Atlanta, I just feel that urge right now that I'm going to win again."</p><div class="RelatedList Enhancement" data-module data-align-center> <div class="RelatedList-title">Related</div> <ul class="RelatedList-items"> <li class="RelatedList-items-item"> <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/chicago-sky/2026/04/06/sky-trade-angel-reese-to-atlanta-dream-for-2027-2028-draft-picks" >Sky trade Angel Reese to Dream for first-round draft picks in 2027, 2028</a> </li> </ul> </div> <p>The Dream went 30-14 last season before falling to the Fever in the quarterfinals. The Sky went a combined 27-61 during Reese's tenure before trading her for first-round draft picks in 2027 and 2028.</p><p>Though early reports indicate Reese did not formally request a trade, her comments at the Coach event alluded to difficult times in Chicago.</p><p>"It's not easy to go be by yourself in the dark and those lonely nights and those long nights thinking by yourself," Reese said. "Social media has it out there to be one way. But at the end of the day, you gotta sit with yourself and tell yourself what you want in life and continue to put yourself in spaces and rooms [that] feel super uncomfortable."</p><p>Reese's dissatisfaction with the Sky became clearer over time. At the end of last season, <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/chicago-sky/2025/09/06/the-meeting-before-the-interview-heard-round-the-world" >she critiqued</a> the team's facilities, medical staff and its ability to attract free agents. Months earlier, she alluded to <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/chicago-sky/2025/08/19/angel-reese-alludes-to-tension-regarding-her-recovery-from-back-injury" >disappointment with how her back injury was handled.</a></p><p>"I'll speak on that after the season," she said when asked about the weeks-long recovery, before <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/chicago-sky/2025/09/22/skys-angel-reese-courtney-vandersloot-and-ariel-atkins-will-not-conduct-exit-interviews" >skipping exit interviews altogether.</a></p><p>In the offseason, she hardly mentioned the Sky on her podcast or in interviews.</p><p>Reese will return to Chicago for the Dream's first preseason game against the Sky on April 29.</p><p></p><p></p><bsp-list-loadmore class="PageListH" data-module data-hide-authors="true" data-hide-categories="true" data-hide-dates="true" data-show-loadmore="true" ><a class="AnchorLink" id="list-010001" name="list-010001"></a><div class="PageList-header" > <div class="PageList-header-title-wrap" > <div class="PageList-header-title"> Latest on the Sky and WNBA </div> </div> </div> <div class="PageListH-items" data-with-borders data-list-loadmore-items> <div class="PageListH-items-item"><div class="PagePromoB"> <div class="PagePromo-media"><a class="Link" aria-label="How many balloons does it take to woo a star? entries.title: Nearly 70 Chicago groups receive micro-grants after SNAP cuts, deportation campaign
entries.summary: <p>Mayor Brandon Johnson announced on Thursday that 67 organizations across Chicago are receiving $4,500 micro-grants each through the Greater Chicago Food Depository.</p><p>The money is being doled out to help offset the strain Chicago’s small businesses and community groups are feeling after the federal deportation campaign and cuts to food assistance under the Trump administration. </p><p>“I have to admit, I do know that sometimes government — we can move a little slow,” Johnson said at Malcolm X College Thursday. “So the $4,500 grants that are issued, we are not just investing in services that you provide, but the leadership that you provide and the trust that you have built with residents. You embody the spirit of our collective responsibility.”</p><p>In November 2025, Johnson signed an executive order directing city resources to the Food Depository to support local organizations and businesses affected by a lapse in the Supplemental Nutrition Assistance Program funding during the 43-day government shutdown.</p><div class="RichTextSidebarModule Enhancement" data-module data-align-center><a class="AnchorLink" id="module-740000" name="module-740000"></a> <div class="RichTextModule-items RichTextBody"><b>Help for SNAP recipients </b><br><ul class="rte2-style-ul" id="rte-59a96830-fb08-11f0-89d5-e38f58753511"><li><b>Update your SNAP information:</b> Illinois created a <a class="Link" href="https://aberp.illinois.gov/screener/ABAWD?lang=EN " target="_blank" >website</a> where you can see if you meet the new SNAP program requirements. You can update your information by calling 1-800-843-6154. <a class="Link" href="https://aberp.illinois.gov/screener/ABAWD?lang=EN" target="_blank" >https://aberp.illinois.gov/screener/ABAWD?lang=EN</a></li><li><b>Find a food pantry:</b> Here’s a <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/chicago/2025/10/29/where-to-find-chicago-area-food-pantries-if-snap-goes-away" target="_blank" >list of pantries</a> in the Chicago area </li><li><b>Where can I volunteer?</b> <br><ul class="rte2-style-ul" style="margin-top:0;margin-bottom:0;padding-inline-start:48px;" id="rte-fa6424d3-1d8c-11f1-a5fc-4b8b8cb93c27"><li>The next Meals on Wheels Chicago pantry is March 24. <a class="Link" href="https://www.mealsonwheelschicago.org/volunteer-individuals-formstack" target="_blank" ><u>Visit</u> this website</a> to volunteer <br><a class="Link" href="https://www.mealsonwheelschicago.org/volunteer-individuals-formstack" target="_blank" ><u>https://www.mealsonwheelschicago.org/volunteer-individuals-formstack</u></a> </li><li>Volunteer shifts at Nourishing Hope’s pantries are available at their <a class="Link" href="https://nourishinghopechi.org/volunteer/" target="_blank" ><u>website</u></a><br><a class="Link" href="https://nourishinghopechi.org/volunteer/" target="_blank" ><u>https://nourishinghopechi.org/volunteer/</u></a></li><li>Care For Friends, 530 W. Fullerton Pkwy., can be reached at (773) 932-1010</li></ul></li></ul></div> </div><p>As part of that effort, the city launched the micro-grant program to <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/news/2025/11/15/mayor-brandon-johnson-snap-funding-food-insecurity" >help businesses</a> hit by both reduced SNAP purchasing power and drops in foot-traffic from federal immigration enforcement.</p><p>The SNAP funding pause was felt for many Chicagoans in early November, but small businesses and organizations were impacted months before due to increased immigration operations. In addition, <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/politics/2026/03/13/older-chicagoans-high-risk-of-losing-snap-food-assistance-new-work-rules-kick-in-55-64-may" >expanded work requirements</a> for SNAP recipients that went into effect this spring are expected to push about 150,000 people out of the program starting in May, according to the latest estimates from state officials.</p><p>Working Family Solidarity lead organizer, Kevin Johnson Jr., 26, said more than half of his organization is Hispanic, and nearly 40% of members are undocumented.</p><p>“Directly after SNAP benefits were cut, it became kind of Armageddon for us a little bit,” he said.</p><p>The Pilsen-based community group provides support to Black and Latino communities across Chicago.</p><p>“One of our workers, who's a single mother of two children, had her benefits cut so this is something that is literally stopping individuals from being able to feed their children and feed themselves,” Johnson said.</p><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="Enhancement-item" data-crop=""> <figure class="Figure"><a class="AnchorLink" id="image-670000" name="image-670000"></a> <picture data-crop="medium"> <source type="image/webp" width="490" height="275" data-srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/4ca195e/2147483647/strip/true/crop/4644x2606+0+249/resize/490x275!/format/webp/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2Fca%2Fa7%2Fa8ebe6594bcfae006f6ca2462bc2%2Fdsc01390.JPG 1x,https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/2da01e2/2147483647/strip/true/crop/4644x2606+0+249/resize/980x550!/format/webp/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2Fca%2Fa7%2Fa8ebe6594bcfae006f6ca2462bc2%2Fdsc01390.JPG 1x,https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/f9c1757/2147483647/strip/true/crop/4644x2606+0+249/resize/980x550!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2Fca%2Fa7%2Fa8ebe6594bcfae006f6ca2462bc2%2Fdsc01390.JPG 2x" width="490" height="275" data-src="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/7e41376/2147483647/strip/true/crop/4644x2606+0+249/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2Fca%2Fa7%2Fa8ebe6594bcfae006f6ca2462bc2%2Fdsc01390.JPG" data-lazy-load="true" src="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" > </picture> <div class="Figure-content"><figcaption class="Figure-caption"><p>Mayor Brandon Johnson (left) speaks with a constituent at Malcolm X College Thursday at an event where organization and businesses were awarded micro-grants to expand food access and supportive services.</p></figcaption><span class="line"></span><div class="Figure-credit"><p>Somer Van Benton/WBEZ</p></div></div> </figure> </div> </div><p>The organization intends to use their grant to host food and resource drives across Chicago.</p><p>Dulce Morales, 45, co-founded Cedillo’s Fresh Produce with her husband. The Englewood-based farm received SNAP benefits to support operational costs.</p><p>“We are still in production,” said Morales, “but it has impacted the income that it's coming to us.”</p><p>The family farm owns a truck used to make deliveries to local markets across Chicago.</p><p>“We want to continue to feed people, but it's very hard when now you got to struggle with ‘and should I drive or not?’” Morales said.</p><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="Enhancement-item" data-crop=""> <figure class="Figure"><a class="AnchorLink" id="image-fe0000" name="image-fe0000"></a> 2x" width="490" height="275" data-src="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/8740fff/2147483647/strip/true/crop/4032x2263+0+306/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2Ff9%2F2b%2F2d8d56c3401cbeca125a44c424c2%2Fimg-4267.jpg" class="Figure-content"><figcaption class="Figure-caption"><p>The Greater Chicago Food Depository launched the mutual aid micro-grant program.</p></figcaption><span class="line"></span><div class="Figure-credit"><p>Somer Van Benton/WBEZ</p></div></div> </figure> </div> </div><p>Morales said the $4,500 grant will help offset rising gas prices and insurance costs for the truck.</p><p>Maria’h Foster from the group Life is Work said food insecurity in Austin has always been prevalent, but SNAP funding cuts have amplified the need for more resources.</p><p>Foster said the organization intends to use their grant to contribute to their existing food pantry, stressing that there is a large range of communities in need.</p><p>“It's not just Black and Latinx communities that are being impacted,” Foster said. “aging populations, elders, individuals with disabilities, individuals who have HIV, individuals who are veterans — those are the populations that also have been vulnerable and impacted by this, that kind of get left out.”</p><div class="RichTextSidebarModule Enhancement" data-module data-align-center><a class="AnchorLink" id="module-3f0000" entries.title: Curb rampant predatory lending against small businesses in Illinois
entries.summary: <p>About 98% of Illinois businesses are small firms with <a class="Link" href="https://sbecouncil.org/about-us/facts-and-data/#:~:text=Based%20on%20data%20from%20the,in%202019%20(latest%20data).&text=Firms%20with%20fewer%20than%20500,for%2099.7%25%20of%20those%20businesses.&text=Firms%20with%20fewer%20than%20100%20employees%20accounted%20for%2098.1%25.&text=Firms%20with%20fewer%20than%2020%20employees%20made%20up%2089.0%25.&text=Firms%20with%20fewer%20than%2010,is%20a%20small%20business%20economy.&text=98.9%25%20had%20less%20than%20500,than%2010%20employees%20registers%2080.8%25" target="_blank" >under 100 employees,</a> or are self-employed solopreneurs and gig workers. These hard-working small businesses are economic lions that create two-thirds of net new jobs, spur innovation and foster community for a diverse, thriving economy. </p><p>Yet, the owners of these small firms are hardly living large: Their median household income is just over <a class="Link" href="https://advocacy.sba.gov/wp-content/uploads/2024/10/Midwest_2024_09_23.pdf" target="_blank" >$46,000</a>, and they often lack the capital needed to strengthen their businesses.</p><p>At the same time, loans from traditional banks are increasingly difficult for small business owners to secure. <a class="Link" href="https://smallbusinessmajority.org/our-research/voice-of-main-street/entrepreneurs-struggle-access-funding-support-policies-increase-availability-responsible-capital" target="_blank" >Research</a> from Small Business Majority found half of small business owners have taken steps to access funding but only around a third who sought it ultimately secured it.</p><div class="RichTextSidebarModule Enhancement" data-module data-align-center><a class="AnchorLink" id="module-ac0000" name="module-ac0000"></a> <div class="RichTextSidebarModule-title">Commentary bug</div> <div class="RichTextModule-items RichTextBody"><h2>Commentary</h2></div> </div><p>Most of those that did receive funding secured $50,000 or less, and almost a quarter reported that the funding they received was insufficient. The gap between need and available resources is particularly wide for small businesses owned by women and people of color. After all, <a class="Link" href="https://www.nbcnews.com/news/latino/latino-owned-businesses-are-seeing-record-growth-big-banks-are-n1256884" target="_blank" >just 20%</a> of Latino-owned businesses that sought more than $100,000 from a national bank received funding. And for <a class="Link" href="https://www.forbes.com/sites/rohitarora/2025/03/07/women-owned-businesses-got-more-loan-approvals-higher-amounts-in-2024/" target="_blank" >women business owners</a>, their loan success rate isn’t much better — only 36%.</p><p>This is where predatory online lenders see an opportunity. They can be found through a simple Google search. They promise $1 million or more in a day, when Illinois small businesses actually collectively lose about $1 million a day in savings due to unregulated loans. With nowhere to turn, desperate entrepreneurs trying to stay afloat often take the tempting option they see before them without realizing these seemingly easy loans are a trap with the power to bankrupt a business.</p><h3>Fees add up quickly</h3><h3></h3><p>Although some online sources of business loans are honest, many non-bank lenders and special interest private companies use a legal loophole to hunt and bait small businesses with loans that have interest rates between 48% to more than 350% — often disguised as “fees.” Although the federal <a class="Link" href="https://www.occ.treas.gov/topics/consumers-and-communities/consumer-protection/truth-in-lending/index-truth-in-lending.html" target="_blank" >Truth in Lending Act</a> requires clear disclosure of true annual percentage rates in consumer loans, no such law exists for small business loans.</p><p>This gives cover to unscrupulous non-bank lenders, many of whom claim their products are not even loans. Others have argued that there’s no need to show a business the true APR for something that is just “a six-month, short-term loan.”</p><p>In reality, entrepreneurs who are unable to make apples-to-apples comparisons for lending products get caught in a financial eddy circling the bankruptcy drain because the real APR for their loan wasn’t disclosed up front before signing. When a non-bank lender claims that a business owner defaults on a loan, the lender can charge the owner hidden fees — even if the owner tries to repay the loan early by paying down the principal. These lenders claim this is all just fair competition, but loopholes reward a corrupted form of capitalism that would make Adam Smith, the father of modern economics, cringe.</p><p>That non-bank lenders are allowed to get away with these deceptive tactics is a bigger problem today than ever given sky-high prices from tariffs and inflation, soaring health costs caused in part by federal cuts to Medicaid (<a class="Link" href="https://smallbusinessmajority.org/press-release/senate-s-big-beautiful-bill-disaster-small-businesses" target="_blank" >a third</a> of all people enrolled in Medicaid nationwide are connected to small businesses), and <a class="Link" href="https://smallbusinessmajority.org/press-release/sba-should-not-block-immigrants-accessing-small-business-loans" target="_blank" >new rules</a> limiting loans backed by the U.S. Small Business Administration based on business owners' country of origin.</p><p>We’ve seen our share of small businesses lose significant money or even their entire enterprise as a result of predatory lending products that did not clearly disclose APR. Equitable opportunities for small businesses to access affordable capital and build generational wealth also reflect Illinois’ history of helping its residents overcome income, gender, race and citizenship inequalities.</p><p>With this in mind, we urge every Illinois small business owner to speak up about the importance of transparent <a class="Link" href="https://aprforall.com/" target="_blank" >APR requirements</a> for small business lending products and to call on Gov. JB Pritzker and other Illinois lawmakers to pass <a class="Link" href="https://woodstockinst.org/small-businesses/sbfta/" target="_blank" >the Small Business Financing Transparency Act</a>. For too long, Illinois has allowed hunters to win by letting them take advantage of small businesses. It’s time for our state to side with the lions who are our nation’s job creators.</p><p><i>Jaime di Paulo is president and CEO of the Illinois Hispanic Chamber of Commerce. Geri Sanchez Aglipay is a senior fellow at Small Business Majority and former Great Lakes regional administrator for the U.S. Small Business Administration. </i></p><div class="RichTextSidebarModule Enhancement" data-module data-align-center><a class="AnchorLink" id="module-d40000" name="module-d40000"></a> <div class="RichTextModule-items RichTextBody">Send letters to <a class="Link" href="mailto:letters@suntimes.com" target="_blank" >letters@suntimes.com</a>. More about <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/submitting-opinion-and-letters" >how to submit here</a>.</div> </div><p></p>
entries.author: Geri Sanchez Aglipay
